title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary Of The Paper

This paper examines the "label trick," a technique that enables the parallel propagation of labels and features across graph neural networks and has demonstrated considerable empirical success in various SOTA GNN architectures. Despite its widespread adoption across multiple research communities and leaderboards, the label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed from a theoretical perspective. This work fills that gap by studying the label trick through formal mathematical analysis. The paper first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy where only a single node is held out during each training iteration. It then demonstrates that the stochastic label trick with random splits can be mathematically interpreted as introducing a regularization effect on the self-excluded label weights. Beyond the theoretical contributions, the paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order to: (a) empower previously parameter-free or non-end-to-end graph-based methods with trainable weights, and (b) eliminate randomness effects by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

## Main Review

### (1) Limited Empirical Gains from Novel Use-Cases

The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Section 4. I understand and appreciate this strategic choice to focus on novel use-cases rather than re-validating the original technique. However, upon careful examination of Sections 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, the results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no meaningful benefit of the label trick compared to baseline methods. 

More specifically, in Table 1 (trainable label propagation), improvements are marginal across all datasets—ranging from 0.96% on Cora-full to 1.37% on ArXiv—and many of these gains fall within the reported standard deviations across ten independent runs. For instance, on Pubmed, the improvement is only 0.07%, which is negligible given the standard deviation of approximately ±0.6%. Similarly, in Table 3 (deterministic label trick applied to linear layer GNNs), while some datasets like ogbn-arxiv show consistent improvements (approximately 1% on average), the Cora-full dataset shows essentially no improvement, with results being statistically indistinguishable. The authors themselves acknowledge this phenomenon in Appendix A.1, noting that "when the training accuracy is near 100%, the label trick minimally benefits the model as the label information has already been adequately embedded in the model."

The most compelling results appear in Table 2 (trainable C&S with GBDT), where more substantial improvements are observed (e.g., 0.057 improvement in R² on the House dataset). However, these results are restricted to regression tasks with tabular node features using gradient boosted decision trees as the base model, which represents a narrower scope than the general GNN setting that motivates the theoretical analysis. Table 4 (trainable C&S with MLP) shows mixed results, with comparable performance between vanilla C&S and the proposed trainable variant, particularly on larger datasets like ogbn-products where test accuracy improvements are negligible (83.16 ± 0.13 vs 83.10 ± 0.15).

This pattern raises an important question: if the theoretical insights are compelling, why do the proposed applications not demonstrate more convincing empirical validation? The limited empirical gains suggest that while the theoretical framework is intellectually interesting, its practical utility for the specific use-cases presented may be limited. The authors should provide more insight into when and why practitioners should adopt these variants over their baseline counterparts.

### (2) Incomplete Analysis of Label Trick's Role in GNN Training

Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of the label trick in GNN training beyond the theoretical perspective. The paper makes a compelling but somewhat shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." While this observation certainly establishes the widespread adoption of the technique, it does not necessarily demonstrate that the label trick itself is the primary driver of superior performance. To be able to argue convincingly that the label trick leads to better performance, one would need to run a rigorous ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick, holding all other hyperparameters and architectural choices constant. Such an ablation would help isolate the specific contribution of the label trick from other potentially confounding factors such as enhanced preprocessing, different feature engineering, or architectural innovations.

Moreover, the paper lacks discussion of other potentially desirable effects that may have made the label trick so popular in the community. For instance, does the label trick provide benefits beyond test accuracy, such as: (a) faster convergence during training, which could be practically valuable for large-scale applications? (b) more robust training dynamics that are less sensitive to random seed initialization? (c) better generalization properties that manifest as smaller gaps between validation and test performance? (d) improved stability when combined with other training techniques like data augmentation or regularization methods? These practical considerations could explain the widespread adoption of the label trick even if the improvements on standard benchmarks are modest.

The paper would be significantly strengthened by investigating these orthogonal benefits. For example, Figure 1 shows the effect of splitting probability α on validation accuracy, but it would be equally informative to show convergence curves comparing models trained with and without the label trick to understand whether there are efficiency gains. Similarly, examining the variance of predictions across different random seeds could reveal whether the label trick provides regularization benefits beyond what is captured by the theoretical analysis. The authors' theoretical framework elegantly decomposes the label trick objective into a data-fitting term and a regularization term (Equation 9), but the empirical validation of whether this regularization actually manifests as improved robustness or stability is absent.

### (3) Strengths of the Theoretical Contribution

Despite the concerns raised above regarding empirical validation, I want to acknowledge the genuine strengths of the theoretical analysis presented in this work. The mathematical framework developed in Section 3 is rigorous and provides valuable insights into the label trick's behavior. The reduction of the stochastic label trick to a deterministic objective (Theorem 1 and Corollary 1) is elegant and provides an interpretable decomposition into a data-fitting term and a graph-dependent regularization factor. This theoretical perspective is genuinely novel and represents a meaningful contribution to understanding GNN training dynamics.

The self-excluded simplification introduced in Section 3.2 is particularly clever, as it naturally resolves label leakage issues while maintaining consistent predictions between training and inference (Remarks 1 and 2). The observation that the regularization term Γ adapts to graph structure and size (Remark 4) is insightful and helps explain why the label trick might be beneficial in different graph settings. The extension to nonlinear models via Theorem 3 also shows good mathematical maturity, even if the results are less complete than in the linear case.

The paper's careful treatment of different loss functions (MSE in Theorem 1 and categorical cross-entropy in Theorem 2) demonstrates thoroughness. The connection between the theoretical analysis and practical GNN architectures is also reasonably well-established, with the experiments in Section 5.3 providing empirical support for the predicted behavior of the regularization parameter α.

### (4) Scope and Applicability of Theoretical Results

While the theoretical contributions are solid, there are important caveats regarding their scope and practical applicability that deserve more discussion. The main theoretical results (Theorems 1 and Corollary 1) assume a linear propagation function f, which is a significant simplification compared to the nonlinear GNN architectures that dominate practical applications. Although the authors acknowledge this limitation and provide Theorem 3 for the nonlinear case, the result for nonlinear models is weaker—it only characterizes the behavior at the extremes (α → 0 or α → 1) rather than providing a complete characterization of the objective across the full range of α.

The gap between theory (linear case) and practice (nonlinear GNNs) is substantial. In the linear setting, the label trick induces an explicit, interpretable objective with clear data-fitting and regularization terms. In the nonlinear case, as the authors note in Section 3.4, "we can no longer guarantee that the predictor implicitly applied to test nodes during training will exactly match the explicit function fGN N [X, Y tr; W] applied at inference time." This mismatch between training and inference objectives could potentially impact generalization in ways that are not fully characterized by the theory. The authors appropriately note that "in practice this is unlikely to be consequential," but this remains an assumption rather than a proven result.

Additionally, the theoretical analysis assumes specific forms for the propagation matrix P and relies on the existence and properties of the diagonal matrix C = diag(P). While these

A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary Of The Paper

The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification for the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick—a technique that adopts a random portion of node labels in the training set as input node attributes during GNN training. Rather than treating this as an ad-hoc heuristic, the paper rigorously analyzes what statistical properties the label trick introduces into the training pipeline. The authors begin from the simplest case of the label trick without node features and progressively derive general formulations that characterize the label trick as inducing a deterministic training objective composed of both a data-fitting term and a graph-dependent regularization factor. Motivated by this theoretical analysis, the authors propose novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios, including trainable variants of label propagation and Correct & Smooth, as well as deterministic applications to GNNs with linear propagation layers.

## Main Review

### Strengths

The work is fundamentally solid with rigorous mathematical formulation, comprehensive theoretical justification, and well-motivated novel use cases of the label trick. This represents a significant contribution to understanding an increasingly important technique in the GNN community.

**Theoretical Rigor and Clarity**: The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression (MSE loss) and classification (cross-entropy loss) settings. The theoretical development is presented in a remarkably smooth and pedagogical way, starting from the simplified case of no node features (Section 3.1) and progressively building toward the full result with both features and labels (Section 3.3). The progression from the stochastic label trick in Equation (4) to the deterministic self-excluded simplification in Equation (6) and finally to the general result in Theorem 1 demonstrates excellent mathematical exposition. The authors clearly delineate their assumptions and carefully explain the intuition behind each step, making the work accessible despite its technical depth.

**Insightful Theoretical Results**: Theorem 1 and Corollary 1 are particularly illuminating, as they reveal that the seemingly complex stochastic label trick can be decomposed into two interpretable components: (i) a data-fitting term using the self-excluded label propagation predictor (P − C)Y_tr that naturally resolves label leakage issues, and (ii) a graph-dependent regularization factor Γ that adapts to graph structure, size, and connectivity. The remarks following these theorems provide valuable intuition—for instance, Remark 4 elegantly explains how the regularization naturally scales with graph connectivity and size, ensuring proper balance with the data-fitting term. These insights go beyond merely validating the label trick; they provide a principled understanding of when and why it should be effective.

**Consistency Between Training and Inference**: A particularly important theoretical finding, highlighted in Remark 2 and Remark 3, is that the label trick ensures consistent predictions on test nodes during both training and inference. This addresses a subtle but critical issue: during stochastic training with random label splits, the model implicitly makes predictions on test nodes using one set of label inputs, but at inference time it uses the full training label set. The theoretical analysis shows that the self-excluded formulation naturally resolves this asymmetry, which could otherwise compromise generalization. This is a non-obvious insight that validates the label trick's practical effectiveness.

**Extension to Nonlinear Settings**: While the main results focus on linear models (analogous to SGC), the authors extend their analysis to arbitrary nonlinear GNNs through Theorem 3, which characterizes the behavior at the extremes (α → 0 and α → 1). This extension, though not providing closed-form equivalents for general α, demonstrates the breadth of applicability of the theoretical framework and provides guidance for practitioners using nonlinear models.

**Novel and Practical Use Cases**: The proposed use cases are genuinely innovative and directly motivated by the theoretical analysis:
- **Trainable Label Propagation (Section 4.1)**: The paper shows how to augment the parameter-free label propagation algorithm with learnable weights by applying the label trick framework. Table 1 demonstrates consistent empirical improvements, suggesting this simple enhancement is practically valuable.
- **Deterministic Label Trick for Linear Models (Section 4.2)**: By leveraging the theoretical insight that the label trick can be expressed deterministically as (P − C)Y_tr, the authors provide a computationally efficient alternative to stochastic splitting for models like SGC, SIGN, and TWIRLS. This is particularly useful for practitioners who want to avoid the computational overhead of random sampling during training.
- **Trainable Correct & Smooth (Section 4.3)**: The extension of C&S to be end-to-end trainable via the label trick is elegant, addressing a long-standing limitation of this otherwise powerful post-processing method. The formulation in Equations (14)-(15) naturally incorporates the label trick framework while maintaining the interpretability of the C&S pipeline.

**Comprehensive Experimental Validation**: The experiments effectively complement the theoretical analysis. Rather than merely re-demonstrating the label trick's effectiveness (which has already been shown in prior work), the experiments specifically validate the theoretical predictions and showcase the novel use cases. Table 3 shows consistent improvements from the deterministic label trick across different architectures and datasets. Figure 1 provides valuable empirical evidence that the splitting probability α indeed controls regularization strength as predicted by theory, with both linear and nonlinear models exhibiting the expected convergence behavior. The ablation studies in Appendix A.1 and A.2 provide additional confidence in the robustness of the findings.

### Areas for Improvement and Clarification

**Gap Between Theory and Practice**: While the theoretical results are elegant, they are derived under the assumption of linear models (or the deterministic self-excluded simplification). The extension to general nonlinear GNNs in Theorem 3 only characterizes the behavior at the extremes (α → 0 and α → 1), leaving a gap for intermediate values of α. The authors acknowledge this limitation in Section 3.4, but it would be valuable to provide additional empirical or heuristic guidance on how the theoretical insights translate to nonlinear settings. For instance, the paper could discuss whether practitioners should expect the regularization effect to manifest similarly in deep GNNs with multiple nonlinear layers as it does in linear models. The experiments in Figure 1 provide some evidence, but a more thorough investigation of this gap would strengthen the work.

**Assumptions and Applicability**: The theoretical analysis relies on several simplifying assumptions that merit more explicit discussion. The assumption of a linear propagation matrix P is reasonable for many common choices (normalized adjacency matrix, diffusion matrices), but the paper could more clearly enumerate which propagation matrices satisfy the required properties. Additionally, the analysis assumes node-wise Bernoulli sampling with parameter α, which is standard practice, but alternative sampling schemes (e.g., stratified sampling to ensure balanced class representation in D_in and D_out) might be more appropriate in some settings. The paper could discuss how robust the theoretical results are to deviations from these assumptions.

**Limited Discussion of Computational Complexity**: While the deterministic label trick variant (Section 4.2) is presented as more efficient than the stochastic version, the paper provides limited explicit analysis of computational complexity. Computing (P − C)Y_tr requires access to the propagation matrix P and its diagonal C, which can be expensive for large graphs with many propagation steps. The paper mentions that this is "computationally expensive" for larger graphs (Section 5.2) and restricts experiments to smaller datasets, but a more detailed discussion of the computational trade-offs would be valuable. For practitioners considering whether to use the deterministic or stochastic variant, explicit complexity comparisons would be helpful.

**Generalization Beyond Semi-Supervised Node Classification**: While the paper demonstrates the label trick's effectiveness on node classification benchmarks, the applicability to other graph learning tasks is less explored. The work briefly mentions extensions to link prediction and graph classification in the introduction, but does not provide theoretical analysis or experiments for these settings. Given the increasing importance of these tasks in applications, discussing how the theoretical framework extends to these domains would broaden the impact of the work.

### Technical Soundness

The mathematical derivations appear sound. The proofs in Appendix B are detailed and careful. Theorem 1 correctly characterizes the label trick loss through a series of algebraic manipulations that isolate the data-fitting and regularization terms. The proof technique—computing expectations over random splits term-by-term—is standard but executed meticulously. Theorem 2 establishes an upper bound relationship between the stochastic label trick with cross-entropy loss and the self-excluded variant, which is a reasonable relaxation given the intractability of deriving a closed-form equivalence for non-quadratic losses. Theorem 3's use of the binomial distribution decomposition to analyze the limit as α → 1 is elegant and correct.

One minor technical point: in Equation (8), the rescaling factor 1/α is introduced to maintain consistent mean and variance across different sampling probabilities. While this is a reasonable design choice, the paper could discuss whether other normalization schemes (e

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary Of The Paper:

The authors investigate the theoretical foundations of the 'label trick,' a technique whereby training labels are concatenated with node features in graph neural networks and randomly masked during training to avoid trivial solutions like identity mappings. The paper shows that under simplifying assumptions (particularly when considering only label propagation without node features), this stochastic procedure can be reduced to a deterministic training objective composed of a data-fitting term and a graph-dependent regularization term. The authors leverage these theoretical insights to propose several practical extensions, including trainable variants of label propagation and Correct & Smooth, as well as deterministic applications to GNNs with linear propagation layers.

## Main Review:

### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

**(+) Fundamental Problem and Community Relevance**

The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental and important to the graph learning community. The paper addresses a genuine gap between classical label propagation methods and modern message-passing neural networks, two paradigms that have historically operated in relative isolation despite their conceptual similarities. The community would indeed benefit significantly from rigorous research that unifies and clarifies the connections between these two approaches. This work fills an important niche by providing theoretical grounding for a technique that has achieved widespread empirical success without formal justification.

**(+) Well-Motivated Approach with Clear Intuition**

The core insight is well-motivated: the main issue with naive label incorporation is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, where the model simply memorizes training labels without learning meaningful patterns. The proposed solution of using off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix (i.e., the self-excluded label propagation mechanism) is elegant and intuitive. Equation (10), which decomposes the predictor into feature propagation and self-excluded label propagation components, makes intuitive sense and provides clear justification for why this particular decomposition should work. The diagonal masking operation effectively blocks the direct pathway from each training node's input label to its predicted label, thereby preventing label leakage while maintaining the benefits of label information from neighboring nodes.

**(+) Comprehensive Treatment of Label Trick Extensions**

The work provides a thorough examination of the label trick beyond its original heuristic motivation, demonstrating its applicability across multiple scenarios. The authors show how the label trick can be applied to previously parameter-free methods (like label propagation) and non-end-to-end methods (like Correct & Smooth) to make them trainable. This demonstrates the versatility of the framework and provides practitioners with concrete guidance on how to adapt the label trick to different settings. The inclusion of both stochastic (random splits) and deterministic (one-versus-all) variants provides useful flexibility for different computational constraints and graph sizes.

**(–) Attribution and Prior Work**

Some further emphasis and credit should be given to prior work on the label trick, particularly https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf, which the authors cite but may not have fully acknowledged as an early or original proponent of this technique. Given that the authors themselves acknowledge in the introduction that they "recently proposed" the label trick in Wang et al. (2021), there is a question of whether earlier work deserves more prominent recognition. A more thorough discussion of the intellectual history and evolution of combining label and feature propagation would strengthen the paper's positioning and give appropriate credit to related contributions. The paper would benefit from a more detailed related work section that traces the conceptual lineage of these ideas.

**(–) Intuition Behind Key Formulations**

The intuition behind Equation (3) deserves clarification. Why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? This is presented somewhat abruptly without sufficient motivation. From a practical standpoint, one might ask: what does this weight matrix represent in terms of the underlying graph learning problem? Is it learning class correlations, or something else? Similarly, in Section 4.3 on trainable Correct & Smooth, the introduction of trainable weights $W_c$ and $W_s$ in the correction and smoothing steps needs better justification. What properties are these weights learning? How do they interact with the propagation matrices $P_c$ and $P_s$? The paper would benefit from providing more intuition about what these trainable components are actually optimizing for and why this leads to improved performance.

**(–) Limited Comparison with Related Theoretical Work**

Given that the paper has a strong theoretical flavor, it should provide more comprehensive comparison with other works that study the relationship between label and feature propagation. The authors mention Jia & Benson (2021) (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf) only briefly, noting that while their work provides a unifying generative framework, "the parallel propagation of both features and labels is not explicitly considered." However, a more detailed comparison of how the theoretical perspectives differ would be valuable. How does the energy function perspective in this work compare to the generative modeling perspective of Jia & Benson? Are there insights from one framework that could inform the other? Additionally, comparisons with other theoretical analyses of GNNs and label propagation (such as recent work on over-smoothing, expressive power, or generalization bounds) would help position this work within the broader theoretical landscape.

**(–) Clarity of Mathematical Exposition**

Several equations are unclear, and notation is sometimes difficult to parse. The subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague and could be more precisely defined. Equation (5) in particular requires clarification. The transition from the second-to-last term to the final term is not immediately obvious. Since $D_{out}$ has cardinality 1 in this simplified case, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{1 \ldots m\}$, which would simplify to:

$$\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell \left(y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i)W]_i \right)$$

What additional step or insight is needed to arrive at the form presented in the paper? The paper's mathematical exposition, while generally rigorous, could be more accessible. More generally, the paper is at times challenging to read due to its notational density, with excessive use of subscripts and superscripts that can obscure the main ideas. The stream of remarks, while attempting to provide intuition, sometimes interrupts the flow of the argument. The paper would benefit significantly from one more careful pass of rewriting, perhaps with a focus on building intuition before presenting formal results. Consider using more narrative explanation alongside equations, and potentially introducing simplified notation for frequently-used quantities.

**(–) Evaluation Metrics and Experimental Details**

For Table 2, which evaluates regression tasks, the use of $R^2$ (coefficient of determination) would be more appropriate than the current presentation. The $R^2$ metric provides a normalized measure of explained variance that is more interpretable and allows for easier comparison across datasets with different scales and ranges. Additionally, the paper would benefit from more detailed experimental methodology, particularly regarding hyperparameter selection, computational costs, and sensitivity analyses. For instance, how sensitive are the results to the choice of propagation matrix $P$? How does performance vary with different graph sizes and sparsity levels?

**(–) Insufficient Detail in Section 4.1**

Section 4.1 on trainable label propagation is unclear and requires substantially greater detail. The paper states that the methodology can be applied to learn a weight matrix $W$ for label propagation, but the practical implementation is not sufficiently explained. Specifically:
- How exactly is this procedure trained? What is the training objective?
- What is the final model architecture? Is it simply supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of the propagation matrix?
- What is the computational complexity compared to standard label propagation?
- How does this relate to other work on learning or modifying the adjacency/propagation matrix? References should be provided to papers by Wang & Leskovec (2020) and others who have explored learnable propagation matrices.

The discussion of potential benefits (e.g., handling interrelated class labels in multi-label settings) is interesting but remains somewhat speculative without more thorough empirical validation or theoretical analysis.

### Questions:

**(?) Experimental Setup in Figure 1**

Why isn't the deterministic label trick (label trick (D)) used for comparison in the right plot of Figure 1? This would provide useful insight into how the stochastic and deterministic variants compare across different values of $\alpha$. Additionally, which specific GNN architecture is used in the right plot? This appears to be unspecified in Section 5.3, making it difficult to reproduce or interpret the results. Providing this information would strengthen the experimental contribution.

**(?) Regularization and Overfitting in Remark 4**

In Remark 4, the statement that "if the graph has no edges, then there is no chance for overfitting to labels" and the regularization shuts off requires more careful explanation. Why exactly does the absence of

With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

Appendix:
There is several overboxes, please check the log file when compiling latex.

References formatting:
ArXiv -> journal and conference publications
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Overall while having justifications of what people do in practice is nice, I think this paper fall short of providing a satisfactory analysis of the label tricks, especially since it does not rigorously explained it superiority compared to other methods.
I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.

reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: "Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs"

## Summary Of The Paper

The paper focuses on understanding the "label trick," a practical technique used in graph-based semi-supervised learning. In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, with the goal of assigning labels to the unlabeled data. The data points form vertices of a graph, with edges constructed based on similarity of the points (potentially with a weighting scheme). Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data through the graph Laplacian. Another way to leverage graph structure is to build a graph neural network and learn its parameters to match labels of labeled data.

The authors argue that the first method (label propagation) utilizes labels while the second method (GNNs) utilizes features based on a graphical model. They contend that both should be leveraged simultaneously, and argue that the label trick accomplishes this goal. The key insight is that known labels can be used along with features as inputs to a GNN. However, if we only require the model to match labels of labeled data, a trivial mapping (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data) can be learned. To prevent this label leakage, the authors employ a dropout strategy where they hide a few labels (particularly the ground-truth label) when learning to predict the labels of labeled data. This is the essence of the label trick.

The paper attempts to understand the label trick by analyzing simple models, particularly linear variants. It also discusses novel applications of the technique to other graph-based methods.

## Main Review

### Strengths

- **Theoretical Contribution**: It is commendable that the authors take a step back to understand what might appear as "wizard engineering" and demonstrate that the label trick can be understood through analysis of simple models. This demystification of a widely-used technique is valuable to the community. The effort to provide formal theoretical justification for a heuristic that has achieved widespread empirical success on benchmarks like OGB is genuinely useful.

- **Presentation and Clarity**: The paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written overall. The progression from the simple case without node features (Section 3.1) to the full version with both features and labels (Section 3.3) provides good pedagogical structure. The use of remarks to highlight key insights (Remarks 1-5) helps readers understand the implications of the theoretical results.

- **Practical Relevance**: The label trick's adoption across numerous top submissions on the OGB leaderboard and KDDCUP 2021 MAG240M challenge demonstrates its practical importance. The paper's focus on understanding this widely-used technique addresses a genuine gap in the literature.

- **Breadth of Applications**: The paper goes beyond theoretical analysis to propose novel applications, including trainable label propagation (Section 4.1), deterministic variants for linear GNNs (Section 4.2), and trainable Correct and Smooth (Section 4.3). These extensions show how the theoretical insights can be leveraged to improve existing methods.

### Major Weaknesses

#### 1. **Limited Theoretical Insights and Practical Relevance**

While the paper provides mathematical analysis of the label trick, the results are not particularly satisfactory in terms of providing actionable insights. The theoretical analysis largely confirms what practitioners have observed empirically: the label trick works well. However, the paper fails to provide deep understanding of *why* it works better than natural alternatives.

- The main result (Theorem 1 and Corollary 1) shows that the stochastic label trick reduces to a deterministic objective with a data-fitting term and a regularization term. While this decomposition is mathematically clean, it is not particularly surprising. The regularization term proportional to `||ΓW||²_F` where `Γ = (diag(P^T P) - C^T C)^{1/2} Y^tr` is somewhat opaque and does not provide clear intuition for when and why this regularization helps.

- A critical question remains unanswered: why does the full execution of the label trick (with arbitrary random splits) work better than the self-excluded simplification (Section 3.2)? The paper provides the mathematical equivalence but offers no insight into the practical superiority of the stochastic version. The experiments in Figure 1 show that performance varies with α, but this is expected from the theory and does not explain the fundamental advantage of randomness.

- The paper does not rigorously compare the label trick to other natural regularization approaches. For instance, how does the implicit regularization from the label trick compare to explicit graph Laplacian regularization (as in Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al.)? How does it compare to mixup-style regularization (Lopez-Paz et al.)? Without such comparisons, it is difficult to assess whether the label trick is fundamentally superior or just one effective regularization strategy among many.

#### 2. **Lack of Rigor in Key Arguments**

The paper makes several claims that are not sufficiently rigorous or are potentially problematic:

- **Label Leakage Resolution (Remark 1)**: The claim that using `(P - C)Y^tr W` resolves label leakage is intuitive but the paper does not formally define what "label leakage" means or prove that this formulation eliminates it. The blocking of the "direct pathway" through the diagonal subtraction is described informally, but a formal treatment would strengthen this claim. What exactly is the information-theoretic or generalization-theoretic consequence of label leakage, and how does the proposed solution address it?

- **Consistency Between Training and Inference (Remark 2)**: While the paper states that predictions are consistent between training and inference for test nodes, it does not rigorously analyze what happens during training for nodes in D_out. The implicit predictions for test nodes during training (when computing gradients) may still differ from inference predictions in subtle ways due to the stochastic nature of the splits. The paper acknowledges this issue for nonlinear models (Theorem 3) but does not adequately address it for the linear case.

- **Graph-Dependent Regularization (Remark 4)**: The observation that the regularization strength depends on graph structure is interesting, but the analysis is incomplete. The claim that the regularization "naturally balances" with problem size is based on the assumption that edges grow proportionally with nodes, which may not hold in real graphs. The paper provides no empirical validation of this scaling behavior or analysis of how the method performs on graphs with different connectivity patterns.

#### 3. **Problematic Conceptual Claims**

Some of the paper's foundational claims warrant critical examination:

- **Feature vs. Label Propagation Dichotomy**: The paper claims that label propagation and GNNs represent two distinct strategies—one propagating labels, the other features. However, this dichotomy is not as clean as presented. If the graph is constructed based on feature similarity (which is common), then label propagation implicitly leverages feature information through the graph structure. The paper does not adequately address this conflation of feature and structural information.

- **Noisy Features Argument**: The paper argues that "when node features are noisy or unreliable, label propagation may outperform GNNs." However, if features are noisy, the graph constructed from these features (via similarity) will also be noisy. This circular dependency is not addressed. In what scenarios are features unreliable but the graph structure (potentially derived from those same features) reliable? This assumption needs clarification and validation.

- **Smoothness Assumption**: The paper assumes that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph. However, many real-world graphs exhibit homophily violations, where connected nodes have dissimilar labels. The paper does not discuss how the label trick performs on heterophilic graphs or whether the theoretical results hold under such conditions.

#### 4. **Experimental Validation is Insufficient**

The experiments, while demonstrating empirical improvements, do not adequately validate the theoretical predictions or address the main conceptual questions:

- **Theory-Practice Gap**: The theory assumes linear models or specific loss functions (MSE, categorical cross-entropy with specific bounds), yet most experiments use nonlinear GNNs. While Section 3.4 provides some extension to nonlinear models, the connection between theory and experiments remains loose. Figure 1 shows that α has the predicted effect, but this is a relatively weak validation of the theory.

- **Comparison with Alternatives**: Table 3 shows improvements from the deterministic label trick on linear models, but there are no comparisons with other regularization strategies. Are these improvements comparable to those achieved by standard L2 regularization, dropout, or other GNN regularization techniques? Without such baselines, it is impossible to assess the relative merit of the approach.

- **Limited Dataset Diversity**: Most experiments use standard benchmark datasets (Cora-full, Pubmed, OGB datasets). These datasets are relatively well-studied and may not be representative of diverse real-world scenarios. Experiments on graphs with different properties (varying homophily, different sizes,
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.

Main Review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

Appendix:
There is several overboxes, please check the log file when compiling latex.

References formatting:
ArXiv -> journal and conference publications

Summary Of The Review:
Overall while having justifications of what people do in practice is nice, I think this paper fall short of providing a satisfactory analysis of the label tricks, especially since it does not rigorously explained it superiority compared to other methods.
I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. reviews.main_review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
