reviews.summary_of_the_paper: A graph neural network (GNN) pre-processing step for completing missing node features is presented: feature propagation (FP). The method is based on minimisation of Dirichlet energy. A more efficient iterative update aproach is derived avoiding matrix inversion (cubic operation) to a series of sparse-to-dense matrix multiplications. The method acts similar to a low-pass filter for missing features, which complements the GNN learning algorithm. Relevant work is covered and similarities and differences mentioned in many cases. Experiments on seven publicly available datasets show that the method is effective and performs especially well when a large proportion of the feature values are missing (above 90%). A run-time analysis of wall-clock performance compares their method to two state-of-the-art methods for handling missing node features in GNNs and their method is 3 x faster in these comparison. A weakness of their method, FP, is analysed; FP assumes the graphs are homophlic, that nearby nodes have similar node features but if this is not the case then the method does not perform well. This is some interesting future work.
reviews.main_review:
This paper addresses a very interesting problem that is relevant to real-world applications; dealing with missing node features for graph neural networks (GNNs). The approach is based on minimising Dirichlet energy, which is a emthod previously used for graph regularisation and has relations with graph Laplcians, so is well suited to working with data on graph topology. The author(s) derive an efficient iterative algorithm which makes the approach scalable to large graphs which makes it more practical for real-world applications. Many public datasets were used for benchmarking that show very promising results.

In the experiments, it's surprising how well the nearest-neigbour averaging worked. In the comparison method's own papers SAT paper has a 'NeighAggre' baseline that seems to do something similar but gets worse scores than their method and GCNMF compares with kNN with k=5, and this also performs worse than their method. Do you have some insights into why neighbourhood averaging performs so well?

This method is a pre-processing step, not integrated into the GNN, as such many other methods for estimating the missing values could be used. One justification of this choice of approach is that is acts as a low-pass filter, and the GNN mainly learns from the lower frequency components.  This work is very close to matrix completion with graph side information.In this domain there are other methods that are highly scalable and are able to accurately estimate missing values when large amounts of the data (>99%) are missing [1, 2, 4]. Furthermore, one method uses a similar optimisation approach of iterative dense-sparse matrix mulitplication [1]. Worth note is that an extension to [1] will also remove edges in the graph that do not have high-homophily that could improve results for low-himopholy results. Also [3] is a kernel method for matrix completion that allows for a choice of covariance functions, including a diffusion kernel that is therefore similar to this approach. I think it is worth at mentioning this in comparison to this approach and even adding emprical evaluation against these methods, especially [1].

Other small comments:

You report using V100 GPUs (16 or 32Gb?), how much memory did GCNMF take before ran out of memory.

It wasn't crystal clear how the approach works for vector node attributes, do you simply repeat the algorithm for each dimension separately?

"Proposition 1: inverse of Laplacian depends on a connected graph, as eigen values are 0 if graph has clusters." How true is this assumption in real graphs, and what can be done in the case where a graph is not connected?

How do you know 40 iterations is enough, is there a easy guide to this and what happens if you keep running the algorithm, will it eventually make all missing featues almost the same if run for a large number of iterations?

"For a fair comparison, we use the same standard hyperparameters for all methods across all experiments." Does this mean that the downstream 2-layer GCN model has the same hyperparameters? 

Edits:
    A_u should be A_uu ?
    
    Zhu and Ghahramani reference is incomplete

    donwstream task -> downstream task


1. Rao, Nikhil, et al. "Collaborative Filtering with Graph Information: Consistency and Scalable Methods." NIPS. Vol. 2. No. 4. 2015.

2. Strahl, Jonathan, et al. "Scalable probabilistic matrix factorization with graph-based priors." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 04. 2020.

3. Zhou, Tinghui, et al. "Kernelized probabilistic matrix factorization: Exploiting graphs and side information." Proceedings of the 2012 SIAM international Conference on Data mining. Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics, 2012.

4. Monti, Federico, Michael M. Bronstein, and Xavier Bresson. "Geometric matrix completion with recurrent multi-graph neural networks." arXiv preprint arXiv:1704.06803 (2017).
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
As this is a preprocessing step that essentially is similar to matrix completion with graph side information, then I believe that comparison with this literature is missing. Otherwise this is a very good paper. The approach they have for the problem, which could be seen as matrix completion with graph side information, is I believe novel in that domain, as well as in this framework for graph neural networks. Therefore, comparison with this domain is important here, as other methods in this domain could also be well justified as pre-processing steps. One such method that both scalable (using similar final equation, but dervied from a different approach, conjugate gradients), must be compared both in the paper and experiments in my opinion. Otherwise, it feels like the work is trying to avoid comparison with similar methods by claiming this work is related to GNN, while really it feels like this is a matrix completion with graph side information method, that is then used for GNNs.

After feedback:

I still feel that the motivation for using this particular method of estimating the missing values is not clearly better than other approaches. Also, the link to GNN seems weak as essentially this is a method for completing missing data and with this seems better suited to the domain for estimating missing values, where a stronger comparison with other missing values estimating methods can be compared - for example in comparison to recommender system approaches like collaborative filtering with graph side information. The link to GNN could be a small subsection in that style of paper. I don't see why running GNN is such a large part of this work when it's a stand alone preprocessing step. Therefore I've lowered my score from 6 to 5.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary Of The Paper:

A graph neural network (GNN) pre-processing step for completing missing node features is presented: feature propagation (FP). The method is based on minimisation of Dirichlet energy, which is a classical approach in graph signal processing and spectral methods. Rather than solving the closed-form solution via matrix inversion—which incurs O(|V_u|³) computational complexity and is intractable for large graphs—the authors derive a more efficient iterative update approach that avoids explicit matrix inversion in favor of a series of sparse-to-dense matrix multiplications. This reformulation makes the approach significantly more scalable while maintaining theoretical guarantees of convergence to the optimal solution.

The method acts similar to a low-pass filter for missing features (as demonstrated in Figure 2 through graph Fourier analysis), which complements the GNN learning algorithm by reconstructing smooth feature estimates that align with the lower frequency components that GNNs primarily leverage. Relevant work is covered and similarities and differences are mentioned in many cases. Experiments on seven publicly available datasets show that the method is effective and performs especially well when a large proportion of the feature values are missing (above 90%), with remarkably consistent performance degradation of only ~4% relative accuracy drop at 99% missing features. A run-time analysis of wall-clock performance compares their method to two state-of-the-art methods (GCNMF and PaGNN) for handling missing node features in GNNs, demonstrating that their method is 3x faster in these comparisons. The authors also provide detailed analysis of a fundamental weakness of their method: FP assumes the graphs are homophilic, meaning that nearby nodes have similar node features. When this assumption is violated (low homophily scenarios), the method does not perform well, as demonstrated in Section 5 with synthetic datasets of varying homophily levels. This analysis opens up interesting future work directions for developing learnable diffusion operators that can adapt to heterophilic graphs.

## Main Review:

### Strengths and Problem Relevance

This paper addresses a very interesting and practically relevant problem that occurs frequently in real-world applications: dealing with missing node features for graph neural networks (GNNs). The motivation is well-articulated through concrete examples such as demographic information in social networks being available only for a subset of users, product descriptions in co-purchase networks being incomplete, and the increasing role of privacy regulations limiting feature availability. The approach is theoretically grounded, being based on minimising Dirichlet energy—a method previously used for graph regularisation and closely related to graph Laplacians—which is particularly well-suited to working with data on graph topology.

The authors derive an efficient iterative algorithm (Algorithm 1) which makes the approach scalable to large graphs, addressing a critical limitation of previous methods. The ability to handle graphs with millions of nodes and hundreds of millions of edges (OGBN-Products with ~2.5M nodes and ~123M edges) on a single GPU in approximately 10 seconds represents a significant practical advancement over existing approaches that run out of memory on such datasets. This scalability is crucial for real-world deployment.

The theoretical development is sound, with Propositions 1 and 2 providing formal guarantees that the iterative scheme converges to the closed-form solution regardless of initialization. The connection to heat diffusion equations and gradient flows of the Dirichlet energy provides good theoretical motivation. Many public datasets were used for benchmarking that show very promising results, particularly in the extreme data scarcity regime.

### Critical Issue: Missing Comparisons with Matrix Completion Literature

However, there is a significant concern that substantially weakens the contribution's positioning. This work is fundamentally a method for matrix completion with graph side information, yet it does not adequately engage with the substantial literature in that domain. The authors frame this as a GNN preprocessing method, but the core problem—reconstructing missing entries in a feature matrix using graph structure—is the classical matrix completion with graph regularization problem that has been extensively studied in the signal processing and machine learning communities.

The reviewer notes that there exist highly scalable methods in the matrix completion literature that are able to accurately estimate missing values when large amounts of data (>99%) are missing. Notably, some of these methods employ similar optimization approaches based on iterative sparse-to-dense matrix multiplication, such as the collaborative filtering approach with graph information described in Rao et al. (2015). Additionally, kernel methods for matrix completion (Zhou et al., 2012) allow for a choice of covariance functions, including diffusion kernels that are conceptually similar to the approach presented here. The extension mentioned in the reviewer's feedback that removes edges in the graph lacking high homophily could potentially improve results for low-homophily scenarios, suggesting that the current approach may not represent the state-of-the-art even within the matrix completion domain.

The authors acknowledge some matrix completion work (Kalofolias et al., 2014; van den Berg et al., 2017; Monti et al., 2017) but dismiss it as computationally intensive without providing direct empirical comparisons. Since the authors have already demonstrated that their method scales to large graphs efficiently, it would be valuable and necessary to include empirical evaluations against these methods, particularly against methods like Rao et al. (2015) that use similar iterative optimization schemes. This would either validate that Feature Propagation represents a genuine advance over existing matrix completion approaches or would reveal that the contribution is primarily a reframing of existing techniques for the GNN context.

The concern is that by emphasizing the GNN application, the paper may be avoiding direct comparison with more closely related work in the matrix completion and graph signal processing domains. The work would be significantly strengthened by either: (1) providing empirical comparisons with state-of-the-art matrix completion methods, or (2) clearly articulating what is genuinely novel about the approach compared to existing matrix completion techniques and why the GNN application specifically justifies the contribution.

### Experimental Observations and Baseline Performance

In the experiments, it is surprising how well the nearest-neighbor averaging baseline performs, particularly across all missing feature rates. In the comparison methods' own papers (the SAT paper includes a 'NeighAggre' baseline and GCNMF compares with kNN with k=5), these methods perform worse than Feature Propagation, yet the authors' own Neighbor Mean baseline is consistently competitive. The authors provide some intuition in Appendix A.4, explaining that neighborhood averaging works well when features are observed for enough neighbors in homophilic graphs, but as missing rates increase, the estimator variance increases since features may be present for only a few neighbors or none at all. They argue that Feature Propagation allows information to travel longer distances through repeated multiplication by the diffusion matrix. However, this explanation, while reasonable, would benefit from more rigorous analysis. Could the strong performance of Neighbor Mean be due to the specific properties of the benchmark datasets used? An ablation study examining the relationship between graph homophily and the performance gap between Neighbor Mean and FP would strengthen this analysis.

The fact that Neighbor Mean performs so comparably raises questions about the practical significance of the method. While Feature Propagation does outperform Neighbor Mean at very high missing rates (>80%), the improvements are often modest until reaching extreme sparsity levels. For practitioners, understanding when the additional complexity of Feature Propagation is justified compared to the simpler Neighbor Mean approach would be valuable.

### Homophily Limitation and Generalization Concerns

A weakness of the Feature Propagation method is clearly analysed by the authors themselves: FP assumes the graphs are homophilic, meaning that nearby nodes have similar node features. The experimental validation of this limitation using synthetic datasets with varying homophily levels (Figure 5) is commendable and demonstrates scientific rigor. However, this limitation is quite significant for real-world applications, as many important graphs exhibit heterophilic properties. For example, in citation networks, papers citing each other may have quite different research topics; in social networks, people often connect with those who have different characteristics or interests.

The performance degradation in low-homophily scenarios is dramatic. At 99% missing features and zero homophily, Feature Propagation provides almost no benefit over simply setting missing features to zero. This suggests the method may not be suitable for a broad class of graphs encountered in practice. While the authors acknowledge this as future work, the current limitation significantly restricts the applicability of the approach. The proposed solution of using "a learnable non-homogeneous diffusion dependent on the features" is mentioned but not explored, leaving a substantial gap between the current method and a more generally applicable approach.

### Methodological and Clarification Issues

**Vector-valued features:** The extension to vector-valued features is mentioned briefly, stating that the algorithm applies to an n×d feature matrix where "multiplying the diffusion matrix A by the feature matrix X diffuses each feature channel independently." However, this deserves more thorough treatment. The assumption that feature channels are independent may not hold in practice, and treating them independently could lose important correlational structure. The paper would benefit from: (1) explicit discussion of when this independence assumption is reasonable, (2) empirical analysis of whether channel correlation matters for the datasets tested, and (3) discussion of how
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
A graph neural network (GNN) pre-processing step for completing missing node features is presented: feature propagation (FP). The method is based on minimisation of Dirichlet energy. A more efficient iterative update aproach is derived avoiding matrix inversion (cubic operation) to a series of sparse-to-dense matrix multiplications. The method acts similar to a low-pass filter for missing features, which complements the GNN learning algorithm. Relevant work is covered and similarities and differences mentioned in many cases. Experiments on seven publicly available datasets show that the method is effective and performs especially well when a large proportion of the feature values are missing (above 90%). A run-time analysis of wall-clock performance compares their method to two state-of-the-art methods for handling missing node features in GNNs and their method is 3 x faster in these comparison. A weakness of their method, FP, is analysed; FP assumes the graphs are homophlic, that nearby nodes have similar node features but if this is not the case then the method does not perform well. This is some interesting future work.

Main Review:
This paper addresses a very interesting problem that is relevant to real-world applications; dealing with missing node features for graph neural networks (GNNs). The approach is based on minimising Dirichlet energy, which is a emthod previously used for graph regularisation and has relations with graph Laplcians, so is well suited to working with data on graph topology. The author(s) derive an efficient iterative algorithm which makes the approach scalable to large graphs which makes it more practical for real-world applications. Many public datasets were used for benchmarking that show very promising results.

In the experiments, it's surprising how well the nearest-neigbour averaging worked. In the comparison method's own papers SAT paper has a 'NeighAggre' baseline that seems to do something similar but gets worse scores than their method and GCNMF compares with kNN with k=5, and this also performs worse than their method. Do you have some insights into why neighbourhood averaging performs so well?

This method is a pre-processing step, not integrated into the GNN, as such many other methods for estimating the missing values could be used. One justification of this choice of approach is that is acts as a low-pass filter, and the GNN mainly learns from the lower frequency components.  This work is very close to matrix completion with graph side information.In this domain there are other methods that are highly scalable and are able to accurately estimate missing values when large amounts of the data (>99%) are missing [1, 2, 4]. Furthermore, one method uses a similar optimisation approach of iterative dense-sparse matrix mulitplication [1]. Worth note is that an extension to [1] will also remove edges in the graph that do not have high-homophily that could improve results for low-himopholy results. Also [3] is a kernel method for matrix completion that allows for a choice of covariance functions, including a diffusion kernel that is therefore similar to this approach. I think it is worth at mentioning this in comparison to this approach and even adding emprical evaluation against these methods, especially [1].

Other small comments:

You report using V100 GPUs (16 or 32Gb?), how much memory did GCNMF take before ran out of memory.

It wasn't crystal clear how the approach works for vector node attributes, do you simply repeat the algorithm for each dimension separately?

"Proposition 1: inverse of Laplacian depends on a connected graph, as eigen values are 0 if graph has clusters." How true is this assumption in real graphs, and what can be done in the case where a graph is not connected?

How do you know 40 iterations is enough, is there a easy guide to this and what happens if you keep running the algorithm, will it eventually make all missing featues almost the same if run for a large number of iterations?

"For a fair comparison, we use the same standard hyperparameters for all methods across all experiments." Does this mean that the downstream 2-layer GCN model has the same hyperparameters? 

Edits:
    A_u should be A_uu ?
    
    Zhu and Ghahramani reference is incomplete

    donwstream task -> downstream task


1. Rao, Nikhil, et al. "Collaborative Filtering with Graph Information: Consistency and Scalable Methods." NIPS. Vol. 2. No. 4. 2015.

2. Strahl, Jonathan, et al. "Scalable probabilistic matrix factorization with graph-based priors." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 04. 2020.

3. Zhou, Tinghui, et al. "Kernelized probabilistic matrix factorization: Exploiting graphs and side information." Proceedings of the 2012 SIAM international Conference on Data mining. Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics, 2012.

4. Monti, Federico, Michael M. Bronstein, and Xavier Bresson. "Geometric matrix completion with recurrent multi-graph neural networks." arXiv preprint arXiv:1704.06803 (2017).

Summary Of The Review:
As this is a preprocessing step that essentially is similar to matrix completion with graph side information, then I believe that comparison with this literature is missing. Otherwise this is a very good paper. The approach they have for the problem, which could be seen as matrix completion with graph side information, is I believe novel in that domain, as well as in this framework for graph neural networks. Therefore, comparison with this domain is important here, as other methods in this domain could also be well justified as pre-processing steps. One such method that both scalable (using similar final equation, but dervied from a different approach, conjugate gradients), must be compared both in the paper and experiments in my opinion. Otherwise, it feels like the work is trying to avoid comparison with similar methods by claiming this work is related to GNN, while really it feels like this is a matrix completion with graph side information method, that is then used for GNNs.

After feedback:

I still feel that the motivation for using this particular method of estimating the missing values is not clearly better than other approaches. Also, the link to GNN seems weak as essentially this is a method for completing missing data and with this seems better suited to the domain for estimating missing values, where a stronger comparison with other missing values estimating methods can be compared - for example in comparison to recommender system approaches like collaborative filtering with graph side information. The link to GNN could be a small subsection in that style of paper. I don't see why running GNN is such a large part of this work when it's a stand alone preprocessing step. Therefore I've lowered my score from 6 to 5.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a fast way to do node feature imputation in graph learning settings. In a nutshell, the idea is to consider the graph topology in the imputation by doing a feature by feature minimization of the Laplacian quadratic form. However, since the exact solution to this requires a matrix inversion, this quantity is minimized by a gradient descent procedure that relies on sparse matrix multiplications.

Main Review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.

Summary Of The Review:
Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary Of The Paper

This paper proposes Feature Propagation (FP), a fast method for handling missing node features in graph learning settings. The core contribution is a feature imputation approach that minimizes the Dirichlet energy (Laplacian quadratic form) on a per-feature basis while considering the underlying graph topology. Rather than computing the exact solution via matrix inversion—which would require O(|V_u|³) complexity and be intractable for large graphs—the authors employ a gradient descent procedure based on iterative sparse matrix multiplications. The method is simple to implement, requiring only repeated multiplication of the feature matrix by the normalized adjacency matrix while resetting known features to their original values at each iteration. The authors demonstrate through extensive experiments that this approach outperforms existing methods, particularly in extreme scenarios where up to 99% of features are missing, while also achieving superior scalability compared to recent baselines like GCNMF and PaGNN.

## Main Review

### Limited Novelty Relative to Existing Literature

My primary concern regarding this work pertains to its limited novelty. The fundamental theoretical foundation of this paper—using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for feature interpolation and signal reconstruction on graphs—has been extensively explored in the graph signal processing literature over the past decade. The authors themselves acknowledge several of these prior works in their related work section (Narang et al., 2013; Kalofolias et al., 2014; van den Berg et al., 2017; Monti et al., 2017; Rao et al., 2015), yet the novelty over these approaches remains unclear.

More specifically, the idea of replacing the exact closed-form solution (which requires inverting the Laplacian submatrix) with an iterative gradient descent approach is not new. The tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin'on the edge... and beyond" explicitly discusses this trade-off, presenting in Section 2.4.2 a review of standard formulations for signals on graphs and showing that iterative applications of the adjacency matrix (I - L)^k can be interpreted as k gradient descent steps minimizing the Dirichlet energy. This connection between iterative matrix multiplication and gradient descent on the Dirichlet energy objective is presented as an established technique, not a novel contribution.

Furthermore, the relationship between the proposed iterative scheme and filter design in graph signal processing is well-established. The exact solution using the Laplacian inverse can be understood as an Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) filter in the frequency domain, while the iterative procedure proposed here is essentially a Finite Impulse Response (FIR) approximation of this filter. The field of graph filter design has studied this type of approximation extensively over the past seven years, making this perspective on the method largely unsurprising to readers familiar with that literature.

### Relationship to Label Propagation and Prior Work

The authors acknowledge the similarity between their Feature Propagation algorithm and Label Propagation (LP), noting that the key difference lies in the application domain: LP performs direct node classification using only graph structure, while FP reconstructs continuous features for downstream GNN models. However, this distinction, while practically important, does not substantially address the theoretical novelty concern. The underlying mathematical mechanism—iterative diffusion via normalized adjacency multiplication with boundary conditions—is fundamentally the same. The authors' claim that FP is "theoretically motivated" because it emerges as a gradient flow minimizing Dirichlet energy is accurate but not novel, as this derivation and interpretation have appeared in prior work on graph-based semi-supervised learning and signal processing.

Additionally, the paper mentions that the derived scheme is "a special case of Neural Graph PDEs" and relates to the iterative scheme in Zhou & Schölkopf (2004), yet these connections are mentioned only in passing without deep engagement. A more thorough discussion of how this work extends or differs meaningfully from these prior approaches would strengthen the novelty argument.

### Algorithmic Simplicity vs. Novelty

While the simplicity of Algorithm 1 is undoubtedly a practical advantage, simplicity alone does not constitute significant novelty. The algorithm is elegantly straightforward—essentially three lines of code performing feature propagation and resetting known values—but this simplicity stems directly from the well-established theory of Dirichlet energy minimization and gradient descent, not from new algorithmic insights. The choice of h=1 in the Euler scheme discretization, while convenient, is a standard parameter selection that has likely been explored in prior work on graph diffusion methods.

The extension to vector-valued features (treating each channel independently) is presented as a contribution but is a straightforward generalization that follows naturally from the scalar case. The paper states this extension requires "simply replacing the feature vector x with an n×d feature matrix X in Algorithm 1," which suggests minimal novelty in this aspect.

### Theoretical Contributions

Propositions 1 and 2 provide theoretical support for the method. Proposition 1 establishes that the sub-Laplacian matrix is invertible for connected graphs, which is a useful result but not surprising given the spectral properties of graph Laplacians. Proposition 2 proves convergence of the iterative scheme to the closed-form solution, which is valuable but again follows relatively directly from the spectral analysis of the normalized adjacency matrix and the fact that its spectral radius is less than one (Lemma 1). These proofs, while correct and useful, represent incremental theoretical contributions rather than fundamental advances.

### Experimental Validation and Practical Contributions

The experimental evaluation is comprehensive and demonstrates strong empirical performance. The results showing only ~4% relative accuracy drop with 99% missing features (Table 1) versus 21.25% for PaGNN and 58.33% for GCNMF are impressive from a practical standpoint. The scalability demonstration on OGBN-Products (2.5M nodes, 123M edges) completing in ~10 seconds is noteworthy, and the fact that competing methods (GCNMF and PaGNN) run out of memory on this dataset is significant for practitioners.

However, strong empirical results, while valuable, do not fully compensate for limited theoretical novelty. The experiments effectively demonstrate that a simple, well-understood technique performs better than more complex recent methods, which is useful but somewhat orthogonal to the novelty question.

### Limitations and Scope

The authors appropriately acknowledge that their method is designed for homophilic graphs and suffers in low-homophily scenarios (Section 5 and Figure 5). The experiments on synthetic datasets with varying homophily clearly show that FP's advantage diminishes as homophily decreases, with performance approaching the zero baseline in extreme cases. This is an important limitation that restricts the applicability of the method. The authors suggest that "a more general learnable diffusion could be adopted to perform well in low homophily scenarios," but this remains future work. Additionally, the method treats feature channels independently and does not account for inter-channel dependencies, which could be important for some applications.

The restriction to homogeneous graphs (single node and edge type) is another limitation, though the authors note this could be extended to heterogeneous settings. The claim that FP is "generic" and can be combined with any GNN is somewhat weakened by these domain restrictions.

### Missing Comparisons and Analysis

The paper does not compare against some relevant graph signal processing baselines beyond MGCNN. Given the strong connection to graph signal interpolation literature, more extensive comparison with classical signal processing approaches would strengthen the paper. Additionally, while the paper shows FP outperforms Label Propagation even at 99% missing features, a deeper analysis of why feature reconstruction is beneficial compared to structure-only methods would be insightful.

The paper also lacks ablation studies on key design choices. For instance, why is 40 iterations the right choice for convergence? How sensitive is the method to this parameter? How does the choice of zero initialization for unknown features affect performance compared to other initialization schemes?

## Summary Of The Review

From the perspective of graph signal processing and prior work on Dirichlet energy minimization, this paper's theoretical contributions are limited. The core idea of minimizing Dirichlet energy via iterative gradient descent is well-established in the literature, and the application to feature imputation, while practical, does not represent a significant conceptual advance. The iterative scheme is a standard FIR approximation of the IIR solution, a technique that has been studied extensively in graph filter design. The theoretical results (Propositions 1 and 2) are sound but represent incremental contributions that follow relatively straightforwardly from spectral graph theory.

The main strengths of the paper lie in its empirical validation and practical utility: the method is simple to implement, highly scalable, and demonstrates strong performance particularly in extreme missing feature scenarios. However, these practical contributions, while valuable to practitioners, do not sufficiently offset the limited novelty in methodology and theory. The paper makes a solid engineering contribution by showing that a simple, well-understood approach outperforms more complex recent methods, but it does not present fundamentally new insights into the problem of handling missing features in graphs.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper address the problem of  missing node features in graph by conducting feature propagation (FP). An iterative updated algorithm is presented. FP is a preprocessing step and can be used in multiple downstream tasks. Experiments on node classification shows the effectiveness of FP. The authors also provided comparison on running time and showed how homophily affects the performance of FP.
reviews.main_review:
The strengths:

1. The paper is clearly written and easy to follow
2. The performance on missing 99% of the features is impressive
3. The speed improvement is promising

The weaknesses:

1. FP is not a new idea/algorithm, it is essentially same as label propagation (LP) by Zhu et al. [1]. Although the authors argued that "Differently from our setting of diffusion of continuous node features, they deal with discrete label classes directly, resulting in a different diffusion operator.", from my understanding, there is not substantial difference. LP also propagate the label probability, which is continuous. The derivation, the update formula of FP are the same as LP, the only difference is to substitute the weight matrix in LP with Laplacian matrix. Interpreting label propagation via Dirchlet energy is also proposed in [2], where instead of using $x_i$, $x_j$, Solomon et al. were using $f_i$, $f_j$, which are label function of node $i$ and $j$. Consider $x_i$'s as the soft label with label function $f_i$, then they are essentially the same thing.
2. The experiment part shows that simple averaging of the neighbors gives good result when the missing portion is $\leq$ 50%. In that case, I think it is also important to show experimental results of algorithms that do not use node features, to see if there is really a gap on the performance. (Note that under strong assumption of homophily, it is not surprising that simple averaging of neighbors works well. FP or LP takes account the topology of the graph and changes simple averaging of neighbors to weighted average of the whole network. The global information of the graph is the key to a better performance in this case, and I doubt other algorithms using the global information can also get similar good performance.) For example, one can use simple label propagation on the node classes, or using LINE, 
DeepWalk or node2vec to get a embedding of each node and then classifying with the embeddings. If the performance is similar as what FP gives when there are 99% features missing, I would argue that in that case we can simply go with classical algorithms using only the network topology. We will only use GNN when we have enough features available, while in which case it seems simple averaging of neighbors is good enough. The paper is not so convincing at the current stage.

[1] Xiaojin, Zhu, and Ghahramani Zoubin. "Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation." Tech. Rep., Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, Carnegie Mellon University (2002).

[2] Solomon, Justin, Raif Rustamov, Leonidas Guibas, and Adrian Butscher. "Wasserstein propagation for semi-supervised learning." In International Conference on Machine Learning, pp. 306-314. PMLR, 2014.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: In general I like the idea of using FP as a preprocessing step. However, I am not convinced that the idea is novel enough to be accepted in ICLR, details are referred to my main review.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary Of The Paper:

The paper addresses the problem of missing node features in graphs by proposing Feature Propagation (FP), an iterative algorithm for feature reconstruction. FP is presented as a preprocessing step derived from Dirichlet energy minimization, which can be combined with any downstream GNN model for node classification tasks. The authors provide theoretical justification through gradient flow analysis, demonstrate empirical effectiveness across multiple benchmarks, and analyze computational efficiency. The paper also includes an analysis of how graph homophily affects FP's performance across varying rates of missing features.

## Main Review:

### Strengths:

1. **Clear presentation and accessibility**: The paper is well-written and easy to follow. The progression from the Dirichlet energy minimization objective through the gradient flow formulation to the final iterative algorithm is logically sound and clearly presented. The mathematical exposition in Section 3 effectively motivates the approach, and Figure 1 provides useful intuition about the overall framework.

2. **Impressive empirical robustness at extreme missing rates**: The performance degradation at 99% missing features is genuinely striking. The observation that FP achieves only a 4.12% relative accuracy drop on average when 99% of features are missing (compared to 58.33% for GCNMF and 21.25% for PaGNN) represents a substantial practical advantage. This robustness across diverse datasets (Cora, CiteSeer, PubMed, Photo, Computers, OGBN-Arxiv, OGBN-Products) suggests the method's broad applicability.

3. **Significant computational advantages**: The speed improvements are noteworthy and practically important. FP takes only ~10 seconds on OGBN-Products (2.5M nodes, 123M edges) on a single GPU, making it approximately 3x faster than competing methods (PaGNN and GCNMF). Moreover, unlike these baselines, FP successfully scales to large graphs without running out of memory, which is a critical practical consideration for real-world applications.

### Weaknesses:

1. **Limited novelty - fundamental similarity to Label Propagation**: The core concern regarding novelty is substantial and not adequately addressed by the authors. FP is essentially the same algorithm as Label Propagation (LP) by Zhu & Ghahramani (2002), with the primary difference being the substitution of the weight matrix with a Laplacian-based diffusion operator. While the authors claim that "Differently from our setting of diffusion of continuous node features, they deal with discrete label classes directly, resulting in a different diffusion operator," this distinction is largely superficial from an algorithmic perspective.

   The fundamental mathematical operation is identical: both methods iteratively multiply feature/label vectors by a normalized adjacency or Laplacian matrix, with known values reset to their original values. The iterative scheme presented in Equation (2), namely x^(k+1) = [I, Ã_uk; 0, Ã_uu] x^(k), is directly analogous to the label propagation update rule. Label Propagation also operates on continuous probability distributions over classes, not strictly discrete labels, making the distinction between "discrete" and "continuous" features somewhat artificial.

   Furthermore, the connection between Label Propagation and Dirichlet energy minimization is not novel. As the authors acknowledge in their related work section, Solomon et al. (2014) already interpreted label propagation through the lens of Dirichlet energy minimization using label functions f_i and f_j. If one views node features x_i as soft labels or continuous feature functions, the mathematical formulation becomes essentially identical to existing work. The authors' contribution is primarily a reinterpretation and reapplication of a well-established algorithm to a different problem setting (feature reconstruction rather than label propagation), rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic innovation.

   The paper would be significantly strengthened by explicitly acknowledging this fundamental similarity and clearly articulating what conceptual or practical novelty is being introduced beyond the application domain. The current framing suggests greater algorithmic novelty than actually exists.

2. **Incomplete experimental analysis regarding necessity of features**: The experimental evaluation, while comprehensive in some respects, lacks crucial comparisons that would establish whether reconstructed features actually provide value over purely topology-based methods. The authors present results showing that simple averaging of neighbors performs well up to 50% missing features, but they do not adequately explore what happens when one abandons feature-based approaches entirely in favor of topology-only methods.

   Specifically, under the strong assumption of homophily (which the authors themselves identify as essential for FP's success), the global graph structure contains significant predictive signal. The paper would benefit from comprehensive comparison against classical topology-based learning methods such as: (1) Label Propagation applied directly to node classes, (2) embedding-based methods like LINE, DeepWalk, or node2vec followed by simple classification, and (3) purely graph-structure-based approaches that make no use of node features whatsoever.

   If these topology-only baselines achieve similar performance to FP+GNN when 99% of features are missing, this would suggest that in extreme missing data regimes, the additional complexity of feature reconstruction and downstream GNN application may be unnecessary. The authors would then need to better justify when and why practitioners should employ their method rather than simpler alternatives. Conversely, if FP+GNN substantially outperforms these baselines, this would provide stronger evidence for the practical value of feature reconstruction. The current experimental design does not adequately address this critical question.

   Moreover, the paper's claim that "reconstructing the missing features using FP is indeed useful for the downstream task" (stated after Figure 3) relies primarily on comparison to Label Propagation. However, Label Propagation is not optimized for the feature reconstruction task and operates under different assumptions. A more direct comparison would pit FP+GNN against Label Propagation on the same classification task, with both methods having equal access to the graph structure.

3. **Limited scope regarding applicability and assumptions**: The paper identifies homophily as a critical requirement for FP's effectiveness and demonstrates this empirically in Section 5 (Figure 5). The experiments on synthetic datasets with varying homophily levels clearly show that FP's advantage diminishes dramatically as homophily decreases, with performance approaching the Zero baseline in low-homophily regimes. This is a significant limitation that substantially restricts the method's applicability.

   While the authors acknowledge this limitation and suggest that "a more general learnable diffusion could be adopted to perform well in low homophily scenarios," they provide no concrete solution or pathway forward. Given that heterophilic graphs are increasingly recognized as important in the GNN literature (as evidenced by recent work on heterophilic GNNs), this limitation is particularly concerning. The paper would be strengthened by either: (1) proposing and evaluating a learnable variant of the diffusion operator that maintains computational efficiency while adapting to heterophilic settings, or (2) providing more detailed guidance on when practitioners can safely apply FP and when alternative methods should be preferred.

   Additionally, the extension to vector-valued features is mentioned only briefly, with the statement that "multiplying the diffusion matrix A by the feature matrix X diffuses each feature channel independently." This independence assumption may be problematic in scenarios where feature channels exhibit strong dependencies or where cross-channel information could improve reconstruction. The paper does not discuss whether incorporating channel mixing or other dependencies could enhance performance, nor does it empirically validate that the independence assumption is reasonable for the datasets considered.

4. **Insufficient analysis of the relationship to existing diffusion-based methods**: While the paper mentions that the derived scheme is "a special case of Neural Graph PDEs" and relates to work by Zhou & Schölkopf (2004), the discussion of these connections is superficial. The paper does not clearly delineate what is genuinely novel compared to these existing frameworks. The discretization of diffusion equations on graphs is not new, and the paper would benefit from a more thorough comparison with related continuous and discrete diffusion-based approaches in the literature.

   Furthermore, the paper's claim that FP "can be interpreted as a low-pass filter" (supported by Figure 2) connects to well-established signal processing theory on graphs. However, the implications of this low-pass filtering property are not fully explored. For instance, if FP necessarily suppresses high-frequency components, this may explain both its successes (in homophilic settings) and failures (in heterophilic settings), but the paper does not deeply analyze these tradeoffs.

### Additional Observations:

**On the theoretical contributions**: While Propositions 1 and 2 establish that the iterative scheme converges to the closed-form solution of the Dirichlet energy minimization problem, these results are relatively straightforward given the mathematical framework. Proposition 1 (invertibility of sub-Laplacian matrices) is a known result in spectral graph theory, and Proposition 2 follows directly from standard convergence analysis of iterative schemes. The theoretical novelty is therefore limited.

**On the experimental setup**: The experimental setup is generally sound, with appropriate train/validation/test splits and multiple runs to report standar
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper address the problem of  missing node features in graph by conducting feature propagation (FP). An iterative updated algorithm is presented. FP is a preprocessing step and can be used in multiple downstream tasks. Experiments on node classification shows the effectiveness of FP. The authors also provided comparison on running time and showed how homophily affects the performance of FP.

Main Review:
The strengths:

1. The paper is clearly written and easy to follow
2. The performance on missing 99% of the features is impressive
3. The speed improvement is promising

The weaknesses:

1. FP is not a new idea/algorithm, it is essentially same as label propagation (LP) by Zhu et al. [1]. Although the authors argued that "Differently from our setting of diffusion of continuous node features, they deal with discrete label classes directly, resulting in a different diffusion operator.", from my understanding, there is not substantial difference. LP also propagate the label probability, which is continuous. The derivation, the update formula of FP are the same as LP, the only difference is to substitute the weight matrix in LP with Laplacian matrix. Interpreting label propagation via Dirchlet energy is also proposed in [2], where instead of using $x_i$, $x_j$, Solomon et al. were using $f_i$, $f_j$, which are label function of node $i$ and $j$. Consider $x_i$'s as the soft label with label function $f_i$, then they are essentially the same thing.
2. The experiment part shows that simple averaging of the neighbors gives good result when the missing portion is $\leq$ 50%. In that case, I think it is also important to show experimental results of algorithms that do not use node features, to see if there is really a gap on the performance. (Note that under strong assumption of homophily, it is not surprising that simple averaging of neighbors works well. FP or LP takes account the topology of the graph and changes simple averaging of neighbors to weighted average of the whole network. The global information of the graph is the key to a better performance in this case, and I doubt other algorithms using the global information can also get similar good performance.) For example, one can use simple label propagation on the node classes, or using LINE, 
DeepWalk or node2vec to get a embedding of each node and then classifying with the embeddings. If the performance is similar as what FP gives when there are 99% features missing, I would argue that in that case we can simply go with classical algorithms using only the network topology. We will only use GNN when we have enough features available, while in which case it seems simple averaging of neighbors is good enough. The paper is not so convincing at the current stage.

[1] Xiaojin, Zhu, and Ghahramani Zoubin. "Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation." Tech. Rep., Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, Carnegie Mellon University (2002).

[2] Solomon, Justin, Raif Rustamov, Leonidas Guibas, and Adrian Butscher. "Wasserstein propagation for semi-supervised learning." In International Conference on Machine Learning, pp. 306-314. PMLR, 2014.

Summary Of The Review:
In general I like the idea of using FP as a preprocessing step. However, I am not convinced that the idea is novel enough to be accepted in ICLR, details are referred to my main review.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Learning Audio-Visual Dereverberation

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines an audio-visual approach for dereverberation, where dereverberation of speech is conditioned on RGB and depth images (either field-of-view or panoramic). It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful. A lot of existing synthetic reverb for such audio tasks is generated using shoebox room simulators, so having more realistic environments seems quite useful. The accompanying data recorded in real environments using loudspeaker playback (such that the clean reference is available) is also quite useful, and I hope the authors plan to release a recipe for this data.

S3) The paper includes a lot of ablations, which I really appreciate. Though the focus of the paper is on audio-visual approaches to the task, I'm really glad the paper evaluates audio-only versions of the model, as well as other components such as the matching loss.

S4) The paper is clear and well-written.

### Weaknesses:

W1) I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, and I feel like I heard a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in some examples: for moderate reverb, the model predictions have a certain "buzziness" to them, and more extreme levels of reverb have a "twanginess" to them. Something that would make this paper stronger would be a subjective human listening evaluation, e.g. MUSHRA, which would measure these kinds of artifacts better than an objective measure like PESQ. Also, alternative loss functions may help reduce artifacts. E.g. L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE on power-compressed spectrograms can provide more perceptually-relevant loss functions.

W2) The processing method of using 2.56s frames, then concatenating the non-overlapping parts together seems suboptimal to me. Why not use e.g. a Hann window to stitch the predicted patches with overlap-add, or invert back to the time-domain and do overlap-add and train through this?

W3) Where does the "random speech embedding sampled from the data batch." come from? How is it computed? Is this a speaker ID embedding?

W4) The improvements by adding visual information are marginal compared to the audio-only model. The audio-only model does perform better than the baselines, but degrades PESQ compared to other baselines on real data. To benchmark the audio-only baseline against the literature, it would be interesting to compare to competitive systems in e.g. the REVERB challenge. I'm also not very convinced that visual input really helps that much.

W5) Table 1 would be improved by adding trainable parameter counts. Is the proposed model only doing better because it has more trainable parameters? WPE itself has no trainable parameters, since it's just a model-based EM algorithm.

W6) The lack of noise in the training and eval data limits real-world applicability, but I understand the focus of the paper in only on dereverberation. I'm glad that the authors tested noise robustness with WHAM! data, but 20 dB is a pretty high SNR for noise robustness. It would be interesting to see how performance degrades with decreasing SNR.

### Minor comments

M1) I feel that this statement isn't quite right: "R is a function of...the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener." I would argue that an RIR can be a function of the absolute positioning of the speaker and the listener. E.g. what if a source is close to a wall, versus in the the middle of a room?

M2) For the real data, would it be possible to be more specific about the source-to-microphone distances used in the real recordings, beyond "near-field to mid-field to far-field."?

M3) Also concerning real data: would it be possible to more more specific for this statement? "For each location, we play around 10 utterances.". How many utterances? Gender balanced? Different speakers across rooms?

M4) Nit: U-Net instead of UNet?

M5) "While our model is agnostic to the audio source type,": depends on the training data.

M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I would say "anechoic speech (upper bound)" instead of "clean speech (upper bound)", because to me "clean" suggests there's additive noise.

M7) About the blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions: "After converging, the average errors for them are 3e-3s, 160 dB and 17.92m respectively." Are these absolute or squared errors?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I vote for marginal acceptance. This is an interesting novel task (audio-visual dereverberation), and the proposed synthetic and real data are quite interesting and useful for the community. But the paper's results show that visual input provides marginal performance gain over the audio-only baseline. The audio-only baseline outperforms some benchmarks in terms of PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic data, and WER and EER for real data, but I have concerns about the subjective listening quality of the demos, compared to other dereverberation algorithms I have listened to. Thus, I am concerned that this isn't a significant improvement over audio-only prior work, at least in terms of subjective listening quality.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines an audio-visual approach for dereverberation, where dereverberation of speech is conditioned on RGB and depth images (either field-of-view or panoramic). It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.

Main Review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful. A lot of existing synthetic reverb for such audio tasks is generated using shoebox room simulators, so having more realistic environments seems quite useful. The accompanying data recorded in real environments using loudspeaker playback (such that the clean reference is available) is also quite useful, and I hope the authors plan to release a recipe for this data.

S3) The paper includes a lot of ablations, which I really appreciate. Though the focus of the paper is on audio-visual approaches to the task, I'm really glad the paper evaluates audio-only versions of the model, as well as other components such as the matching loss.

S4) The paper is clear and well-written.

### Weaknesses:

W1) I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, and I feel like I heard a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in some examples: for moderate reverb, the model predictions have a certain "buzziness" to them, and more extreme levels of reverb have a "twanginess" to them. Something that would make this paper stronger would be a subjective human listening evaluation, e.g. MUSHRA, which would measure these kinds of artifacts better than an objective measure like PESQ. Also, alternative loss functions may help reduce artifacts. E.g. L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE on power-compressed spectrograms can provide more perceptually-relevant loss functions.

W2) The processing method of using 2.56s frames, then concatenating the non-overlapping parts together seems suboptimal to me. Why not use e.g. a Hann window to stitch the predicted patches with overlap-add, or invert back to the time-domain and do overlap-add and train through this?

W3) Where does the "random speech embedding sampled from the data batch." come from? How is it computed? Is this a speaker ID embedding?

W4) The improvements by adding visual information are marginal compared to the audio-only model. The audio-only model does perform better than the baselines, but degrades PESQ compared to other baselines on real data. To benchmark the audio-only baseline against the literature, it would be interesting to compare to competitive systems in e.g. the REVERB challenge. I'm also not very convinced that visual input really helps that much.

W5) Table 1 would be improved by adding trainable parameter counts. Is the proposed model only doing better because it has more trainable parameters? WPE itself has no trainable parameters, since it's just a model-based EM algorithm.

W6) The lack of noise in the training and eval data limits real-world applicability, but I understand the focus of the paper in only on dereverberation. I'm glad that the authors tested noise robustness with WHAM! data, but 20 dB is a pretty high SNR for noise robustness. It would be interesting to see how performance degrades with decreasing SNR.

### Minor comments

M1) I feel that this statement isn't quite right: "R is a function of...the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener." I would argue that an RIR can be a function of the absolute positioning of the speaker and the listener. E.g. what if a source is close to a wall, versus in the the middle of a room?

M2) For the real data, would it be possible to be more specific about the source-to-microphone distances used in the real recordings, beyond "near-field to mid-field to far-field."?

M3) Also concerning real data: would it be possible to more more specific for this statement? "For each location, we play around 10 utterances.". How many utterances? Gender balanced? Different speakers across rooms?

M4) Nit: U-Net instead of UNet?

M5) "While our model is agnostic to the audio source type,": depends on the training data.

M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I would say "anechoic speech (upper bound)" instead of "clean speech (upper bound)", because to me "clean" suggests there's additive noise.

M7) About the blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions: "After converging, the average errors for them are 3e-3s, 160 dB and 17.92m respectively." Are these absolute or squared errors?

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I vote for marginal acceptance. This is an interesting novel task (audio-visual dereverberation), and the proposed synthetic and real data are quite interesting and useful for the community. But the paper's results show that visual input provides marginal performance gain over the audio-only baseline. The audio-only baseline outperforms some benchmarks in terms of PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic data, and WER and EER for real data, but I have concerns about the subjective listening quality of the demos, compared to other dereverberation algorithms I have listened to. Thus, I am concerned that this isn't a significant improvement over audio-only prior work, at least in terms of subjective listening quality.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: Learning Audio-Visual Dereverberation

## Summary Of The Paper:

This paper introduces an audio-visual approach to speech dereverberation, where the dereverberation process is conditioned on both RGB and depth images (captured either as field-of-view or panoramic views) along with the reverberant audio signal. The core contribution is the VIDA (Visually-Informed Dereverberation of Audio) model, which leverages a Visual Acoustics Network (VAN) to extract room acoustic features from visual inputs and combines these with a UNet-based dereverberation module. To support this novel task, the authors develop a comprehensive dataset leveraging the SoundSpaces simulator with 82 Matterport3D environments and LibriSpeech speech data, allowing for controlled generation of reverberant audio with known ground truth. The paper also includes real-world data collected using iPhone 11 panoramic images and a ZYLIA ZM-1 microphone across diverse acoustic environments. The model is evaluated on three downstream tasks—speech enhancement (PESQ), automatic speech recognition (WER), and speaker verification (EER)—on both synthetic and real data. While the audio-visual model demonstrates improvements over an audio-only baseline, the gains are relatively modest. The audio-only version itself outperforms several literature baselines on synthetic data, though results are more mixed on real data.

## Main Review:

### Strengths:

**S1) Novel task formulation and first audio-visual dereverberation approach.** To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation in the literature. The motivation is compelling and well-articulated: the visual stream provides rich information about room geometry, materials, object locations, and speaker positioning that should theoretically inform the dereverberation process. The authors make a convincing case that this information is complementary to what can be inferred from audio alone, particularly in practical scenarios such as video conferencing and augmented reality where both modalities are naturally available. The task formulation itself represents a meaningful contribution to the field, opening up a new research direction at the intersection of audio-visual learning and speech enhancement.

**S2) High-quality and well-motivated dataset contribution.** The proposed dataset represents a significant contribution that extends beyond this single paper. Rather than relying on traditional shoebox room simulators that produce overly simplistic acoustic environments, the authors leverage SoundSpaces with real-world 3D scans from Matterport3D, offering substantially more realistic and diverse acoustic environments. The use of 82 large-scale multi-room homes provides considerable variability in room acoustics, as evidenced by the RT60 distribution shown in Table 4 of the supplementary materials, ranging from 0.211s in bedrooms to 0.393s in conference rooms. The dataset construction methodology is particularly well-designed: by inserting clean speech at known source positions, convolving with precomputed RIRs, and rendering synchronized RGB and depth images at receiver positions, the authors maintain perfect alignment between visual and acoustic properties while preserving ground truth clean audio. The accompanying real-world data collection effort is also commendable, involving recordings across diverse environments (auditoriums, meeting rooms, atriums, corridors, classrooms) with varying speaker distances (near-field to far-field) and ambient sounds. The commitment to publicly release both code and data (if accepted) further enhances the value of this contribution to the research community. Such a dataset could enable future work on audio-visual room acoustics understanding beyond just dereverberation.

**S3) Comprehensive experimental evaluation with extensive ablations.** The paper includes a thorough ablation study that provides valuable insights into the model's behavior and design choices. The authors evaluate not only the full VIDA model but also critical variants: audio-only dereverberation (removing the VAN entirely and the matching loss), VIDA without the matching loss, VIDA without the 3D humanoid mesh, and VIDA with random images. These ablations clearly demonstrate that each component contributes to performance: removing the matching loss degrades WER from 4.44% to 4.57% (2.9% relative), omitting the human mesh increases WER to 4.59%, and random images increase it to 4.70%. The paper also provides ablations on visual sensor modalities (Table 7), showing that both RGB and depth are necessary—removing either one degrades performance, with depth being particularly important for capturing room geometry. The comparison with R-vectors (Section A.10) and with models augmented with predicted room acoustics characteristics (Table 6) further strengthens the evaluation by demonstrating that simply providing room acoustic parameters as auxiliary information is insufficient compared to learning end-to-end visual representations. Additionally, the analysis of learned visual features via t-SNE projections (Figure 4c and 4d) reveals that the model discovers semantically meaningful properties like RT60 and room volume without explicit supervision, suggesting that the visual encoding captures genuine acoustic properties of the environment.

**S4) Clear presentation and well-structured paper.** The paper is well-written with clear motivation, problem formulation, and methodology. The visual presentation is effective: Figure 1 provides compelling motivation by showing how identical speech utterances exhibit different reverberation characteristics in different environments; Figure 3 clearly illustrates the model architecture; and Figure 5 shows qualitative examples of the dereverberation results. The task formulation in Section 3 is mathematically precise, properly introducing the RIR convolution model and clearly stating the prediction objective. The related work section effectively positions this work relative to prior efforts in audio dereverberation, visual room acoustics estimation, and audio-visual learning, making clear distinctions from prior work (e.g., how this differs from previous methods that use unknown camera-microphone offsets or focus on lip-reading for source separation).

### Weaknesses:

**W1) Subjective audio quality concerns and lack of perceptual evaluation.** While I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, I observed a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in several examples that raises concerns about the perceptual quality of the enhancement. For moderate levels of reverberation, the model's predictions exhibit a noticeable "buzziness" or metallic quality, while at more extreme reverberation levels, a "twanginess" becomes apparent. These artifacts suggest that while the model may be optimizing the chosen objective metrics (PESQ, WER, EER), it may not be producing enhancement that sounds natural to human listeners. A subjective human listening evaluation using standard methodologies such as MUSHRA (Mean Opinion Score variants) would be significantly more informative than objective metrics alone for assessing the perceptual quality of the enhanced speech. Such evaluation would directly measure listener preferences and perceptual artifacts in a way that PESQ cannot capture. The reliance on PESQ is particularly problematic because this metric has known limitations in correlating with human perception, especially for speech enhancement tasks where artifacts can be present even when PESQ scores are reasonable. Additionally, the choice of loss function may be contributing to these artifacts. The current approach uses MSE loss on magnitude and phase spectrograms separately (Equations 1-2), which is known to produce perceptually suboptimal results. Alternative formulations such as L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE computed on power-compressed spectrograms (e.g., mel-scale or Bark-scale), would provide more perceptually-relevant optimization objectives. Time-domain losses or losses that incorporate perceptual weighting could also reduce the kinds of artifacts observed in the demos. The Griffin-Lim algorithm used for phase reconstruction (30 iterations) may also contribute to artifacts, and more sophisticated phase reconstruction methods from the recent literature could be explored.

**W2) Suboptimal spectrogram stitching and reconstruction procedure.** The current approach of segmenting spectrograms into fixed-size 256×256 chunks (approximately 2.56 seconds each) with 50% overlap, processing each independently, and then concatenating only the non-overlapping middle 50% of frames seems unnecessarily crude and potentially suboptimal. This approach discards half of the model's output at each boundary, which is wasteful and may introduce discontinuities. A more principled approach would be to apply a Hann window or similar smooth windowing function to the predicted spectrogram patches with overlap-add reconstruction, which would ensure smooth transitions between adjacent segments and make full use of the model's predictions. Alternatively, the authors could train the model end-to-end through the overlap-add process in the time domain, using the inverse STFT and overlap-add operations as differentiable layers during training. This would allow the model to learn to produce outputs that reconstruct smoothly across boundaries. The current approach essentially treats each 2.56-second segment as independent, which may prevent the model from learning temporal coherence across longer timescales. The choice of 2.56-second segments also seems arbitrary—no justification is provided for why this particular window length was selected, and it's unclear whether this is optimal or merely convenient given the 256×256 input constraint of the UNet. A more adaptive approach that processes variable-length sequences (e.g., using recurrent or attention-based architectures

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines an audio-visual approach for dereverberation, where dereverberation of speech is conditioned on RGB and depth images (either field-of-view or panoramic). It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.

Main Review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful.
