Summary Of The Paper:
This paper target the task of automatically determining the best augmentation method to obtain improved accuracy. While the previous related studies focus on the image-level augmentation and ignore the semantic information of the augmented images, the proposed algorithm augments the grid-wise patches of the given input with the preserved semantic information. To overcome the enlarged number of combinations to consider all the patches, the algorithm utilizes the MARL algorithm with the unified reward function. By MARL algorithm, the number of parameters can be reduced and the training speed can be much improved, compared to the previous auto-augmentation methods. Through the image classification and fine-grained image recognition tasks, the proposed algorithm was validated, and it shows the state-of-the-art performance among the compared methods.

Main Review:
Strengths
+ It seems effective to utilize the MARL algorithm to reduce the computational complexity for the multiple patches.
+ As a result, the computation cost of the proposed algorithm becomes much reduced when it is compared to the previous auto-augmentation algorithms. Furthermore, the algorithm can select the optimal combinations at every epoch without any additional computations, which can improve its generality across the various tasks.

Weaknesses & Questions
- About state and observation. In the proposed algorithm, the state and the observation are obtained by the deep feature from the trained model. My question is the image to obtain the deep feature. When the image is the original image before the determined augmentation, the state and the observation are not affected by the selected action. Then, the best action can be determined just by one simple simulation, and no reinforcement learning algorithm is necessary to find the optimal augmentation. If the augmented images are fed into the model again, the computation cost cannot be reduced as shown in the experiments. What is the detailed sequence to obtain the deep feature?
- Since the deep feature is utilized, its invariant property can affect the determination of the optimal augmentation. In other words, when the trained model is robust to the translation shift, the deep features from the model would be similar even after various translation shifts. Then, the training loss becomes also similar, which results in no selection of translation augmentation. This means that the algorithm would select only the augmentation method that is not considered in the initial model, and this can work as one of the biased optimizations for the optimal augmentation methods. Is there any analysis about the robustness of the initial model for the deep features?
- In the experiments, the proposed algorithm is only compared with the out-of-date algorithms, so its performance cannot be validated well. I recommend the authors add the state-of-the-art studies related to auto-augmentation and the learning-based augmentation algorithms, which include Saliency-mix[1] and Co-mixup[2].
- What would be the limitation of the proposed algorithm?

[1] Uddin et al. "SaliencyMix: A Saliency Guided Data Augmentation Strategy for Better Regularization", ICLR2021
[2] Kim et al. "Co-Mixup: Saliency Guided Joint Mixup with Supermod-ular  Diversity", ICLR2021

Summary Of The Review:
As I said above, I have some questions about the necessity of the reinforcement learning-based algorithm in the framework. In addition, the comparison with the state-of-the-art algorithms should be added for the validation. I will change my score when I solve the above issues.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper proposes an evolution of the traditional
  pipeline of image data augmentation used to reduce ML
  model overfitting.

  Instead of applying transformations such as shear,
  rotate, CutOut, etc. at the image level, the proposed
  technique divides the images into a fixed grid and
  applies a potentially different transformation to each
  cell. The problem of selecting a transform for each cell
  is cast as a multi-agent RL (MARL) task, and the agents
  learn as the main network trains within a (multi-agent)
  Advantage Actor Critic framework.

  The agents use a shared reward mechanism, with the reward
  defined as the difference between the loss on the
  augmented sample and the original loss.

  The regular grid is fixed for a dataset and
  hyperparameters like the magnitude of the augmentations
  follow a fixed schedule; the agents only pick _which_
  augmentation to apply on a patch.

  Experiments performed on CIFAR-{10,100}, ImageNet,
  CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, and FGVC-Aircraft show
  relatively small but very consistent improvements in
  terms of image classification accuracy. Different design
  choices (MARL vs. single-agent vs. random, grid size,
  etc.) are ablated and discussed in detail.
reviews.main_review:
## Strengths

  - S1: Ablation studies and theoretical explanations
    motivate the choice for multi-agent reinforcement
    learning reasonably well.
  - S2: The efficiency of the proposed method is
    investigated very clearly in Section 4.3. It seems
    considerably more efficient than many other
    learning-based DA methods.
  - S3: The patch-wise data augmentation is a powerful idea
    which can spark a large body of interesting follow-up
    research.


## Limitations

  - L1: While this is not the main focus of the paper, the
    empirical improvement over RL baselines like Fast
    AutoAugment and non-RL baselines like RandAugment is
    relatively small.
  - L2: (Minor) While multiple datasets are used for
    evaluation, only two Resnet variants are investigated
    for ImageNet. It would be nice to see an evaluation of
    the proposed data augmentation procedure on other NN
    architectures as well on ImageNet, but I also
    understand that the computational costs of this can be
    quite large, so it may not always be possible.
  - L3: (Minor) The agents rely on a pre-trained ResNet to
    produce features to be used as observations.


## Suggestions (Per-Section)

### Related Work

  - This is minor, but it may be worth briefly mentioning
    [hong2019patch]. While their work doesn't do patch
    level data augmentation, and instead "patches" objects
    from an existing bank onto training samples, it still
    has connections to things like CutMix that make it
    relevant.
  - Some recent papers on automatic augmentation are
    missing and would be worth discussing. This includes
    [DADA], [DDAS], [hendrycks2019augmix] and
    [mueller2021trivialAugment]. In particular, Mueller and
    Hutter's paper highlights an interesting limitation of
    their otherwise robust baseline, which is that it
    doesn't work too well for object detection, an area
    where the current method may have the upper hand!
  - Similarly, there are some further papers, such as […]
    that perform a kind of "local" (though not strictly
    patch-based) data augmentation by "pasting in" objects
    into the 3D point cloud.


### Method

  - "directly applying reinforcement learning (RL)
    algorithm to …" => "algorithms"
  - "In MARL configuration" => "In the MARL configuration"
  - Towards the end of Section 3.2, it may make more sense
    to replace the "Until all patches" to "Once all
    patches…" or maybe "After".
  - Q1: Can an agent output more than one augmentation for
    a patch? Would it be possible for a patch to, e.g., get
    rotated AND colorized?
  - Q2: Given the more "local" nature of this augmentation
    method, have you considered evaluating it on more
    "local" tasks, such as object detection and semantic
    segmentation. Perhaps it could make a bigger impact
    there.
  - Q3: Given that the parameters of actor networks of all
    agents are shared (see above Eq. (5)), does that mean
    that each actor's policy is identical, and the only
    difference between the actors is what observation each
    one gets (the local patch features)?


### Experiments

  - At the start of Section 4.1, "Exactly, we focus" =>
    "Specifically, we focus"
  - In "Policy Visualization", "important levels" ->
    "importance levels"
  - In Section 4.3 I would say the "main methods for
    reducing the computational cost", since what you next
    describe are methods for reducing the cost, not what
    one would typically call reasons.
  - Table 3 is a little hard to follow because of the
    different hardware employed by each column. After
    staring at it for a while it's becoming a bit clearer,
    but at first glance it was a bit confusing.
  - Q4: Do the times from Table 3 include the inference
    time required to compute the agent state and
    observations with the auxiliary ResNet-18?


### Conclusion

  - The parallels to vision transformers are very
    interesting and help give the reader a broader
    perspective on possible applications of the current
    paper.
  - (Minor) The word "Furthermore" is repeated for two
    almost consecutive sentences. Using something like
    "additionally" to start one of the sentences can
    improve the flow of the paragraph a little.


## References

  - [hong2019patch]: Hong, Sungeun, Sungil Kang, and
    Donghyeon Cho. "Patch-level
  augmentation for object detection in aerial images."
  Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on
  Computer Vision Workshops. 2019.
  - [cubuk2020randaugment]: Cubuk, Ekin D., et al.
    "Randaugment: Practical automated
  data augmentation with a reduced search space."
  Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision
  and Pattern Recognition Workshops. 2020.
  - [DDAS] Liu, Aoming, et al. "Direct Differentiable
    Augmentation Search." arXiv preprint arXiv:2104.04282
    (2021).
  - [DADA] Yonggang Li, Guosheng Hu, Yongtao Wang, Timothy

reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The paper proposes a new way of looking at the classic
  task of image augmentation for classification. Instead of
  learning an image-level augmentation policy using RL, the
  proposed approach uses multi-agent RL to learn
  patch-specific augmentations. While the benefit of
  multi-agent RL is relatively small, it seems significant
  and the general idea of learning to apply different
  augmentations to different parts of the image is
  interesting and, to the best of my knowledge, new. The
  idea to apply image augmentations at a local level is the
  core contribution of this paper, in my opinion.

  While it would be interesting to see how this technique
  works on tasks which, unlike image-level classification,
  are themselves more "local", such as semantic
  segmentation or detection, overall the proposed approach
  is evaluated thoroughly.

  The writing is clear and while the authors don't
  explicitly mention an intent to publish their source
  code, it should be possible to implement the proposed
  approach just based on the description from the paper. As
  such, I recommend accepting the paper to ICLR.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes an evolution of the traditional
  pipeline of image data augmentation used to reduce ML
  model overfitting.

  Instead of applying transformations such as shear,
  rotate, CutOut, etc. at the image level, the proposed
  technique divides the images into a fixed grid and
  applies a potentially different transformation to each
  cell. The problem of selecting a transform for each cell
  is cast as a multi-agent RL (MARL) task, and the agents
  learn as the main network trains within a (multi-agent)
  Advantage Actor Critic framework.

  The agents use a shared reward mechanism, with the reward
  defined as the difference between the loss on the
  augmented sample and the original loss.

  The regular grid is fixed for a dataset and
  hyperparameters like the magnitude of the augmentations
  follow a fixed schedule; the agents only pick _which_
  augmentation to apply on a patch.

  Experiments performed on CIFAR-{10,100}, ImageNet,
  CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, and FGVC-Aircraft show
  relatively small but very consistent improvements in
  terms of image classification accuracy. Different design
  choices (MARL vs. single-agent vs. random, grid size,
  etc.) are ablated and discussed in detail.

Main Review:
## Strengths

  - S1: Ablation studies and theoretical explanations
    motivate the choice for multi-agent reinforcement
    learning reasonably well.
  - S2: The efficiency of the proposed method is
    investigated very clearly in Section 4.3. It seems
    considerably more efficient than many other
    learning-based DA methods.
  - S3: The patch-wise data augmentation is a powerful idea
    which can spark a large body of interesting follow-up
    research.


## Limitations

  - L1: While this is not the main focus of the paper, the
    empirical improvement over RL baselines like Fast
    AutoAugment and non-RL baselines like RandAugment is
    relatively small.
  - L2: (Minor) While multiple datasets are used for
    evaluation, only two Resnet variants are investigated
    for ImageNet. It would be nice to see an evaluation of
    the proposed data augmentation procedure on other NN
    architectures as well on ImageNet, but I also
    understand that the computational costs of this can be
    quite large, so it may not always be possible.
  - L3: (Minor) The agents rely on a pre-trained ResNet to
    produce features to be used as observations.


## Suggestions (Per-Section)

### Related Work

  - This is minor, but it may be worth briefly mentioning
    [hong2019patch]. While their work doesn't do patch
    level data augmentation, and instead "patches" objects
    from an existing bank onto training samples, it still
    has connections to things like CutMix that make it
    relevant.
  - Some recent papers on automatic augmentation are
    missing and would be worth discussing. This includes
    [DADA], [DDAS], [hendrycks2019augmix] and
    [mueller2021trivialAugment]. In particular, Mueller and
    Hutter's paper highlights an interesting limitation of
    their otherwise robust baseline, which is that it
    doesn't work too well for object detection, an area
    where the current method may have the upper hand!
  - Similarly, there are some further papers, such as […]
    that perform a kind of "local" (though not strictly
    patch-based) data augmentation by "pasting in" objects
    into the 3D point cloud.


### Method

  - "directly applying reinforcement learning (RL)
    algorithm to …" => "algorithms"
  - "In MARL configuration" => "In the MARL configuration"
  - Towards the end of Section 3.2, it may make more sense
    to replace the "Until all patches" to "Once all
    patches…" or maybe "After".
  - Q1: Can an agent output more than one augmentation for
    a patch? Would it be possible for a patch to, e.g., get
    rotated AND colorized?
  - Q2: Given the more "local" nature of this augmentation
    method, have you considered evaluating it on more
    "local" tasks, such as object detection and semantic
    segmentation. Perhaps it could make a bigger impact
    there.
  - Q3: Given that the parameters of actor networks of all
    agents are shared (see above Eq. (5)), does that mean
    that each actor's policy is identical, and the only
    difference between the actors is what observation each
    one gets (the local patch features)?


### Experiments

  - At the start of Section 4.1, "Exactly, we focus" =>
    "Specifically, we focus"
  - In "Policy Visualization", "important levels" ->
    "importance levels"
  - In Section 4.3 I would say the "main methods for
    reducing the computational cost", since what you next
    describe are methods for reducing the cost, not what
    one would typically call reasons.
  - Table 3 is a little hard to follow because of the
    different hardware employed by each column. After
    staring at it for a while it's becoming a bit clearer,
    but at first glance it was a bit confusing.
  - Q4: Do the times from Table 3 include the inference
    time required to compute the agent state and
    observations with the auxiliary ResNet-18?


### Conclusion

  - The parallels to vision transformers are very
    interesting and help give the reader a broader
    perspective on possible applications of the current
    paper.
  - (Minor) The word "Furthermore" is repeated for two
    almost consecutive sentences. Using something like
    "additionally" to start one of the sentences can
    improve the flow of the paragraph a little.


## References

  - [hong2019patch]: Hong, Sungeun, Sungil Kang, and
    Donghyeon Cho. "Patch-level
  augmentation for object detection in aerial images."
  Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on
  Computer Vision Workshops. 2019.
  - [cubuk2020randaugment]: Cubuk, Ekin D., et al.
    "Randaugment: Practical automated
  data augmentation with a reduced search space."
  Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision
  and Pattern Recognition Workshops. 2020.
  - [DDAS] Liu, Aoming, et al. "Direct Differentiable
    Augmentation Search." arXiv preprint arXiv:2104.04282
    (2021).
  - [DADA] Yonggang Li, Guosheng Hu, Yongtao Wang, Timothy
    M. Hospedales, Neil Martin Robertson, and Yongxing
    Yang. DADA: differentiable automatic data augmentation.
    CoRR, abs/2003.03780, 2020.
  - [mueller2021trivialAugment]: Müller, Samuel G., and
    Frank Hutter. "TrivialAugment: Tuning-free Yet
    State-of-the-Art Data Augmentation." ICCV 2021.
  - [hendrycks2019augmix]: Hendrycks, Dan, et al. "Augmix:
    A simple data processing method to improve robustness
    and uncertainty." arXiv preprint arXiv:1912.02781
    (2019).

Summary Of The Review:
The paper proposes a new way of looking at the classic
  task of image augmentation for classification. Instead of
  learning an image-level augmentation policy using RL, the
  proposed approach uses multi-agent RL to learn
  patch-specific augmentations. While the benefit of
  multi-agent RL is relatively small, it seems significant
  and the general idea of learning to apply different
  augmentations to different parts of the image is
  interesting and, to the best of my knowledge, new. The
  idea to apply image augmentations at a local level is the
  core contribution of this paper, in my opinion.

  While it would be interesting to see how this technique
  works on tasks which, unlike image-level classification,
  are themselves more "local", such as semantic
  segmentation or detection, overall the proposed approach
  is evaluated thoroughly.

  The writing is clear and while the authors don't
  explicitly mention an intent to publish their source
  code, it should be possible to implement the proposed
  approach just based on the description from the paper. As
  such, I recommend accepting the paper to ICLR.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: Local Patch AutoAugment with Multi-Agent Collaboration

## Summary Of The Paper

The paper proposes an evolution of the traditional pipeline of image data augmentation used to reduce ML model overfitting. Instead of applying transformations such as shear, rotate, CutOut, etc. at the image level, the proposed technique divides the images into a fixed grid and applies a potentially different transformation to each cell. The problem of selecting a transform for each cell is cast as a multi-agent RL (MARL) task, and the agents learn as the main network trains within a multi-agent Advantage Actor Critic framework. This represents a meaningful departure from existing automated augmentation methods that operate exclusively at the image level, as exemplified in Figure 1 of the manuscript.

The agents use a shared reward mechanism, with the reward defined as the difference between the loss on the augmented sample and the original loss, as formulated in Equation (1). The regular grid is fixed for a dataset and hyperparameters like the magnitude of the augmentations follow a fixed schedule; the agents only pick which augmentation to apply on a patch. This design choice significantly reduces the action space compared to searching over both operation type and magnitude parameters simultaneously.

Experiments performed on CIFAR-{10,100}, ImageNet, CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, and FGVC-Aircraft show relatively small but very consistent improvements in terms of image classification accuracy. Different design choices (MARL vs. single-agent vs. random, grid size, etc.) are ablated and discussed in detail in Section 4.4 and Table 6-7. The authors also demonstrate the applicability of their method to object detection tasks using Pascal VOC 2007, which strengthens the claim about the method's versatility.

## Main Review

### Strengths

**S1: Ablation studies and theoretical explanations motivate the choice for multi-agent reinforcement learning reasonably well.**

The paper provides comprehensive ablation studies that effectively justify the use of MARL over simpler alternatives. Table 6 presents three variants: Patch RandAugment (PRA), which applies random augmentations to patches without learning; Patch SingleAutoAugment (PSAA), which applies single-agent RL independently to each patch; and the proposed PAA with full MARL. The results clearly demonstrate that PSAA underperforms PAA across all datasets (e.g., 97.3% vs 97.5% on CIFAR-10, 83.1% vs 83.4% on CIFAR-100, and 78.0/93.9 vs 78.3/94.1 on ImageNet), providing empirical evidence that considering contextual relationships between patches through cooperative multi-agent learning yields tangible benefits.

Furthermore, the authors provide intuitive explanations for why MARL is superior to single-agent approaches. They argue that single-agent methods ignore contextual relationships between regions, which may lead to non-globally optimal effectiveness of DA policies across the entire image. The MARL formulation, with its centralized training and decentralized execution framework, allows agents to share parameters (as noted in the text following Equation 5) while still receiving different observations based on their respective patches. This design elegantly balances the need for global coordination with the flexibility of local decision-making.

The theoretical grounding is further strengthened by the connection to the MAMDP formulation presented in Section 3.1, which properly extends the standard MDP framework to account for multiple agents with individual observations but access to shared global state information. The team reward mechanism (Equation 1) ensures that all agents are incentivized to optimize the same objective, which is the improvement of the target network's performance on augmented data.

**S2: The efficiency of the proposed method is investigated very clearly in Section 4.3. It seems considerably more efficient than many other learning-based DA methods.**

The computational efficiency analysis presented in Table 3 is thorough and demonstrates a significant practical advantage of PAA. Comparing GPU hours across methods, PAA requires only 7.5 hours for CIFAR-10 training (including negligible search time), compared to 5006 hours for AutoAugment, 9.5 hours for FastAA, and 15160 hours for AdvAA on ImageNet. The total computational cost of PAA (610 GPU hours on ImageNet) is substantially lower than AdvAA (15160 GPU hours), representing approximately a 25x reduction in computational requirements.

The authors identify three specific mechanisms for achieving this efficiency improvement. First, by jointly optimizing the augmentation policy network with the target network in an online manner (similar to AdvAA and other recent methods), they eliminate the expensive offline search phase that requires evaluating thousands of policies on a proxy network. Second, by fixing the probability and magnitude schedules and having agents only select the operation type, they dramatically reduce the search space from what would be a continuous optimization problem to a discrete action selection problem. Third, the use of MARL with parameter sharing allows all agents to learn in parallel, and the parallel tensor-based transformations on GPU (utilizing the Kornia library as mentioned in Section 4.3) further accelerate the augmentation process during training.

It is particularly noteworthy that despite the added complexity of maintaining a feature extractor (ResNet-18) to compute state and observations, the total parameter overhead of PAA is less than 1% of the target network (0.23M parameters for PAA vs. 25.5M for ResNet-50), making it a practical addition to existing training pipelines. The authors also note that training time includes the inference time required to compute state and observations, providing transparency about what is included in the reported figures.

**S3: The patch-wise data augmentation is a powerful idea which can spark a large body of interesting follow-up research.**

The core contribution of moving from image-level to patch-level augmentation represents a conceptual advance that opens new research directions. The intuition is compelling: different regions of an image may benefit from different augmentations based on their content and semantic importance. As the authors motivate in the introduction, applying a single augmentation policy to an entire image may inadvertently damage critical semantic information in important regions while missing opportunities to augment less critical regions more aggressively.

The visualization results in Section 4.5 provide valuable insights that support this intuition. Figure 4 demonstrates that patches of different importance levels indeed prefer different augmentation operations throughout training. For instance, important patches (those identified by Grad-CAM as containing task-relevant features) predominantly select color transformation operations, which are relatively mild and preserve semantic information. In contrast, unimportant patches tend to favor more aggressive transformations like RandomErasing and Cutout. This finding aligns with the hypothesis that the method learns to protect semantically important regions while allowing more aggressive augmentation in less critical areas.

The Grad-CAM visualizations in Figure 3 and Figure 5 further demonstrate that models trained with PAA develop better localization abilities and focus on more task-relevant image regions compared to baseline and AutoAugment-trained models. This suggests that patch-level augmentation not only improves accuracy but also leads to more interpretable and robust learned representations.

### Limitations

**L1: While this is not the main focus of the paper, the empirical improvement over RL baselines like Fast AutoAugment and non-RL baselines like RandAugment is relatively small.**

Examining the results across Tables 1, 2, and 4, the improvements are indeed modest. On CIFAR-10 with WRN-28-10, PAA achieves 97.5±0.1% compared to FastAA's 97.3% and RandAugment's 97.3%, a difference of only 0.2 percentage points. On CIFAR-100 with the same model, the improvement is 83.4±0.3% (PAA) vs. 83.3% (RandAugment), which is within the standard deviation. Even on ImageNet, where PAA shows more substantial gains (78.3±0.3 / 94.1±0.2 for ResNet-50 vs. 77.6/93.7 for FastAA and 77.5/93.5 for RandAugment), the absolute improvement is approximately 0.7 percentage points for top-1 accuracy.

While these improvements are consistent across multiple datasets and architectures, one might question whether the added complexity of the MARL framework and the requirement for a feature extractor network is justified by such modest gains. The standard deviations reported in the tables (e.g., ±0.1 to ±0.3) further suggest that the improvements, while consistent, are relatively small in magnitude. This raises questions about the practical significance of the method, particularly for practitioners who may prioritize simplicity and ease of implementation over marginal accuracy gains.

It would be valuable to understand whether these small improvements translate to meaningful benefits in downstream applications or whether they are primarily of academic interest. Additionally, the paper would benefit from statistical significance testing to determine whether the reported improvements are statistically significant given the reported standard de

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes an automatic data augmentation approach. Different from existing works, they proposed to augment patches in the image rather than the whole image. The approach is formulated as a multi-agent reinforcement learning problem. They empirically show the effectiveness of their approach across several image classification datasets.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths:
- 1.	The paper conducted a comprehensive evaluation of their approach over several datasets. They achieve gains over the compared baseline method validating the efficacy of their proposed approach.
- 2.	The approach considers patched based augmentation, which is sufficiently different/novel from existing work.

### Weaknesses:
- 3.	The motivation of the approach is somewhat lacking. For example, it is unclear why MARL on patches is much faster. I think the paper could benefit from motivating more on why MARL and what is its benefit with respect to the task. 
- 4.	The paper did not reference or compared to a key related work, “Differentiable automatic data augmentation” [A]. Based on its relevance, the paper should discuss and contrast with this work, as they also have minimal search time. Specifically, comparison in Table 2 and Table 3 would strengthen the paper.
- 5.	How is the model architecture of the actor/critic network determined? Also, what ranges of hyperparameters for MADDPG are considered? I saw the hyperparameters for the target model but not MADDPG. RL is known to be sensitive to hyperparameters, I wonder if the authors will release the code and pre-trained models for reproducibility. 
- 6.	Ablation with independent agents as a baseline would be interesting. It would further motivate the use of MADDPG and the multi-agent setting.
 
### References
[A] Li, Yonggang, et al. "Differentiable automatic data augmentation.", Proc. ECCV, 2020.

reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

This paper proposes an automatic data augmentation approach. Different from existing works, they proposed to augment patches in the image rather than the whole image. The approach is formulated as a multi-agent reinforcement learning problem. They empirically show the effectiveness of their approach across several image classification datasets.

Main Review:
### Strengths:
- 1.	The paper conducted a comprehensive evaluation of their approach over several datasets. They achieve gains over the compared baseline method validating the efficacy of their proposed approach.
- 2.	The approach considers patched based augmentation, which is sufficiently different/novel from existing work.

### Weaknesses:
- 3.	The motivation of the approach is somewhat lacking. For example, it is unclear why MARL on patches is much faster. I think the paper could benefit from motivating more on why MARL and what is its benefit with respect to the task. 
- 4.	The paper did not reference or compared to a key related work, “Differentiable automatic data augmentation” [A]. 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: Local Patch AutoAugment with Multi-Agent Collaboration

## Summary of the Paper

This paper proposes Patch AutoAugment (PAA), an automated data augmentation approach that operates at the patch level rather than the image level. The core innovation lies in formulating the problem of searching for optimal augmentation policies across image patches as a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) problem. Each patch is assigned an agent that learns an augmentation policy based on both the patch's local content and the global semantic context of the entire image. The agents cooperate through a shared team reward signal to achieve jointly optimal augmentation effects. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of their approach across multiple benchmark datasets including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet, and several fine-grained recognition datasets, as well as object detection tasks.

## Main Review

### Strengths

1. **Comprehensive Experimental Evaluation**: The paper presents a thorough empirical evaluation across diverse tasks and datasets. The experiments span general image classification (CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet), fine-grained recognition (CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, FGVC-Aircraft), and object detection (Pascal VOC 2007). This breadth of evaluation is commendable and demonstrates that the proposed approach generalizes across different problem settings. The consistent improvements over baseline methods across these diverse domains provide strong empirical validation of the approach's efficacy.

2. **Novel Patch-Based Augmentation Formulation**: The shift from image-level to patch-level augmentation is a sufficiently novel contribution that distinguishes this work from existing automated augmentation methods. The intuition that different regions of an image may benefit from different augmentation strategies is reasonable and well-motivated by the observation that coarse-grained augmentation may damage critical semantic information (as illustrated in Figure 1). This represents a meaningful departure from prior work in the automated augmentation literature.

3. **Insightful Visualization and Analysis**: The paper provides valuable insights through visualization of learned policies. The policy visualization in Figure 4, which categorizes patches by importance and tracks operation selection over training epochs, reveals interesting patterns—for instance, that important patches tend to prefer mild transformations like color adjustments while unimportant patches favor more aggressive operations like RandomErasing and Cutout. This analysis provides useful insights to the data augmentation community about how different regions should be treated differently. The Grad-CAM visualizations in Figures 3 and 5 further demonstrate that PAA helps the target network focus on more task-relevant features compared to baseline and AutoAugment approaches.

4. **Computational Efficiency**: The paper demonstrates that PAA achieves competitive or superior performance while requiring significantly fewer computational resources compared to prior automated augmentation methods. Table 3 shows that PAA requires only 7.5 GPU hours on CIFAR-10 (including both search and training) compared to 5006 hours for AutoAugment and 9.5 hours for FastAA. On ImageNet, PAA requires 610 GPU hours total compared to 15160 hours for AutoAugment. The approach achieves this efficiency through online policy learning (co-training with the target network), fixed schedules for hyperparameters, and parallel tensor-based transformations.

### Weaknesses

3. **Insufficient Motivation for MARL Formulation**: While the paper proposes using MARL for the patch augmentation problem, the motivation for this specific choice could be substantially stronger. The paper argues that MARL is necessary to capture contextual relationships between patches, but the justification for why MARL specifically is the right tool for this task is somewhat underdeveloped. The paper states that "it is of great necessity to take the patch content and the contextual relationship between patches into account" (Section 3), but does not clearly explain why independent single-agent RL would be fundamentally inadequate or how the multi-agent formulation directly addresses this need beyond sharing a team reward. Furthermore, the claim that MARL is "much faster" due to parallel learning of agents lacks clear justification—parallel policy learning does not necessarily imply faster convergence compared to sequential single-agent approaches. The paper would benefit from a more rigorous analysis of the computational and statistical advantages of the MARL formulation. Additionally, the specific mechanism by which agents "cooperate" through the shared team reward could be explained more clearly, as the current explanation feels somewhat circular: agents cooperate because they share a reward signal, but the paper doesn't deeply explain what cooperation patterns emerge or why they are beneficial beyond the intuitive notion that all agents want to maximize the same objective.

4. **Missing Critical Related Work and Incomplete Comparisons**: The paper fails to cite or compare against DADA (Differentiable Automatic Data Augmentation) [Li et al., 2020], which is listed in Table 2 but not properly discussed in the related work section. More critically, the paper does not include a reference to or comparison with DDAS (Direct Differentiable Augmentation Search) [Liu et al., 2021a], which is mentioned only briefly in Section 2.1. These omissions are problematic given that both DADA and DDAS achieve minimal search time through differentiable relaxations of the discrete policy search problem, making them highly relevant baselines. The comparison in Table 2 shows DADA results on ImageNet, but there is no discussion of how DADA's approach differs from PAA or why MARL is superior to the differentiable optimization approach used in DADA. The paper would be substantially strengthened by: (1) explicitly discussing DADA and DDAS in the related work section, (2) providing a detailed comparison of the computational complexity and search efficiency of these methods versus PAA, and (3) analyzing the trade-offs between differentiable relaxation approaches and the MARL formulation. The fact that Table 3 omits DADA and DDAS from the computational cost comparison is a notable gap that weakens the paper's claims about computational efficiency.

5. **Insufficient Details on Architecture and Hyperparameter Selection**: The paper provides limited justification for key architectural and hyperparameter choices. While Appendix C provides the policy network architecture (Table 9), there is no explanation of how these specific architectural choices were determined or validated. Why is a simple 3-layer convolutional network sufficient for the actor network? Were alternative architectures explored? Similarly, the paper mentions using the Multi-agent Advantage Actor-Critic (MADDPG) algorithm but provides only limited hyperparameter details. Appendix D supplies hyperparameters for the target models but notably lacks detailed hyperparameters for the MARL algorithm itself (learning rates, entropy coefficients, discount factors, etc. for the policy networks). Since reinforcement learning is known to be highly sensitive to hyperparameter choices, the absence of these details raises reproducibility concerns. The paper states that "we use the SGD optimizer with an initial learning rate of 1e-4 to train the actor network and the critic network" but provides no information about other critical RL hyperparameters such as: the discount factor γ used in the advantage estimation, entropy regularization coefficients, the specific advantage function formulation beyond Equation 3, or the update frequency and batch size for policy network training. The authors should either provide these details in the main paper or appendix, or commit to releasing code and pre-trained models to enable reproducibility.

6. **Lack of Ablation Study Comparing Independent Agents**: While Table 6 provides a useful ablation comparing Patch RandAugment (PRA) and Patch Single AutoAugment (PSAA) against PAA, the ablation study would be significantly strengthened by explicitly comparing PAA against a baseline using independent single-agent RL agents. The current PSAA comparison mentions that it "ignores the contextual relationship between patches, where the agents are independent and patches are non-cooperative," but PSAA is not clearly described as using true single-agent RL in a multi-agent setting—it could be interpreted as simply running independent RL searches sequentially. A clearer ablation would involve: (1) training N independent single-agent RL agents, each with its own policy network and critic network, (2) having each agent receive only its local patch features as observation (without access to global state), and (3) having each agent receive individual rewards based solely on the impact of its patch's augmentation. This would provide a more direct comparison that isolates the benefit of the multi-agent formulation with shared parameters, centralized training, and team rewards. The results in Table 6 show only marginal improvements of PAA (97.5%) over PSAA (97.3%) on CIFAR-10, which raises questions about whether the complexity of the MARL formulation is justified. A more thorough ablation comparing different cooperation mechanisms (e.g., parameter sharing alone, team rewards alone, vs. the full MARL approach) would help clarify which components are essential.

7. **Limited Analysis of the Team Reward Design**: The reward function in Equation 1 is relatively simple—the difference between training loss on augmented and original data. While the paper mentions that this design is "inspired by AdvAA,"
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes an automatic data augmentation approach. Different from existing works, they proposed to augment patches in the image rather than the whole image. The approach is formulated as a multi-agent reinforcement learning problem. They empirically show the effectiveness of their approach across several image classification datasets.

Main Review:
### Strengths:
- 1.	The paper conducted a comprehensive evaluation of their approach over several datasets. They achieve gains over the compared baseline method validating the efficacy of their proposed approach.
- 2.	The approach considers patched based augmentation, which is sufficiently different/novel from existing work.

### Weaknesses:
- 3.	The motivation of the approach is somewhat lacking. For example, it is unclear why MARL on patches is much faster. I think the paper could benefit from motivating more on why MARL and what is its benefit with respect to the task. 
- 4.	The paper did not reference or compared to a key related work, “Differentiable automatic data augmentation” [A]. Based on its relevance, the paper should discuss and contrast with this work, as they also have minimal search time. Specifically, comparison in Table 2 and Table 3 would strengthen the paper.
- 5.	How is the model architecture of the actor/critic network determined? Also, what ranges of hyperparameters for MADDPG are considered? I saw the hyperparameters for the target model but not MADDPG. RL is known to be sensitive to hyperparameters, I wonder if the authors will release the code and pre-trained models for reproducibility. 
- 6.	Ablation with independent agents as a baseline would be interesting. It would further motivate the use of MADDPG and the multi-agent setting.
 
### References
[A] Li, Yonggang, et al. "Differentiable automatic data augmentation.", Proc. ECCV, 2020.

Summary Of The Review:
The main concern with the paper is with the motivation and missing reference/comparison with a key existing work. 
Currently, I recommend a weak reject.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper proposed a fine-grained automated data augmentation approach, Patch AutoAugment (PAA), which tries to increase diversity in local regions by divide an image into a grid of patches and search for the joint optimal augmentation policies for the patches. The proposed PAA considers the task as a multi-agent reinforcement learning problem,  and adopt a multi-agent reinforcement learning
algorithm to automatically search for the optimal augmentation policies by considering the contextual relationship between the patches. They verify the proposed method on many classification and fine-grained recognition dataset(CIFAR10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet, CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars and FGVC-Aircraft). The experiments show a good result and visualization results provide some insights that the PAA  help the target network to localize more class-related cues.
reviews.main_review:
Over all, this paper is well written and technically sound, the experimental evaluation is comprehensive and the results are compelling, but I have several concerns:
1. The most important contribution of this paper in my mind is the new search space focus on image patches for data augmentation. It seems like an incremental job relative to mixup series or cutout series.
2. The proposed PPA formulate the task as a multi-agent reinforcement learning problem, and the results show it can find better policy than random, but [1] demonstrate that only use reinforcement learning can not get better policy than random baseline. The two experimental results are contrary.
3.The calimed SOTA results is inappropriate, methods with better experimental results were not compared. For example, the paper  compare with AdvAA on the  computational cost, but not coapre on the accuracy.
4.Another question is that recent work [2] with careful designed augmentation policy and advanced training strategy can get much better results, It is better to compare with it to give more insights.

[2] Wightman R, Touvron H, Jégou H. ResNet strikes back: An improved training procedure in timm

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: Local Patch AutoAugment with Multi-Agent Collaboration

## Summary Of The Paper

This paper proposes Patch AutoAugment (PAA), a fine-grained automated data augmentation approach that addresses limitations of existing image-level augmentation methods. The key innovation is dividing images into a grid of patches and searching for joint optimal augmentation policies for each patch rather than applying a single policy to the entire image. The authors formulate this as a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) problem where each patch is controlled by an agent that learns policies based on local patch content and global image semantics. The proposed method employs a centralized training with decentralized execution framework, allowing agents to cooperate through a shared team reward signal. The approach is evaluated comprehensively across multiple domains including image classification (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet), fine-grained recognition (CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, FGVC-Aircraft), and object detection (Pascal VOC 2007). The experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art data augmentation methods with reduced computational overhead compared to existing automated augmentation approaches.

## Main Review

### Overall Assessment

Overall, this paper is well-written and technically sound with a comprehensive experimental evaluation and compelling results across diverse datasets and tasks. The motivation for patch-level augmentation is intuitive and the visualization results provide valuable insights into how different regions benefit from different augmentation strategies. However, there are several substantive concerns that limit the contribution and require careful consideration.

### 1. Incremental Nature of the Core Contribution

The most important concern regarding this paper is that the primary contribution—searching for augmentation policies at the patch level rather than the image level—appears to be an incremental advance relative to existing work in region-based data augmentation. While the paper correctly identifies that previous automated augmentation methods like AutoAugment, RandAugment, and others operate at the image level, the concept of applying different augmentations to different regions is not entirely novel. 

The paper acknowledges in Section 2.1 that region-based augmentation methods already exist, including RandomErasing (Zhong et al., 2020), CutMix (Yun et al., 2019), Hard Chips (Hong et al., 2019), and saliency-guided approaches like SaliencyMix (Uddin et al., 2020) and SnapMix (Huang et al., 2020). The authors claim their work is "conceptually orthogonal" to these methods because they automatically search for augmentation policies rather than using random or fixed transformations. However, this distinction feels incremental—essentially combining existing patch-based augmentation ideas with automated policy search. The jump from image-wise AutoAugment to patch-wise AutoAugment, while useful, does not represent a fundamental conceptual advance in the field. The paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of what constitutes a truly novel contribution beyond applying existing techniques at a finer granularity.

### 2. Questionable Claims About MARL Necessity and Effectiveness

A critical concern is the justification and demonstrated necessity of using multi-agent reinforcement learning. The paper claims that MARL is essential for achieving "joint optimal DA policy" by considering contextual relationships between patches. However, the ablation study in Table 6 raises questions about whether this complexity is truly justified.

The comparison between Patch SingleAutoAugment (PSAA) and PAA shows relatively modest improvements: on CIFAR-10, the improvement is only 0.2% (97.3% to 97.5%), on CIFAR-100 it is 0.3% (83.1% to 83.4%), and on ImageNet it is 0.3% (78.0% to 78.3%). These improvements are within or very close to the reported standard deviations for some datasets. This raises a fundamental question: does the additional complexity of MARL truly provide substantial benefits over a simpler single-agent approach?

More importantly, the paper's claim that MARL provides better policies than random baseline contradicts findings from recent work. RandAugment (Cubuk et al., 2020) [1] demonstrates that simple random augmentation policies can be surprisingly effective and sometimes match or exceed the performance of learned policies. The paper shows in Table 6 that Patch RandAugment (PRA) achieves 97.1% on CIFAR-10, while PAA achieves 97.5%—only a 0.4% improvement. This modest gain raises the question of whether the learned policies are genuinely discovering superior augmentation strategies or simply marginal refinements over randomization. The paper does not adequately address this contradiction or provide sufficient justification for why MARL-learned policies should substantially outperform random policies when the empirical evidence suggests otherwise.

### 3. Incomplete and Potentially Misleading Comparisons

The paper's claims of achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results are not fully substantiated due to incomplete experimental comparisons. While the paper compares computational costs with AdvAA in Table 3, it does not provide accuracy comparisons with AdvAA on the same benchmarks. This is a significant omission, as AdvAA is mentioned as a closely related method that also uses adversarial learning to guide augmentation policy search.

More problematically, the paper does not compare against some of the most relevant recent baselines. The claim of SOTA results would be more convincing if the method were evaluated against recent strong baselines that employ carefully designed augmentation policies combined with advanced training strategies. For instance, the paper does not compare against methods that combine improved training procedures with augmentation strategies, which could provide important context for interpreting the reported improvements. Without such comparisons, it is difficult to assess whether the improvements come from the patch-level formulation, the MARL approach, or simply from the joint online training strategy (which is shared with AdvAA).

### 4. Missing Comparisons with Recent Advanced Training Methods

A related concern is that the paper does not adequately contextualize its results against very recent advances in training procedures and augmentation strategies. For example, recent work [2] (ResNet Strikes Back: An Improved Training Procedure in timm) demonstrates that careful attention to training hyperparameters, learning rate schedules, and other training details can yield substantial improvements in model performance. The paper's improvements over baselines could potentially be partially attributable to the joint training strategy rather than the patch-level policy search itself.

The paper would be significantly strengthened by: (1) comparing PAA against AdvAA on accuracy metrics across all datasets, (2) evaluating against recent strong baselines that employ advanced training procedures, and (3) more carefully isolating which components of the method (patch-level search vs. MARL vs. online training) contribute to the observed improvements. Without these comparisons, the significance of the contribution remains unclear.

### 5. Reward Function Design and Theoretical Justification

While the reward function in Equation (1) is intuitive, the paper lacks theoretical justification for why the difference in training losses between augmented and original data should guide effective augmentation policy learning. The reward is formulated as r = l(φ(x̃), ỹ) - l(φ(x), y), which encourages policies that increase training loss. This is inspired by AdvAA but the paper does not thoroughly discuss potential issues with this approach.

One concern is that maximizing training loss (or minimizing the negative loss difference) could potentially lead to overly aggressive augmentation that damages semantic information, particularly for important patches. While the visualization results (Figure 4) suggest the method learns to apply mild transformations to important patches and aggressive ones to unimportant patches, the mechanism by which the shared team reward naturally encourages this behavior is not clearly explained. A more detailed analysis of how the reward function properties lead to the observed patch-specific policy selection would strengthen the paper.

### 6. Limited Analysis of Failure Cases and Method Limitations

The paper does not discuss scenarios where patch-level augmentation might be suboptimal or where the method might fail. For instance, what happens with images containing objects that span multiple patches? How does the method handle cases where semantic information is distributed across patch boundaries? The ablation study on patch numbers (Table 7) shows performance degradation at extreme values (N=1 and N=1024), but the paper provides only a brief intuitive explanation without deeper analysis.

Additionally, the paper claims that PAA is "naturally aligned" with vision transformers due to their patch-based architecture, but this is mentioned only in the conclusion without any experimental validation or detailed discussion. Given the increasing importance of vision transformers, this claim deserves more thorough investigation.

### 7. Computational Cost Claims Require Careful Interpretation

While Table 3 demonstrates that PAA has lower total computational cost than AutoAugment and comparable cost to AdvAA, the comparison is somewhat misleading. The paper notes that the computational cost comparison uses different hardware (GTX 1080 Ti for PAA vs. Tesla P100 for AdvAA and V100 for FastAA, as mentioned in

title: Stabilized Likelihood-based Imitation Learning via Denoising Continuous Normalizing Flow

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper proposes a straightforward non-adversarially-trained imitation learning method. The goal is to avoid mode collapse and training instability that can occur in some adversarially-trained imitaiton learning methods, while still addressing the distribution shift problem that basic Behavior Cloning is subject to. 

The paper:
- Motivates a two-stage learning procedure by starting with a likelihood-based state-action distribution matching objective that would require bi-level optimization
- Presents a lower-bound of the objective in which optimization can occur serially of two components: MLE of the expert's vistation distribution, and then policy optimization over two terms, one of which is the LL of visited states under the the modelled visitation distribution, and the other is the behavior cloning loss.
- Presents a proof that it is a lower bound. 
- Proposes a concrete approach to performing this optimization problem by using a denoising conditional normalizing flow for modeling the expert's visitation distribution, where the CNF seems to be motivated because of its ability to model densities of complex distributions, and the denoising is motivated to enable stable training in situations in which the data lie in a lower-dimensional manifold. 
- Performs experiments on 10 mujoco environments that illustrate that the proposed approach achieves better mode coverage than some comparable methods, that the denoising is empirically effective for generalization, that the algorithm is more robust than GAIL to variation in learning rate, among other hyperparmaeter variations.
reviews.main_review:
Main strength
--
This paper's main strength is that it proposes a straightforward method that both conceptually and empirically satisfies important desiderata of imitation learning methods (1) address the cascading error problem of BC-based methods (2) address the instability (mode collapse) of adversarially-trained IL methods (3) avoid dependence on an interactive demonstrator (i.e. DAGGER).

Main weakness
--
The paper's main weakness is some imprecision in a few places

### Imprecision
There are some issues (a,b,c) with the proof. (a) The proof refers to component $\enclose{circle}{2}$ in Eq 1, yet Eq. 1 has no denoted components. Is this reference correct? If so, then Eq 1. should denote the second component with an underbrace. If not, should it instead be Eq. 2 or Eq. 3? (b) The proof states "with sufficient expert data $\mathcal D_E$, $\enclose{circle}{2}$ can be expressed with $\sum_{s \in \mathcal D_\pi} \frac{P_E(s)}{P_\pi(s)} \log P_\pi(s)$. I don't see how this is true, because $\enclose{circle}{2}$ in Eq (3) is $\sum_{s \in \mathcal D_\pi} \log P_E(s)$, i.e. the state distribution is the state distribution of the policy. Again, is the reference to $\enclose{circle}{2}$ correct? These ambiguities / errors prevent me from being able to completely follow the proof. (c) It's not obvious from the definition of $g(\pi, P_\pi)$ that $\sum_{s \in \mathcal D_{\pi^*}} \log P_{\pi^*}(s) \geq \max_\pi \sum_{s \in \mathcal D_\pi} \log P_E(s)$ for two reasons (c.i) because the sums are not normalized by the the dataset size, it would be easy to invalidate the inequality by changing the dataset sizes (c.ii) What if $\pi$ was such that $\mathcal D_\pi$ only contains $s$ at the mode of $P_{\pi^*}(s)$? Wouldn't the RHS be at least as large as the LHS, in that case?

S4.1: $P_E$ is undefined (it's mentioned that it's the expert's state distribution, but a formal definition is needed). Is it more correctly denoted $P_{\pi_E}$, to "typecheck" with the subscripting of $P$ with a policy (i.e. $P_\pi$) to denote the state visitation distribution induced by policy $\pi$?

### Other issues
- The motivation of the gating parameter ($\lambda$) seems to come from dataset size imbalance (between $\mathcal D_E$ and $\mathcal D_{\pi_{\theta_i}}$). Why can't this dataset balance be addressed by simply normalizing the losses by the size of the datasets? Can the authors comment? If this is insufficient, the paper needs to discuss why.
- Missing related work: like the submitted paper, [A] also performs likelihood-based imitation learning with a normalizing flow for expert distribution matching, is not adversarially trained, and uses perturbation of the expert trajectories with gaussian noise. Discussion of the differences is important to contextualize the current paper's contributions, e.g. [A] used the distribution for planning rather than to learn a final policy
- The description of the experiment corresponding to Figs 11, 12, and 13 is lacking. It's not clear what environment(s) the experiment are run on.
- The introduction needs more justification for why DCNF is used in favor of other likelihood-based deep generative modeling approaches. Is continuity necessary? Is denoising necessary? Section 3 talks about the latter (C#2), perhaps incorporate some of this motivation into the introduction.

[A] Rhinehart N, McAllister R, Levine S. Deep imitative models for flexible inference, planning, and control. arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.06544. 2018 Oct 15.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Despite some important issues with method presentation and justification, I think the paper presents a solid method and solid evidence of its utility. The paper can definitely be improved, but as it stands now, I think it's a good contribution.

There are some issues (a,b,c) with the proof. (a) The proof refers to component $\enclose{circle}{2}$ in Eq 1, yet Eq. 1 has no denoted components. Is this reference correct? If so, then Eq 1. should denote the second component with an underbrace. If not, should it instead be Eq. 2 or Eq. 3? (b) The proof states "with sufficient expert data $\mathcal D_E$, $\enclose{circle}{2}$ can be expressed with $\sum_{s \in \mathcal D_\pi} \frac{P_E(s)}{P_\pi(s)} \log P_\pi(s)$. I don't see how this is true, because $\enclose{circle}{2}$ in Eq (3) is $\sum_{s \in \mathcal D_\pi} \log P_E(s)$, i.e. the state distribution is the state distribution of the policy. Again, is the reference to $\enclose{circle}{2}$ correct? These ambiguities / errors prevent me from being able to completely follow the proof. (c) It's not obvious from the definition of $g(\pi, P_\pi)$ that $\sum_{s \in \mathcal D_{\pi^*}} \log P_{\pi^*}(s) \geq \max_\pi \sum_{s \in \mathcal D_\pi} \log P_E(s)$ for two reasons (c.i) because the sums are not normalized by the the dataset size, it would be easy to invalidate the inequality by changing the dataset sizes (c.ii) What if $\pi$ was such that $\mathcal D_\pi$ only contains $s$ at the mode of $P_{\pi^*}(s)$? Wouldn't the RHS be at least as large as the LHS, in that case?

S4.1: $P_E$ is undefined (it's mentioned that it's the expert's state distribution, but a formal definition is needed). Is it more correctly denoted $P_{\pi_E}$, to "typecheck" with the subscripting of $P$ with a policy (i.e. $P_\pi$) to denote the state visitation distribution induced by policy $\pi$?

### Other issues
- The motivation of the gating parameter ($\lambda$) seems to come from dataset size imbalance (between $\mathcal D_E$ and $\mathcal D_{\pi_{\theta_i}}$). Why can't this dataset balance be addressed by simply normalizing the losses by the size of the datasets? Can the authors comment? If this is insufficient, the paper needs to discuss why.
- Missing related work: like the submitted paper, [A] also performs likelihood-based imitation learning with a normalizing flow for expert distribution matching, is not adversarially trained, and uses perturbation of the expert trajectories with gaussian noise. Discussion of the differences is important to contextualize the current paper's contributions, e.g. [A] used the distribution for planning rather than to learn a final policy
- The description of the experiment corresponding to Figs 11, 12, and 13 is lacking. It's not clear what environment(s) the experiment are run on.
- The introduction needs more justification for why DCNF is used in favor of other likelihood-based deep generative modeling approaches. Is continuity necessary? Is denoising necessary? Section 3 talks about the latter (C#2), perhaps incorporate some of this motivation into the introduction.

[A] Rhinehart N, McAllister R, Levine S. Deep imitative models for flexible inference, planning, and control. arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.06544. 2018 Oct 15.

Summary Of The Review:
Despite some important issues with method presentation and justification, I think the paper presents a solid method and solid evidence of its utility. The paper can definitely be improved, but as it stands now, I think it's a good contribution.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

# Expanded Peer Review: STABILIZED LIKELIHOOD-BASED IMITATION LEARNING VIA DENOISING CONTINUOUS NORMALIZING FLOW

## Summary Of The Paper

This paper proposes SLIL (Stabilized Likelihood-based Imitation Learning), a non-adversarially-trained imitation learning method designed to avoid mode collapse and training instability that can occur in adversarially-trained imitation learning methods such as GAIL, while still addressing the distribution shift problem that basic Behavior Cloning is subject to. The core motivation is compelling: existing state-of-the-art IL approaches either suffer from covariate shift (BC) or training instability and mode collapse (GAIL), and the paper aims to address both limitations simultaneously.

The paper makes several interconnected contributions:
- It motivates a two-stage learning procedure by starting with a likelihood-based state-action distribution matching objective that would require bi-level optimization (Eq. 2)
- It presents a lower-bound of the objective in Theorem 4.1, in which optimization can occur serially in two components: first, maximum likelihood estimation of the expert's visitation distribution, and second, policy optimization over two terms consisting of the behavior cloning loss and the log-likelihood of visited states under the modeled visitation distribution
- It provides a proof that this two-stage formulation is a tight lower bound on the original bilevel optimization problem
- It proposes a concrete approach to performing this optimization by using a denoising continuous normalizing flow (DCNF) for modeling the expert's visitation distribution, where the CNF is motivated by its ability to model densities of complex distributions without requiring bijective transformations, and the denoising is motivated to enable stable training in situations where data lie in lower-dimensional manifolds
- It performs comprehensive experiments on 10 MuJoCo environments demonstrating that the proposed approach achieves better mode coverage than comparable methods, that the denoising mechanism is empirically effective for generalization, and that the algorithm is more robust than GAIL to variations in learning rate and other hyperparameters

## Main Strengths

### Primary Strength: Addresses Multiple Key IL Desiderata

This paper's main strength is that it proposes a straightforward method that both conceptually and empirically satisfies important desiderata of imitation learning methods. Specifically, the method addresses three critical challenges in the IL literature: (1) the cascading error problem inherent to BC-based methods (Ross et al., 2011), (2) the training instability and mode collapse problems of adversarially-trained IL methods like GAIL, and (3) the need to avoid dependence on an interactive demonstrator (as required by DAGGER-style approaches). This is a significant achievement, as most prior work has focused on addressing only one or two of these issues in isolation.

The paper's framing around the manifold hypothesis challenge (Section 3, Challenge C#2) is particularly insightful. The authors clearly articulate why standard continuous normalizing flows fail when expert state distributions lie on low-dimensional manifolds, and they provide a compelling visual illustration in Figure 5 showing how FFJORD's latent space recovery fails while their DCNF approach succeeds. This diagnosis of the problem is well-motivated and directly leads to their proposed solution.

### Secondary Strength: Comprehensive Empirical Validation

The experimental evaluation is thorough and well-designed. The paper evaluates the method across multiple dimensions:
- **Mode coverage preservation** (Figures 1, 7, and Table 1): The visualization of mode coverage using kernel density estimation effectively demonstrates that SLIL preserves the multi-modal structure of expert demonstrations while baselines exhibit mode collapse. The quantitative evaluation using Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) provides additional rigor.
- **Expert state distribution quality** (Figure 9 and Table 1): The comparison of test state log-likelihood and latent space recovery between DCNF, SoftFlow, and CNF clearly validates the necessity of the denoising mechanism.
- **Policy performance** (Figures 10 and 8): The results show that SLIL achieves comparable or superior performance to GAIL while avoiding the instability issues associated with adversarial training.
- **Training stability analysis** (Figures 11, 12, 13): The ablation studies on the gating parameter λ, learning rate robustness, and sample efficiency provide valuable insights into the method's behavior and demonstrate clear advantages over GAIL in terms of hyperparameter robustness.

The fact that SLIL outperforms baselines particularly with limited expert demonstrations (as shown in Figure 10) is noteworthy, as this is a practically important regime where many IL methods struggle.

### Tertiary Strength: Clear Motivation for Two-Stage Decomposition

The motivation for decomposing the bilevel optimization problem in Eq. (2) into a two-stage problem (Eq. 3) is well-articulated. The authors clearly identify two key challenges (C#1 training stability and C#2 manifold hypothesis) and propose targeted solutions. The two-stage approach is not merely a computational convenience but is theoretically justified through Theorem 4.1, which establishes that the two-stage objective provides a tight lower bound on the original bilevel problem. This principled approach distinguishes the paper from purely heuristic methods.

## Main Weaknesses

### Critical Issue: Imprecision in Proof and Mathematical Presentation

The paper's main weakness is some imprecision in several key places, particularly in the mathematical formulation and proof of Theorem 4.1. These issues prevent complete verification of the theoretical contributions and raise questions about the rigor of the theoretical foundation.

#### Issue (a): Unclear Component References in Proof

The proof refers to "component ②" in Eq. 1, yet Eq. 1 is presented without explicitly denoted components. The proof statement in Appendix A reads: "with sufficient expert data $\mathcal{D}_E$, ② can be expressed with $\sum_{s \in \mathcal{D}_\pi} \frac{P_E(s)}{P_\pi(s)} \log P_\pi(s)$." It is unclear whether this reference is to Eq. 1, Eq. 2, or Eq. 3. If the reference is indeed to Eq. 1, then Eq. 1 should explicitly denote its second component with an underbrace or similar notation for clarity. If not, the proof should specify which equation's component is being referenced. This ambiguity makes it difficult for readers to follow the logical flow of the proof and verify its correctness.

To address this, the authors should either: (i) explicitly number or denote the components in the equations being referenced, or (ii) rewrite the proof to refer to equations by number rather than component numbers, or (iii) provide a clearer mapping between component numbers and equation numbers in the appendix.

#### Issue (b): Inconsistency in Component Definitions

The proof states that "with sufficient expert data $\mathcal{D}_E$, ② can be expressed with $\sum_{s \in \mathcal{D}_\pi} \frac{P_E(s)}{P_\pi(s)} \log P_\pi(s)$." However, component ② in Eq. (3) is defined as $\sum_{s \in \mathcal{D}_\pi} \log P_E(s)$, where the expectation is taken over states sampled from the learner policy's state distribution. These two expressions are fundamentally different: the proposed expression includes a ratio term $\frac{P_E(s)}{P_\pi(s)}$ that does not appear in the original formulation. The relationship between these expressions is not explained. Either the proof statement is incorrect, or there is a missing derivation step that explains how the original component can be rewritten in this form. This discrepancy is a significant concern that undermines confidence in the proof.

The authors should provide a detailed derivation showing how the expression $\sum_{s \in \mathcal{D}_\pi} \log P_E(s)$ can be rewritten as $\sum_{s \in \mathcal{D}_\pi} \frac{P_E(s)}{P_\pi(s)} \log P_\pi(s)$ (if this is indeed the intended claim), or clarify what the correct relationship is.

#### Issue (c): Validity of the Lower Bound Inequality

It is not immediately obvious from the definition of $g(\pi, P_\pi)$ that the inequality $\max_{\pi^*} \sum_{s \in \mathcal{D}_{\pi^*}} \log P_E(s) \geq \max_\pi \sum_{s \in \mathcal{D}_\pi} \log P_E(s)$ holds in general. There are two specific concerns:

**(c.i) Dataset size normalization:** The sums in the inequality are

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper present a novel method for imitation learning aiming to stabilize training and mode coverage compared to existing methods. The proposed method (SLIL) extends behavioral cloning by an additional objective of maximizing a state-only reward that is defined by a density estimate (trained once beforehand) of the expert's state distribution.
For density estimation SLIL uses a continuous normalizing flow where the expert states are corrupted by Gaussian noise, where the noise-level is decreased during training. The noise is introduced to tackle the "Manifold Hypothesis Challenge", that is, the inability of normalizing flows to accurate approximate distributions that lie on a lower-dimensional manifold. 

The main contributions are:
1. A novel imitation learning method that combines the BC loss with a RL loss for reaching expert states
2. A slightly modified approach to tackle the manifold hypothesis challenge (Kim et al. [2020] used Gaussian noise where the noise-level was sampled from a uniform distribution, independently for each data set, rather than using the same noise-level for all data and annealing it during optimization)
reviews.main_review:
Strong Points
* The empirical results are good: SLIL achieves good results, no matter whether few demonstrations or many demonstrations are provided.
* The presentation is good, however, I have some suggestions below.

Weak Points
* Technical correctness (1): The main claim (Theorem 4.1) that SLIL tightly bounds the likelihood objective (LIL) is not supported. The SLIL objective differs by substituting the term $E_{p_\text{E}(s)}\big[ \log p_{\pi}(s) \big]$ with $E_{p_{\pi}(s)}\big[ \log p_{\text{E}}(s) \big]$, that is, switching the expert' and agent's state distribution. The proof has several issues: First of all, it starts with circular reasoning, by assuming that the optimal policy of the SLIL objective, $\pi^\star$,  matches the expert's state distribution. It then derives the bound (which is only valid for $\pi^\star$) that uses the equality $\max_x  f(x) + \max_x g(x) \ge \max_x \big[ f(x) + g(x) \big]$ which is not at all tight in general.
* Technical correctness (2): The SLIL objective is further modified by introducing a hyper-parameter that disables the RL objective ever $\lambda$ iterations, which effectively changes the objective being optimized without any theoretical justification.

* Novelty (1): SLIL is a hybrid between BC and Energy-Based Imitation Learning (EBIL) [2], a reference certainly missing. Like SLIL, State-only EBIL starts by estimating the expert's state distribution and uses it as a fixed reward function for imitation learning. As the SLIL loss is basically an interpolation between two prior imitation learning losses the contribution seems minor, in particular given that the objective of SLIL is not well motivated (Technical correctness (1)). It is also worth noting that AIRL uses noise contrastive estimation to estimate the expert policy and uses this density estimate directly as reward for MaxEnt-RL, which is similar to the 2nd term of the SLIL objective. The differences are thus that AIRL ues MaxEnt-RL instead of RL, $r=\log p_E(a|s)$ instead of $r=\log p_E(s)$, and NCE with an EBM for density estimation rather than maximum likelihood with normalizing flows. 

* Novelty (2): The "Denoising continuous normalizing flow" seems to be a minor variant of Softflow [Kim et al. 2020]. Furthermore, this minor contribution is not evaluated at all (neither Softflow, nor any other methods for handling the manifold hypothesis challenge were compared). 

* Evaluation:  The improved stability compared to GAIL was not demonstrated. Please add learning curves for the data of Fig. 10 to the Appendix (choosing a suitable number of demonstrations (e..g. 5) for each experiment).

Additional Comments
* It is not clear why the hyper-parameter lambda was introduced in favor of a fixed coefficient that trades off the two objectives, which would at least maintain a fixed objective function. 
* Much of 4.2 seems to be related work. I think it would make more sense to put it before the "Method" section to make the contributions more clear. In exchange, the problem formulation of LIL could be put under method, since it is introduced to "derive" the SLIL objective.
* Algorithmbox 1 is almost useless, because it mainly shows that we have an iterative training loop, but doesn't really show what happens at each iteration.
* End of page 5. The definition of $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s}|s)$ looks like  $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s},s)$ to me.

References:
[1] Liu, R., Gao, J., Zhang, J., Meng, D., & Lin, Z. (2021). Investigating bi-level optimization for learning and vision from a unified perspective: A survey and beyond. arXiv preprint arXiv:2101.11517.

[2] Liu, M., He, T., Xu, M., & Zhang, W. (2020). Energy-based imitation learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2004.09395.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The contributions seem rather incremental and the claims are not well-supported.
I still gave a relatively high recommendation, because the results are fine and the overall ideas of 1) tackling covariance shift of BC by using an RL objective to match the expert state distribution, and 2) estimating the expert state distribution once instead of iteratively based on NCE with adapted noise to improve stability, are reasonable.
reviews.correctness: 2: Several of the paper’s claims are incorrect or not well-supported.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
