The noise is introduced to tackle the "Manifold Hypothesis Challenge", that is, the inability of normalizing flows to accurate approximate distributions that lie on a lower-dimensional manifold. 

The main contributions are:
1. A novel imitation learning method that combines the BC loss with a RL loss for reaching expert states
2. A slightly modified approach to tackle the manifold hypothesis challenge (Kim et al. [2020] used Gaussian noise where the noise-level was sampled from a uniform distribution, independently for each data set, rather than using the same noise-level for all data and annealing it during optimization)

Main Review:
Strong Points
* The empirical results are good: SLIL achieves good results, no matter whether few demonstrations or many demonstrations are provided.
* The presentation is good, however, I have some suggestions below.

Weak Points
* Technical correctness (1): The main claim (Theorem 4.1) that SLIL tightly bounds the likelihood objective (LIL) is not supported. The SLIL objective differs by substituting the term $E_{p_\text{E}(s)}\big[ \log p_{\pi}(s) \big]$ with $E_{p_{\pi}(s)}\big[ \log p_{\text{E}}(s) \big]$, that is, switching the expert' and agent's state distribution. The proof has several issues: First of all, it starts with circular reasoning, by assuming that the optimal policy of the SLIL objective, $\pi^\star$,  matches the expert's state distribution. It then derives the bound (which is only valid for $\pi^\star$) that uses the equality $\max_x  f(x) + \max_x g(x) \ge \max_x \big[ f(x) + g(x) \big]$ which is not at all tight in general.
* Technical correctness (2): The SLIL objective is further modified by introducing a hyper-parameter that disables the RL objective ever $\lambda$ iterations, which effectively changes the objective being optimized without any theoretical justification.

* Novelty (1): SLIL is a hybrid between BC and Energy-Based Imitation Learning (EBIL) [2], a reference certainly missing. Like SLIL, State-only EBIL starts by estimating the expert's state distribution and uses it as a fixed reward function for imitation learning. As the SLIL loss is basically an interpolation between two prior imitation learning losses the contribution seems minor, in particular given that the objective of SLIL is not well motivated (Technical correctness (1)). It is also worth noting that AIRL uses noise contrastive estimation to estimate the expert policy and uses this density estimate directly as reward for MaxEnt-RL, which is similar to the 2nd term of the SLIL objective. The differences are thus that AIRL ues MaxEnt-RL instead of RL, $r=\log p_E(a|s)$ instead of $r=\log p_E(s)$, and NCE with an EBM for density estimation rather than maximum likelihood with normalizing flows. 

* Novelty (2): The "Denoising continuous normalizing flow" seems to be a minor variant of Softflow [Kim et al. 2020]. Furthermore, this minor contribution is not evaluated at all (neither Softflow, nor any other methods for handling the manifold hypothesis challenge were compared). 

* Evaluation:  The improved stability compared to GAIL was not demonstrated. Please add learning curves for the data of Fig. 10 to the Appendix (choosing a suitable number of demonstrations (e..g. 5) for each experiment).

Additional Comments
* It is not clear why the hyper-parameter lambda was introduced in favor of a fixed coefficient that trades off the two objectives, which would at least maintain a fixed objective function. 
* Much of 4.2 seems to be related work. I think it would make more sense to put it before the "Method" section to make the contributions more clear. In exchange, the problem formulation of LIL could be put under method, since it is introduced to "derive" the SLIL objective.
* Algorithmbox 1 is almost useless, because it mainly shows that we have an iterative training loop, but doesn't really show what happens at each iteration.
* End of page 5. The definition of $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s}|s)$ looks like  $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s},s)$ to me.

References:
[1] Liu, R., Gao, J., Zhang, J., Meng, D., & Lin, Z. (2021). Investigating bi-level optimization for learning and vision from a unified perspective: A survey and beyond. arXiv preprint arXiv:2101.11517.

[2] Liu, M., He, T., Xu, M., & Zhang, W. (2020). Energy-based imitation learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2004.09395.

Summary Of The Review:
The contributions seem rather incremental and the claims are not well-supported.
I still gave a relatively high recommendation, because the results are fine and the overall ideas of 1) tackling covariance shift of BC by using an RL objective to match the expert state distribution, and 2) estimating the expert state distribution once instead of iteratively based on NCE with adapted noise to improve stability, are reasonable.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

# Expanded Review: STABILIZED LIKELIHOOD-BASED IMITATION LEARNING VIA DENOISING CONTINUOUS NORMALIZING FLOW

## Summary Of The Paper

The paper presents a novel method for imitation learning that aims to stabilize training and improve mode coverage compared to existing methods like GAIL. The proposed approach, SLIL (Stabilized Likelihood-based Imitation Learning), extends behavioral cloning by incorporating an additional objective of maximizing a state-only reward defined by a density estimate of the expert's state distribution. This density estimate is trained once in a preliminary stage before policy learning begins.

For density estimation, SLIL employs a continuous normalizing flow where expert states are corrupted by Gaussian noise with a noise level that decreases during training. The authors introduce this noise corruption mechanism to address what they term the "Manifold Hypothesis Challenge"—the inability of standard normalizing flows to accurately approximate distributions that lie on lower-dimensional manifolds embedded in higher-dimensional spaces. The paper demonstrates results on ten physics-based control tasks from MuJoCo, showing improvements in mode coverage preservation, training stability, and policy performance compared to baselines including GAIL, BC, DRIL, and GPRIL.

The main contributions claimed are:
1. A novel imitation learning method that combines the BC loss with an RL loss for reaching expert states, framed as a likelihood-based approach with theoretical justification via Theorem 4.1
2. A denoising continuous normalizing flow (DCNF) approach that systematically anneals noise levels during training to tackle the manifold hypothesis challenge
3. Empirical validation demonstrating superior mode coverage preservation and training stability across multiple tasks

## Strong Points

* **Comprehensive empirical evaluation**: The experimental results are convincing and well-presented. SLIL achieves strong performance across a diverse set of tasks, demonstrating effectiveness whether few or many expert demonstrations are available. The visualization of mode coverage in Figures 1 and 7 effectively illustrates the key advantage of the method over GAIL, which exhibits clear mode collapse on multi-modal tasks like Reacher4 and HalfCheetah2. The quantitative EMD results in Figure 8 and Table 1 provide additional support for the mode preservation claims.

* **Clear presentation and motivation**: The paper is generally well-written with good motivation for the problem. The introduction effectively uses Figure 1 to illustrate the mode collapse problem in GAIL on the Reacher4 task, making the motivation immediately clear. The two-stage framework is intuitive and easy to understand, and the progression from the LIL formulation to SLIL is logical.

* **Practical stability improvements**: The experimental analysis of training stability (Section 5.4) provides useful insights. The robustness to learning rate changes (Figure 12) is particularly noteworthy, showing that SLIL works reliably across a wide range of learning rates (1e-5 to 1e-4) while GAIL fails at higher learning rates (1e-3 and above). This is a meaningful practical advantage for practitioners. Similarly, the faster convergence demonstrated in Figure 13 (achieving comparable performance with fewer environment interactions) is valuable.

* **Addresses a real problem**: The focus on mode coverage preservation in imitation learning is well-motivated and important. Many real-world expert demonstrations exhibit multi-modal behavior, and the failure of GAIL to preserve these modes is a genuine limitation that deserves attention.

* **Reasonable denoising mechanism**: The intuition behind adding noise to handle the manifold hypothesis is sound, and the systematic annealing schedule (decreasing noise over iterations) is more principled than alternatives.

## Major Weaknesses

### Technical Correctness (1): Theorem 4.1 and the Tightness Claim

The central theoretical claim of the paper—that SLIL provides a tight lower bound on the LIL objective—is not adequately supported and contains significant issues. This is particularly concerning since the theoretical justification is presented as a key contribution.

**The core problem**: Theorem 4.1 claims to show that the LIL objective in Eq. (2) is "tightly lower bounded" by the SLIL objective in Eq. (3). However, the SLIL objective fundamentally changes what is being optimized by substituting $E_{s \sim D_E}[\log P_\pi(s)]$ (the expected log probability of expert states under the learner's state distribution) with $E_{s \sim D_\pi}[\log P_E(s)]$ (the expected log probability of the learner's states under the expert's state distribution). These are not equivalent expressions; they involve different expectations over different distributions.

**Issues with the proof**: The proof in Appendix A exhibits circular reasoning. It begins by assuming that the optimal policy $\pi^*$ of the SLIL objective already matches the expert's state distribution (i.e., $P_{\pi^*}(s) = P_E(s)$). Under this assumption, it then derives the bound. However, this reasoning is circular: the bound is only valid for policies that already satisfy the property we are trying to achieve. The proof then invokes the inequality $\max_x f(x) + \max_x g(x) \geq \max_x [f(x) + g(x)]$ to separate the optimization, but this inequality is notoriously loose in general and does not guarantee a tight bound. The separation of the two optimization problems (policy learning and state distribution estimation) may be practically useful, but the tightness claim is not justified.

**Implications**: Without a valid theoretical justification, the SLIL framework becomes an engineering choice rather than a principled approach derived from the LIL objective. While the empirical results may still be good, the theoretical contribution—presented as a key novelty—is undermined. The paper would be stronger if it either: (1) provided a correct proof of the bound's tightness, (2) acknowledged that the bound is loose and quantified how loose it is, or (3) reframed the contribution as a practical two-stage decomposition without claiming theoretical tightness.

### Technical Correctness (2): Unjustified Modification via Gating Function

The introduction of the gating function $\mathbb{1}_\lambda(i)$ in Eq. (4) that disables the expert state distribution matching objective every $\lambda$ iterations is a significant modification to the optimization objective, yet it lacks theoretical justification.

**The problem**: The paper modifies Eq. (3) to become Eq. (4) by introducing $\mathbb{1}_\lambda(i)$, which sets the second term to zero whenever $(i \mod \lambda) \neq 0$. This effectively changes the objective function being optimized at different iterations. The motivation provided is intuitive (balancing the learning speed between the two components), but this is an engineering heuristic without theoretical grounding.

**Missing analysis**: The paper does not analyze what objective function is actually being optimized when using this gating mechanism. Is it still optimizing the SLIL objective in Eq. (3)? What are the convergence properties? The paper instead relegates the justification to a single sentence referencing Lee et al. (2018), which is insufficient given the significance of this modification.

**Hyperparameter sensitivity**: Figure 11 shows that performance is quite sensitive to the choice of $\lambda$, with $\lambda=2$ performing best, $\lambda=1$ showing high variance, and $\lambda \geq 3$ showing slower convergence. This suggests that the gating function introduces a critical hyperparameter that requires careful tuning. The paper does not provide principled guidance on how to set $\lambda$ for new tasks, nor does it analyze how sensitive results are to this choice across different domains.

**Alternative approaches**: A fixed coefficient weighting the two objectives would maintain a consistent optimization target and would be more principled than the gating function. The paper does not justify why the gating approach is superior to such alternatives.

### Novelty (1): Limited Novelty and Missing Related Work

The core contribution of combining behavior cloning with a state distribution matching objective is not sufficiently novel, and the paper omits important related work that undermines the novelty claims.

**Relationship to EBIL**: The paper does not cite or discuss Energy-Based Imitation Learning (EBIL) by Liu et al. (2020), which is mentioned in the related work section but not adequately analyzed. EBIL also estimates the expert's state distribution in an initial stage and then uses it as a fixed reward function for subsequent policy learning. The key difference between SLIL and EBIL is the method used for state distribution estimation (normalizing flows vs. score matching) and the RL algorithm used (PPO vs. MaxEnt-RL). However, the overall framework—decomposing IL into state distribution estimation followed by policy learning—is not novel to SLIL.

**Relationship to AIRL and other methods**: The paper briefly mentions AIRL but does not adequately discuss how SLIL relates to inverse RL approaches that also estimate expert rewar
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper present a novel method for imitation learning aiming to stabilize training and mode coverage compared to existing methods. The proposed method (SLIL) extends behavioral cloning by an additional objective of maximizing a state-only reward that is defined by a density estimate (trained once beforehand) of the expert's state distribution.
For density estimation SLIL uses a continuous normalizing flow where the expert states are corrupted by Gaussian noise, where the noise-level is decreased during training. The noise is introduced to tackle the "Manifold Hypothesis Challenge", that is, the inability of normalizing flows to accurate approximate distributions that lie on a lower-dimensional manifold. 

The main contributions are:
1. A novel imitation learning method that combines the BC loss with a RL loss for reaching expert states
2. A slightly modified approach to tackle the manifold hypothesis challenge (Kim et al. [2020] used Gaussian noise where the noise-level was sampled from a uniform distribution, independently for each data set, rather than using the same noise-level for all data and annealing it during optimization)

Main Review:
Strong Points
* The empirical results are good: SLIL achieves good results, no matter whether few demonstrations or many demonstrations are provided.
* The presentation is good, however, I have some suggestions below.

Weak Points
* Technical correctness (1): The main claim (Theorem 4.1) that SLIL tightly bounds the likelihood objective (LIL) is not supported. The SLIL objective differs by substituting the term $E_{p_\text{E}(s)}\big[ \log p_{\pi}(s) \big]$ with $E_{p_{\pi}(s)}\big[ \log p_{\text{E}}(s) \big]$, that is, switching the expert' and agent's state distribution. The proof has several issues: First of all, it starts with circular reasoning, by assuming that the optimal policy of the SLIL objective, $\pi^\star$,  matches the expert's state distribution. It then derives the bound (which is only valid for $\pi^\star$) that uses the equality $\max_x  f(x) + \max_x g(x) \ge \max_x \big[ f(x) + g(x) \big]$ which is not at all tight in general.
* Technical correctness (2): The SLIL objective is further modified by introducing a hyper-parameter that disables the RL objective ever $\lambda$ iterations, which effectively changes the objective being optimized without any theoretical justification.

* Novelty (1): SLIL is a hybrid between BC and Energy-Based Imitation Learning (EBIL) [2], a reference certainly missing. Like SLIL, State-only EBIL starts by estimating the expert's state distribution and uses it as a fixed reward function for imitation learning. As the SLIL loss is basically an interpolation between two prior imitation learning losses the contribution seems minor, in particular given that the objective of SLIL is not well motivated (Technical correctness (1)). It is also worth noting that AIRL uses noise contrastive estimation to estimate the expert policy and uses this density estimate directly as reward for MaxEnt-RL, which is similar to the 2nd term of the SLIL objective. The differences are thus that AIRL ues MaxEnt-RL instead of RL, $r=\log p_E(a|s)$ instead of $r=\log p_E(s)$, and NCE with an EBM for density estimation rather than maximum likelihood with normalizing flows. 

* Novelty (2): The "Denoising continuous normalizing flow" seems to be a minor variant of Softflow [Kim et al. 2020]. Furthermore, this minor contribution is not evaluated at all (neither Softflow, nor any other methods for handling the manifold hypothesis challenge were compared). 

* Evaluation:  The improved stability compared to GAIL was not demonstrated. Please add learning curves for the data of Fig. 10 to the Appendix (choosing a suitable number of demonstrations (e..g. 5) for each experiment).

Additional Comments
* It is not clear why the hyper-parameter lambda was introduced in favor of a fixed coefficient that trades off the two objectives, which would at least maintain a fixed objective function. 
* Much of 4.2 seems to be related work. I think it would make more sense to put it before the "Method" section to make the contributions more clear. In exchange, the problem formulation of LIL could be put under method, since it is introduced to "derive" the SLIL objective.
* Algorithmbox 1 is almost useless, because it mainly shows that we have an iterative training loop, but doesn't really show what happens at each iteration.
* End of page 5. The definition of $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s}|s)$ looks like  $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s},s)$ to me.

References:
[1] Liu, R., Gao, J., Zhang, J., Meng, D., & Lin, Z. (2021). Investigating bi-level optimization for learning and vision from a unified perspective: A survey and beyond. arXiv preprint arXiv:2101.11517.

[2] Liu, M., He, T., Xu, M., & Zhang, W. (2020). Energy-based imitation learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2004.09395.

Summary Of The Review:
The contributions seem rather incremental and the claims are not well-supported.
I still gave a relatively high recommendation, because the results are fine and the overall ideas of 1) tackling covariance shift of BC by using an RL objective to match the expert state distribution, and 2) estimating the expert state distribution once instead of iteratively based on NCE with adapted noise to improve stability, are reasonable.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

The paper proposes an approach to the problem of state-action based imitation learning, in which an agent aims to solve a specific task given state-action expert trajectories. Their objective SLIL combines (or interpolates between) generative modeling and behavioral cloning in order to avoid mode collapse of the agent policy (common issue of adversarial approaches to IL like GAIL) and to mitigate distributional shift which BC typically suffers from.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary Of The Paper

The paper proposes SLIL (Stabilized Likelihood-based Imitation Learning), an approach to address fundamental challenges in state-action based imitation learning. The core problem the authors tackle is that existing methods suffer from complementary limitations: behavioral cloning (BC) struggles with distributional shift and requires large amounts of expert data, while adversarial approaches like GAIL are prone to training instability and mode collapse when learning from multi-modal expert demonstrations. Their solution combines generative modeling with behavioral cloning to avoid mode collapse while mitigating distributional shift. The key innovation is a two-stage optimization framework where the first stage estimates the expert state distribution using a novel denoising continuous normalizing flow (DCNF), and the second stage trains a learner policy to match both the expert's policy and state distribution.

## Contributions

The paper makes three primary contributions that the authors clearly articulate:

- **Likelihood-based IL methodology**: The authors develop the first likelihood-based imitation learning approach that explicitly tackles the training instability issues inherent in GAIL. Their two-stage optimization framework is theoretically motivated by a tight lower bound on the joint policy and state distribution training objective derived from the likelihood-based IL formulation (presented in Theorem 4.1 and Section 4.1).

- **Denoising Continuous Normalizing Flow (DCNF)**: The authors propose DCNF as an extension of continuous normalizing flows to address the manifold hypothesis challenge. Rather than assuming data lies in the full ambient space, DCNF explicitly handles the case where expert states concentrate on low-dimensional manifolds by progressively adding and then removing Gaussian noise during training. This is trained via maximum likelihood estimation with a carefully designed noise schedule.

- **Comprehensive empirical validation**: The method is evaluated across ten distinct physics-based control tasks with detailed ablations demonstrating robustness to hyperparameter variations, superior performance on mode coverage preservation, and improved training stability compared to multiple baselines.

## Main Review

### Strengths

- **Well-motivated approach with clear problem formulation**: The paper is exceptionally well-motivated, clearly articulating how the proposed approach aims to mitigate the complementary limitations of both BC (distributional shift problem) and GAIL-like approaches (mode collapse and instability of minimax optimization). The use of Figure 1 effectively illustrates the mode collapse problem in GAIL and demonstrates how SLIL preserves the expert's multi-modal distribution. The motivation is further strengthened by the bilevel optimization perspective presented in Section 3, which naturally leads to the two-stage formulation.

- **Strong empirical performance across diverse benchmarks**: The algorithm demonstrates consistently superior performance across all ten benchmark environments, which are themselves sufficiently distinct to provide meaningful validation. The experiments span from relatively simple tasks like CartPole to complex humanoid locomotion. Particularly compelling are the mode coverage results in Figures 1 and 7, where SLIL visibly preserves the expert's multi-modal state distribution while baselines like GAIL exhibit clear mode collapse. The quantitative EMD results in Figure 8 further support these qualitative observations, showing that SLIL achieves both lower distribution mismatch and higher expected returns.

- **Thorough ablation studies demonstrating robustness**: The ablations presented in Section 5.4 are comprehensive and insightful. Figure 11 demonstrates the importance of the gating parameter τ, showing that the method's performance is sensitive to the balance between behavior cloning and state distribution matching, which is an important finding for practitioners. Figure 12 provides particularly valuable evidence that SLIL is more robust to learning rate choices than GAIL—SLIL maintains reasonable performance across learning rates from 1e-4 to 1e-2, while GAIL fails at higher learning rates due to the instability of adversarial training. This robustness is a significant practical advantage. Figure 13 shows that SLIL achieves better performance with fewer environment interactions, suggesting the pre-trained expert state distribution provides effective guidance.

- **Clear presentation and intuitive visualizations**: The paper is generally well-written with effective use of visualizations. Figure 5 provides an intuitive illustration of how DCNF addresses the manifold hypothesis by comparing the latent space recovery of FFJORD versus DCNF on 1D and 2D manifolds. The algorithmic framework is presented clearly with Figures 2, 3, and 4 effectively conveying the differences between the naive LIL bilevel optimization and the proposed two-stage SLIL approach.

### Weaknesses

- **Technical issues in the DCNF formulation and presentation**: There are several concerning technical issues in the description of the proposed denoising continuous normalizing flow approach that require clarification or correction:

  - **Conditional probability notation concern**: The paper claims that $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s}|s) = P_E(s)\mathcal{N}(\tilde{s}| s, \sigma^2I)$. However, the right-hand side appears to be the joint distribution $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s}, s)$ rather than the conditional distribution $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s}|s)$ as claimed. If this is indeed meant to represent the joint distribution, then the conditional would be obtained through marginalization: $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s}|s) = \mathcal{N}(\tilde{s}| s, \sigma^2I)$ (independent of $P_E(s)$), and the marginal would be $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s}) = \int P_E(s)\mathcal{N}(\tilde{s}| s, \sigma^2I) ds$. This distinction is crucial for understanding how the denoising mechanism works mathematically. The authors should clarify the precise probabilistic model they are using and provide the correct derivations.

  - **Zero-noise limit argument**: The statement that "when the noise level is very small, i.e., $\sigma \to 0$, $\tilde{s}$ approaches $s$" is intuitively correct but the mathematical argument that follows lacks rigor. A proper treatment would require careful analysis of how the learned distribution $P_{E,\omega}(\tilde{s})$ converges to $P_E(s)$ as $\sigma \to 0$. The authors invoke the idea that perturbed data expands from a low-dimensional manifold to the high-dimensional ambient space, but this argument would benefit from more formal justification. Specifically, how does the likelihood objective ensure that as noise decreases, the learned model maintains fidelity to the original distribution? This is particularly important given that the model is trained on perturbed data rather than the original data.

  - **Noise schedule notation inconsistency**: In Algorithm 1, line 3, the notation shows sampling noise with $\sigma_j I$ (where $j$ is the batch index), but the text and noise schedule definition use $\sigma_i$ (where $i$ is the iteration index). This appears to be a notational error. The noise level should vary by iteration (as it follows a decreasing schedule), not by sample within a batch. The correct formulation should use $\sigma_i^2$ as the noise variance at iteration $i$, applied uniformly to all samples in that iteration's batch. This inconsistency makes it difficult to understand the exact algorithm being implemented and raises questions about whether the authors have carefully verified their algorithm.

- **Incomplete theoretical justification for the two-stage relaxation**: While Theorem 4.1 claims that the LIL bilevel optimization is "lower bounded tightly" by the two-stage optimization problem in Equation 3, the proof is relegated to the appendix and the intuition is not fully explained in the main text. The phrase "tightly lower bounded" requires precise definition—does this mean the gap between the original objective and the relaxed objective is zero under certain conditions? The paper would benefit from more discussion of when this bound is tight and what assumptions are required. Additionally, the relationship between the lower bound and the actual performance of the method in practice is unclear. How much does the gap between the original bilevel objective and the two-stage relaxation affect the quality of the learned policy? This theoretical gap could have practical implications that should be discussed.

- **Limited discussion of failure cases and method limitations**: The paper does not thoroughly discuss when SLIL might fail or perform poorly. For instance:

  - What happens when the expert demonstrations have very limited state coverage? While the paper shows results with different numbers of trajectories, it does not analyze how the method degrades as data becomes increasingly sparse.
  
  - How sensitive is DCNF to the choice of initial noise level $\sigma_1$ and the noise schedule? The paper fixes these choices but provides no sensitivity analysis or principled way to select them for new domains.
  
  - The method requires learning a separate normalizing flow model for state distribution estimation. How does the quality of this model affect downstream policy learning? What happens if the

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposed Stabilized Likelihood-based Imitation Learning (SLIL) which iteratively estimates the expert state distribution by using Denoising Continuous Normalizing Flow (DCNF) and maximizes the policy learning objective in Eq. (3) that matches expert policy and state distribution. Compared to other baselines such as BC, DRIL, GAIL, SLIL was shown to find out better expert state distribution (in terms of seeking multiple modes, Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) while achieving the empirical return close to that of the expert (which is widely used in the imitation learning literature). The strength of using DCNF instead of using Continuous Normalizing Flow (CNF) is well desribed and supported by the experiments evaluating the test log likelihood in Figure 9. Authors also analyze SLIL's hyperparameter sensitivity that supports the claim on the SLIL's stability.
reviews.main_review:
Regarding the strength of this work, the contribution of this work over given baselines is clear, and I'm satisfied with the detailed explanations and experiments, e.g., comparison with LIL, the justification of using DCNF rather than CNF, how DCNF is trained algorithmically, quantitative tests using EMD and test log likelihoods, etc. 

Regarding the weakness of this work, I think some recent relevant works are missed, e.g., Dadashi et al., "Primal Wasserstein Imitation Learning", Kim et al., "Imitation with Neural Density Models", and some references therein. Although these papers didn't aim to solve the mode collapsing directly, their algorithms are much more sample-efficient than GAIL in terms of the number of expert trajectories and the number of environment interactions (i.e., GAIL is no more a SOTA algorithm as stated in this work), and both are relevant to this work in the sense that expert support estimation is used. Primal Wasserstein Imitation Learning (PWIL) shows their algorithm works efficiently even when we have a small number of expert trajectories (1 or 11 trajectories), whereas this work presented empirical results assuming more than 4 expert trajectories (more than 80 expert trajectories for Humanoid). In "Imitation with Neural Density Models", energy-based model is used to estimate expert support estimation, which are based on maximmum likelihood estimation similar to this work.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, writing is clear, and the empirical results are shown to outperform GAIL and some other baselines. However, some SOTA algorithms (which I believe is relevant to this work) are missed, and it is unclear that the mode collapsing issue also happens for those relevant works or not. Although I agree that there is an algorithmic novelty for this work, I believe more elaborated results should be given with the aforementioned references.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary Of The Paper:
This work proposed Stabilized Likelihood-based Imitation Learning (SLIL) which iteratively estimates the expert state distribution by using Denoising Continuous Normalizing Flow (DCNF) and maximizes the policy learning objective in Eq. (3) that matches expert policy and state distribution. Compared to other baselines such as BC, DRIL, GAIL, SLIL was shown to find out better expert state distribution (in terms of seeking multiple modes, Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) while achieving the empirical return close to that of the expert (which is widely used in the imitation learning literature). The strength of using DCNF instead of using Continuous Normalizing Flow (CNF) is well desribed and supported by the experiments evaluating the test log likelihood in Figure 9. Authors also analyze SLIL's hyperparameter sensitivity that supports the claim on the SLIL's stability.

Main Review:
Regarding the strength of this work, the contribution of this work over given baselines is clear, and I'm satisfied with the detailed explanations and experiments, e.g., comparison with LIL, the justification of using DCNF rather than CNF, how DCNF is trained algorithmically, quantitative tests using EMD and test log likelihoods, etc. 

Regarding the weakness of this work, I think some recent relevant works are missed, e.g., Dadashi et al., "Primal Wasserstein Imitation Learning", Kim et al., "Imitation with Neural Density Models", and some references therein. Although these papers didn't aim to solve the mode collapsing directly, their algorithms are much more sample-efficient than GAIL in terms of the number of expert trajectories and the number of environment interactions (i.e., GAIL is no more a SOTA algorithm as stated in this work), and both are relevant to this work in the sense that expert support estimation is used. Primal Wasserstein Imitation Learning (PWIL) shows their algorithm works efficiently even when we have a small number of expert trajectories (1 or 11 trajectories), whereas this work presented empirical results assuming more than 4 expert trajectories (more than 80 expert trajectories for Humanoid). In "Imitation with Neural Density Models", energy-based model is used to estimate expert support estimation, which are based on maximmum likelihood estimation similar to this work.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, writing is clear, and the empirical results are shown to outperform GAIL and some other baselines. However, some SOTA algorithms (which I believe is relevant to this work) are missed, and it is unclear that the mode collapsing issue also happens for those relevant works or not. Although I agree that there is an algorithmic novelty for this work, I believe more elaborated results should be given with the aforementioned references.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: STABILIZED LIKELIHOOD-BASED IMITATION LEARNING VIA DENOISING CONTINUOUS NORMALIZING FLOW

## Summary of the Paper

This work proposes Stabilized Likelihood-based Imitation Learning (SLIL), a novel imitation learning approach that addresses the training instability and mode collapse issues inherent in adversarial imitation learning methods like GAIL. The core contribution is a two-stage optimization framework that first estimates the expert state distribution using a newly proposed Denoising Continuous Normalizing Flow (DCNF) algorithm, and then trains the learner policy to match both the expert's policy and state distribution. The authors provide theoretical justification for their approach through Theorem 4.1, which establishes a tight lower bound on the likelihood-based imitation learning objective. The experimental evaluation on ten physics-based control tasks demonstrates that SLIL achieves superior mode coverage preservation, more stable training, and comparable or better policy performance compared to several baselines including BC, GAIL, DRIL, and GPRIL.

## Strengths

The contribution of this work over the given baselines is clear and well-motivated. The paper provides detailed explanations and comprehensive experiments that substantiate the main claims. Specifically, I appreciate the following aspects:

**Theoretical Foundation and Problem Formulation**: The paper clearly articulates the limitations of existing approaches and motivates the proposed solution through a well-structured problem formulation. The introduction of the Likelihood-based Imitation Learning (LIL) framework in Section 3 provides a principled foundation for the subsequent methodology. Theorem 4.1 offers theoretical justification for relaxing the bilevel optimization problem into a two-stage framework, which is a meaningful theoretical contribution that helps explain why the proposed approach is more stable than joint training schemes.

**Technical Innovation with DCNF**: The justification for using DCNF rather than standard Continuous Normalizing Flow (CNF) is particularly well-described and thoroughly supported by experiments. The paper clearly identifies the manifold hypothesis challenge—that real-world data often concentrates on low-dimensional manifolds in high-dimensional spaces—and provides both intuitive visual explanations (Figure 5) and quantitative validation of the proposed solution. The algorithmic description of DCNF in Algorithm 1 is clear and implementable, with the denoising mechanism elegantly addressing a fundamental limitation of normalizing flows. The comparison in Figure 9 showing superior test state log-likelihood for DCNF compared to both CNF and SoftFlow provides compelling evidence for the necessity of the denoising mechanism.

**Comprehensive Experimental Evaluation**: The experimental section is thorough and addresses multiple aspects of the proposed method. The mode coverage analysis in Section 5.1, particularly the visualization using kernel density estimation and quantitative EMD measurements, effectively demonstrates that SLIL preserves expert mode distributions while baselines suffer from mode collapse. The latent space recovery experiments in Table 1 provide additional validation that DCNF successfully addresses the manifold hypothesis challenge. The training stability analysis in Section 5.4 is particularly valuable, examining multiple dimensions of stability including sensitivity to the gating parameter τ, robustness to learning rate changes, and convergence behavior with respect to environment interactions.

**Clear Presentation**: The writing is generally clear and well-organized. The use of figures, particularly Figure 1's visual comparison of mode coverage and Figure 4's illustration of the SLIL framework, effectively communicates the key ideas. The paper appropriately uses appendices to provide implementation details without cluttering the main narrative.

## Major Weaknesses

Despite the strengths outlined above, there are significant concerns that prevent this work from meeting the acceptance threshold.

**Incomplete and Potentially Misleading Related Work**: The paper claims to address problems that are not unique to GAIL, yet several highly relevant recent works are either missed or inadequately discussed. Most critically, the paper states that "from the best of our knowledge, none of existing works solve the unstable training and mode collapse problem of GAIL" (Abstract), which is a strong claim that is not sufficiently supported given the existing literature.

The work of Dadashi et al. (2020) on Primal Wasserstein Imitation Learning (PWIL) is mentioned only briefly in the related work section and appears in Figure 1 as a baseline, yet the paper does not adequately discuss how PWIL addresses similar concerns or how SLIL differs fundamentally in its approach. PWIL demonstrates sample efficiency with as few as 1 or 11 expert trajectories, which is substantially more data-efficient than the experimental setup in this paper (which uses 4-25 trajectories for most tasks, and over 80 for Humanoid). The fact that PWIL avoids adversarial training and has been shown to be more sample-efficient than GAIL suggests that the claim about being the "first" to tackle these issues is overstated.

Similarly, Kim et al. (2020b) on Imitation with Neural Density Models (NDI) uses energy-based models for expert support estimation based on maximum likelihood principles, which is conceptually related to the likelihood maximization objective in this work (Eq. 2). The paper cites NDI but does not provide a detailed comparison of how the approaches differ or how SLIL's performance compares to NDI in the experimental evaluation. This omission is particularly problematic because NDI is also motivated by addressing limitations of GAIL and uses density estimation, making it directly relevant to understanding the novelty of this work.

Furthermore, the paper mentions GPRIL (Schroecker et al., 2019) as a baseline but provides limited analysis of its poor performance. GPRIL also attempts to avoid adversarial training by learning predecessor state-action distributions using normalizing flows. The paper states that "GPRIL have the lowest performances in all tasks" and attributes this to difficulty in joint training (page 8), but does not provide sufficient analysis of whether this is a fundamental limitation of the approach or a result of implementation/hyperparameter choices. The dismissal of GPRIL results as "no meaningful results are obtained" (Figure 8 caption) without further explanation is unsatisfying.

**Experimental Limitations and Incomplete Comparisons**: While the experimental evaluation is comprehensive in some respects, there are notable gaps that limit the strength of the conclusions.

First, the mode collapse experiments are limited to relatively simple multi-modal tasks (Reacher with 2-4 targets, HalfCheetah with 2 directions). These tasks, while useful for visualization, represent a narrow slice of the multi-modal imitation learning problem. The paper does not evaluate whether mode collapse occurs in more complex, high-dimensional multi-modal settings or in tasks where modes are not geometrically separated. Additionally, the claim that SLIL "solves" the mode collapse problem is overstated—Figure 1(h) shows that SLIL still has non-zero EMD compared to the expert, indicating imperfect mode preservation.

Second, the comparison with PWIL in Figure 1 shows that PWIL exhibits mode collapse in the Reacher4 task, but the paper provides no analysis of why PWIL fails in this setting or whether PWIL was properly tuned. Given that PWIL is a strong baseline that avoids adversarial training, a more thorough comparison would strengthen the paper's claims. The paper also does not evaluate PWIL's performance in the single-mode tasks (Figure 10), which would provide a more complete picture of how SLIL compares to this relevant baseline.

Third, the experimental setup relies on expert policies obtained by running TRPO with ground-truth reward functions. This is a somewhat artificial setting that may not reflect real-world imitation learning scenarios where expert demonstrations come from human behavior or other sources. The paper does not discuss how results might differ with suboptimal or stochastic expert policies, which would be more realistic.

Fourth, the number of expert trajectories used in experiments is relatively high compared to some recent works. For instance, the paper uses 11-25 trajectories for most tasks and over 80 for Humanoid, whereas PWIL demonstrates effectiveness with 1-11 trajectories. While the paper does show results with varying numbers of trajectories, it does not adequately address the sample efficiency comparison with PWIL and other recent methods.

**Insufficient Analysis of the Gating Function**: The introduction of the gating function τ in Equation 4 is presented as necessary for training stability, and Figure 11 shows that τ=2 works well for the Walker task. However, the paper provides limited guidance on how to select τ for new tasks. Is τ=2 optimal across all tasks, or does it need to be tuned per task? The paper states that "a lower τ leads to more iterations to match the expert distribution" but does not provide principled guidance for setting this hyperparameter. This is particularly concerning because the gating function is presented as a solution to training instability, yet it introduces another hyperparameter that appears to require tuning. The paper should provide either a principled method for selecting τ or demonstrate that a fixed value works robustly across diverse tasks.

**Limited Theoretical Analysis**: While Theorem 4.1 provides a useful theoretical
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This work proposed Stabilized Likelihood-based Imitation Learning (SLIL) which iteratively estimates the expert state distribution by using Denoising Continuous Normalizing Flow (DCNF) and maximizes the policy learning objective in Eq. (3) that matches expert policy and state distribution. Compared to other baselines such as BC, DRIL, GAIL, SLIL was shown to find out better expert state distribution (in terms of seeking multiple modes, Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) while achieving the empirical return close to that of the expert (which is widely used in the imitation learning literature). The strength of using DCNF instead of using Continuous Normalizing Flow (CNF) is well desribed and supported by the experiments evaluating the test log likelihood in Figure 9. Authors also analyze SLIL's hyperparameter sensitivity that supports the claim on the SLIL's stability.

Main Review:
Regarding the strength of this work, the contribution of this work over given baselines is clear, and I'm satisfied with the detailed explanations and experiments, e.g., comparison with LIL, the justification of using DCNF rather than CNF, how DCNF is trained algorithmically, quantitative tests using EMD and test log likelihoods, etc. 

Regarding the weakness of this work, I think some recent relevant works are missed, e.g., Dadashi et al., "Primal Wasserstein Imitation Learning", Kim et al., "Imitation with Neural Density Models", and some references therein. Although these papers didn't aim to solve the mode collapsing directly, their algorithms are much more sample-efficient than GAIL in terms of the number of expert trajectories and the number of environment interactions (i.e., GAIL is no more a SOTA algorithm as stated in this work), and both are relevant to this work in the sense that expert support estimation is used. Primal Wasserstein Imitation Learning (PWIL) shows their algorithm works efficiently even when we have a small number of expert trajectories (1 or 11 trajectories), whereas this work presented empirical results assuming more than 4 expert trajectories (more than 80 expert trajectories for Humanoid). In "Imitation with Neural Density Models", energy-based model is used to estimate expert support estimation, which are based on maximmum likelihood estimation similar to this work.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, writing is clear, and the empirical results are shown to outperform GAIL and some other baselines. However, some SOTA algorithms (which I believe is relevant to this work) are missed, and it is unclear that the mode collapsing issue also happens for those relevant works or not. Although I agree that there is an algorithmic novelty for this work, I believe more elaborated results should be given with the aforementioned references.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary Of The Paper

This paper proposes PhaseFool, a novel adversarial attack method targeting automatic speech recognition (ASR) networks through phase-oriented perturbations. The core insight is that phase information perturbations are likely to be imperceptible to human listeners because phase information is known to produce relatively small perceptible differences compared to magnitude information in the frequency domain. A key theoretical contribution is that perturbations on phase information influence the magnitude information through the spectrogram consistency property of the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT), a phenomenon the authors term "energy dissipation." The experimental results demonstrate that PhaseFool achieves imperceptibility comparable to state-of-the-art methods while requiring significantly fewer optimization steps—achieving a 6.64x speedup over the previous best imperceptible attack (Qin's attack). The authors also provide qualitative analysis showing that energy dissipation occurs primarily in harmonic regions of speech spectrograms, offering interpretability into how the attack affects the spectrogram structure.

## Main Review

### Strengths

1. **Well-motivated theoretical foundation**: The idea of phase-based adversarial attacks is well-grounded in established signal processing principles. The authors correctly leverage the known psychoacoustic property that phase information contributes less to human perception than magnitude information in single-channel audio. This motivation is clearly articulated in the introduction and builds naturally on prior work in phase-aware audio processing (Section 2). The connection between phase manipulation and imperceptibility is intuitive and aligns with decades of research in audio signal processing.

2. **Rigorous theoretical analysis of energy dissipation**: The authors provide a mathematically sound derivation showing that phase-based perturbations necessarily lead to energy dissipation in the magnitude spectrogram. The analysis in Section 3.2 is particularly valuable, where they derive the relationship between phase perturbations in adjacent STFT frames and the resulting magnitude spectrogram changes. Specifically, Equations 3-9 establish that the energy ratio γ_m[k] is bounded within [0,1], mathematically proving that phase perturbations always reduce magnitude energy. This theoretical contribution goes beyond intuition and provides a principled foundation for the attack method. The use of spectrogram consistency (Equation 7) to connect phase and magnitude domains is elegant and well-explained through Figure 2.

3. **Comprehensive and convincing experimental results**: The experimental evaluation is thorough and demonstrates clear advantages over existing methods. The human study results in Table 1 are particularly compelling—PhaseFool achieves 43-46% "classified as noisy" rates compared to 45-49% for Qin's attack, demonstrating comparable imperceptibility while requiring only 700 steps versus Qin's 5000 steps. The 6.64x speedup (Table 2) is substantial and practically significant for generating adversarial examples. The robustness evaluations against both environmental distortions (Table 4) and state-of-the-art defenses (Table 3) are thorough, showing that the adversarial examples remain effective even after applying realistic transformations. The authors test against multiple defense mechanisms (TD, Dropout, Downsample, Noise) and environmental conditions (reverberation, white Gaussian noise), providing confidence in the robustness of their approach.

4. **Effective weighted loss function design**: The proposed weighted loss function (Equation 11) is thoughtfully designed based on the principle of energy dissipation rather than relying on generic lp-norm constraints. By differentiating between spectrogram regions based on their importance for human perception (local maxima vs. other signals vs. inaudible regions), the method achieves better imperceptibility than simple l2-based constraints. The ablation study in Table 5 and Table 6 clearly demonstrates this—PhaseFool with the weighted loss achieves 43-44% noise classification compared to 75-72% with l2 loss and 64-67% without constraints. This shows that the weighted loss function is essential for balancing imperceptibility and attack effectiveness.

5. **Clear presentation and reproducibility**: The paper is well-written with clear explanations of complex concepts. The visualizations, particularly Figure 1 and Figure 3, effectively illustrate the differences between attacks and provide insight into how PhaseFool generates perturbations. The authors provide implementation details, experimental setup descriptions, and promise to release code and adversarial examples for reproducibility. The supplementary material includes extensive ablation studies, convergence analysis (Figure 6), and detailed experimental setup information.

### Weaknesses

1. **Limited evaluation on raw-waveform-based ASR architectures**: The experimental evaluation focuses exclusively on spectrogram-based ASR systems—DeepSpeech 2 (which uses log spectrograms) and a low-rank Transformer (which uses log-mel spectrograms). While these are standard benchmarks, the paper would be strengthened by evaluation on raw-waveform-based ASR networks that have become increasingly popular in recent years. Systems like wav2vec, HuBERT, or other end-to-end models that directly process audio waveforms might respond differently to phase perturbations since they don't explicitly use STFT features. This limitation raises questions about the generalizability of the approach to modern ASR architectures that bypass traditional spectrogram representations. The authors should discuss whether the energy dissipation principle would still apply when the ASR model doesn't explicitly operate in the frequency domain.

2. **Limited diversity in tested ASR architectures**: Beyond the waveform-based limitation, the paper tests only two ASR models—DeepSpeech 2 (LSTM-based) and a low-rank Transformer. While these represent different architectural paradigms, the evaluation would be more convincing with a broader range of models. Testing on other established architectures such as conformer-based models, other transformer variants, or hybrid approaches would strengthen claims about the generality of the method. Additionally, the paper does not evaluate on commercially deployed ASR systems or other popular open-source models like Whisper, Kaldi, or other modern systems. The limited architectural diversity makes it unclear whether the 6.64x speedup and imperceptibility advantages hold across the full spectrum of modern ASR systems.

3. **Incomplete analysis of phase reconstruction assumptions**: The theoretical analysis assumes perfect reconstruction from STFT (Section 3.1.2), where the window function w, window length N, and window shift R are fixed and allow perfect reconstruction. However, real-world implementations may have variations in these parameters, and the analysis doesn't thoroughly discuss how violations of these assumptions affect the energy dissipation principle. Additionally, the paper doesn't explore how the method performs with different window functions (Hann, Hamming, etc.) or different window shift ratios beyond the assumed 50% overlap. This could be important since different ASR systems may use different STFT configurations.

4. **Limited discussion of the threat model and practical applicability**: While the authors acknowledge the white-box threat model assumption, the practical applicability of the method remains somewhat limited. The paper briefly mentions that white-box attacks have been extended to black-box settings in prior work but doesn't explore whether PhaseFool can be effectively adapted to black-box scenarios. Given that real-world attacks would likely need to work against unknown ASR systems, evaluation or discussion of black-box transferability would significantly strengthen the practical relevance of the work. The authors mention this as future work, but it represents a notable gap in the current evaluation.

5. **Insufficient exploration of phase perturbation bounds and constraints**: The formulation in Equation 2 constrains phase perturbations to [-π, π], which is mathematically necessary for phase values. However, the paper doesn't thoroughly investigate whether tighter constraints on phase perturbations could further improve imperceptibility or whether the full [-π, π] range is necessary for achieving high attack success rates. Additionally, the interaction between the phase perturbation bounds and the weighted loss function parameters (β₁ = 0.98, β₂ = 0.90) could be explored more thoroughly. Sensitivity analysis on these hyperparameters would provide insight into the robustness of the method's design choices.

### Minor Issues and Suggestions

1. The paper could benefit from more detailed discussion of why phase perturbations are particularly effective for ASR systems compared to other audio processing tasks. While the psychoacoustic motivation is sound for human perception, it would be valuable to analyze why ASR models are vulnerable to energy dissipation in the magnitude spectrogram.

2. Figure 3 provides useful visualization of perturbation patterns, but the paper could expand on the interpretation of why perturbations concentrate around harmonics. A more detailed analysis of the relationship between harmonic structure and ASR model vulnerabilities would strengthen the interpretability contribution.

3. The convergence analysis in Figure 6 shows that Levenshtein distance reaches zero before 700 steps for all tested samples, but the paper doesn't discuss variance in convergence behavior across different audio samples or transcription pairs. Understanding which types of audio or target transcriptions are easier or harder to attack would provide additional insights.

## Summary Of The Review

The paper presents a solid and well-

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: PhaseFool - Phase-Oriented Audio Adversarial Examples via Energy Dissipation

## Summary of the Paper

This paper proposes PhaseFool, a phase-oriented algorithm designed to efficiently construct imperceptible audio adversarial examples against automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems through energy dissipation mechanisms. Rather than following the conventional approach of adding perturbations directly to the audio waveform or constraining magnitude spectrograms, the authors leverage the spectrogram consistency property of the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) to adversarially manipulate phase spectrograms. The key insight is that phase perturbations in one frame of the phase spectrogram transfer to adjacent frames of the magnitude spectrogram, causing energy dissipation that is less perceptible to human listeners. The authors propose a weighted loss function based on this energy dissipation principle to improve imperceptibility. Empirical evaluations on the LibriSpeech dataset against DeepSpeech 2 and a Transformer-based ASR system demonstrate that PhaseFool achieves comparable imperceptibility to state-of-the-art methods while providing a 6.64x speedup in generation time.

## Main Review

### Strengths

This paper presents an interesting and relatively novel perspective on generating stealthy adversarial audio examples by exploiting phase information, which has been largely neglected in prior adversarial audio attack research. The motivation is well-grounded in signal processing principles, particularly the observation from Vary & Eurasip (1985) that phase manipulation in single-channel audio has minimal perceptual effects on human listeners. The paper is generally well-organized and easy to follow, with clear mathematical exposition of the core concepts.

The theoretical contribution regarding energy dissipation is noteworthy. The authors provide a rigorous mathematical analysis (Equations 3-9) demonstrating how phase perturbations in frame m inevitably reduce the magnitude of the reconstructed spectrogram in the corresponding areas through the overlap-add process inherent in STFT reconstruction. This analysis elegantly shows that γm[k] ∈ [0, 1], where γm[k] represents the ratio of reconstructed signal energy to clean audio energy, providing a principled foundation for their approach. The derivation leveraging spectrogram consistency (Equation 7) is particularly insightful and represents a genuine contribution to understanding the relationship between phase and magnitude spectrograms in the context of adversarial examples.

The proposed weighted loss function (Equations 10-11) demonstrates thoughtful engineering by differentiating between spectrogram regions based on their perceptual importance. By constraining energy dissipation differently for locally prominent spectral peaks (β1 = 0.98) versus less important regions (β2 = 0.90), and leaving inaudible regions unconstrained, the method shows practical awareness of psychoacoustic principles. The ablation studies in Tables 5 and 6 effectively demonstrate the importance of this weighted constraint compared to unconstrained optimization or simple l2-norm constraints.

The computational efficiency gains are substantial and practically significant. The reported 6.64x speedup over Qin et al. (2019) (approximately 3 minutes vs. 20+ minutes for DeepSpeech 2, and 33 seconds vs. 305 seconds for the Transformer system) is a meaningful contribution that could facilitate more practical deployment of this attack methodology. The single-stage optimization approach, as opposed to the two-stage optimization required by Qin's method, contributes to this efficiency advantage.

### Major Weaknesses

The evaluation of the proposed attack's robustness against adversarial defenses is incomplete and raises significant concerns about the practical effectiveness of the method. While the paper does present robustness evaluations in Section 5.3 and Table 3, the scope is limited to only four defenses (Temporal Dependency, Dropout, Downsample, and Noise defenses), and the results are mixed at best. The AUC scores reported in Table 3 range from 57% to 68%, which, while not catastrophic, do not demonstrate clear superiority over baseline methods. More critically, the paper lacks evaluation against several important defense mechanisms that have been proposed in recent years, such as certified defenses, detection-based approaches, or defenses specifically tailored to phase-based attacks. Given that the paper explicitly claims the adversarial examples "remain effective even after applying realistic simulated environmental distortions and several state-of-the-art defenses," the limited scope of defense evaluation undermines this claim.

Furthermore, Table 4 reveals concerning vulnerabilities in specific scenarios. Against reverberant environments, PhaseFool achieves only 38% success rate compared to 47% for C&W's attack, and the imperceptibility degrades significantly with 61% of users perceiving noise (compared to 43% in clean conditions). While the authors argue that phase-based perturbations sound like "echoes or blurred pronunciation" and thus remain relatively imperceptible in reverberant conditions, this explanation feels post-hoc and is not rigorously validated. The noise defense scenario shows PhaseFool achieving 44% success rate with 48% of users perceiving noise, which is not substantially better than some baseline methods and raises questions about the robustness claims.

The evaluation across different ASR systems is insufficient. While the paper does test against two systems—DeepSpeech 2 (a CTC-based system) and a Transformer-based ASR model—this represents a limited diversity of architectures. Modern ASR systems employ various approaches including attention mechanisms, conformer architectures, and end-to-end models with different loss functions and feature representations. The paper would benefit from evaluation against additional diverse ASR architectures to establish the generalizability of the attack. The choice of only two systems makes it difficult to assess whether the attack's effectiveness is dependent on specific architectural properties or represents a more fundamental vulnerability.

### Moderate Weaknesses

The lack of adversarial transferability analysis is a significant gap that limits the practical applicability of the work. The paper focuses exclusively on white-box attacks, as acknowledged in Section 4 (Threat Model). While the authors argue that white-box attacks serve as base methods for black-box attacks and reference prior work showing such transferability, they provide no empirical evidence that PhaseFool-generated adversarial examples transfer to different ASR systems or models. This is particularly important because: (1) the attack is phase-based and may have different transferability properties compared to waveform or magnitude-based attacks; (2) adversarial transferability is often model-dependent and cannot be assumed without empirical validation; (3) understanding transferability properties would significantly strengthen the practical threat assessment.

The human study for imperceptibility evaluation, while valuable, has some limitations that should be acknowledged more explicitly. The study involved 24 online participants and asked them to identify background noise, which is a reasonable proxy for imperceptibility but may not capture all perceptual dimensions. For instance, the study does not evaluate whether listeners can detect subtle artifacts, phase distortions, or other auditory phenomena that might not be characterized as "noise" but could still alert an attacker. Additionally, the study shows that 43-46% of PhaseFool's examples are classified as noisy, which is only marginally better than the 45-49% for Qin's attack—a difference that may not be statistically significant given the sample size. The paper would benefit from reporting confidence intervals or statistical significance tests for these human study results.

The mathematical analysis, while generally sound, makes some simplifying assumptions that deserve more discussion. The assumption that window shift R equals half the window length N (50% overlap) is standard but is not universally applied in all ASR systems. The paper should discuss how the analysis extends to different overlap percentages or different STFT configurations. Additionally, the analysis assumes perfect reconstruction from STFT, which may not hold exactly in practice when using different window functions or when numerical precision issues arise. The impact of these practical deviations on the energy dissipation property should be addressed.

### Minor Weaknesses

The paper's treatment of the phase perturbation constraint (−π ≤ δ ≤ π) is somewhat underexplored. While phase wrapping is mathematically natural, the paper does not thoroughly discuss how this constraint affects the optimization landscape or whether it introduces local minima that might affect convergence. Some discussion of how the optimization navigates this circular constraint space would strengthen the technical contribution.

The weighted loss function design, while motivated by psychoacoustic principles, appears somewhat ad-hoc. The thresholds β1 = 0.98 and β2 = 0.90 are not thoroughly justified beyond intuition. A sensitivity analysis exploring how performance varies with different threshold values would provide useful insights into the robustness of these design choices. Similarly, the weighting parameters α1 = 1 and α2 = 0.05 are set without systematic justification, and ablation studies on these values would strengthen the contribution.

The paper's discussion of related work in phase-aware audio processing (Section 2) is somewhat disconnected from the main contribution. While the authors cite several works using phase information for signal enhancement and speech synthesis,
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a phase-oriented algorithm PhaseFool to efficiently construct imperceptible audio adversarial attacks with energy dissipation.  The authors leverage the spectrogram consistency of STFT to adversarially transfer phase perturbations to the adjacent frames and dissipate the energy that is crucial for ASR systems. Empirical evaluations show that the attack effectiveness of the proposed attack is high.

Main Review:
This paper provides an interesting perspective to generate stealthy adversarial audios. The paper is easy to follow and the proposed method is well-motivated.
However, the evaluation of the proposed attack in the paper is a bit weak. For instance, the paper didn't evaluate the proposed attack against different adversarial audio defenses to evaluate the resiliency of the attack. 
It would be helpful if the authors can test the generated adversarial audios against different ASR systems.
Further, it would be important and interesting to test the adversarial transferability and other properties of the generated adversarial audios.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall the paper is well-organized and the proposed method is interesting.
If the authors can add necessary evaluation, such as the attack effectiveness comparison with other attacks against the state of the art defenses; the adversarial transferability analysis, and the adversarial evaluation against different ASR systems, I would like the increase my score.

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces a new way of constructing adversarial examples for automatic speech recognition using the phase of the short-time Fourier transformation. The authors conduct experiments and subjective evaluations to show how their adversarial examples compare to previous state-of-the-art algorithms. The experimental results show that the proposed phase-oriented audio adversarial samples can be produced in reduced time as well as they are less perceivable by humans.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, I believe that the paper has great potential since the idea of energy dissipation is quite interesting but there are several concerns that need to be addressed. If the following concerns are addressed, I will consider to increase my score. Please see below my concerns and questions to the authors in descending order of importance (0 is the most important):

0. The main problem of this paper stems from the limited experimental setup that the authors have chosen to show the significance of their contributions. Specifically, the authors have only tested their algorithm for generating sequences with 100% WER and with a white-box ASR which was trained by the authors. It would be interesting to see if those adversarial examples are valid for pre-trained black box models even with not 100% WER. Moreoever, the 100% WER might be misleading since it would make much more sense to have a small change in a few words that would change the meaning than completely altering the whole target sentence. Furthermore, it might be the case that the other algorithms could alter a couple of words fairly easily but not all of them.

1. The authors provide only a very limited amount of adversarial examples in their supplementary material (which the authors also do not provide details if they are random or cherry-picked). Even from those limited examples I would admit that there are evident artifacts in PhaseFool’s adversarial examples. To the best of my hearing ability I would also say that the adversarial examples coming from the “Imperceptable” sound less artifact-y. I do not consider that the difference is so significant but I would like to hear more random examples to conclude.

2. There are several premises and claims in this paper which are at least ill-posed and sometimes wrong. For example, “Since phase is not relevant for speech recognition, the existing audio adversarial attacks neglect the influence of phase spectrogram.” is not true in cases where there are several speakers and a model (or even a human) uses the phase (aka inter-aural time-difference) between the two sources to better isolate and understand the words spoken by a speaker from a specific location. Another example, “Till now, we reveal the bi-directional essence of phase-oriented audio adversarial attack: it dissipates the critical energy for the classification model and constructs the adversarial perturbations in the meantime.” How is the latter statement shown? To the best of my knowledge, there is no “known” frequency region critical energy for classification for all ASR models. 

3. I think that calling the inference/convergence times “efficiency (s)” is rather misleading. Moreover, the authors should report the number of trainable parameters, as well as the actual memory requirements in order to conclude that their algorithm is indeed more efficient and also discuss ways if their algorithm could be somehow more efficiently parallelized.

4. I am not fully convinced that an ASR model which does not use an STFT or a log-mel representation would also be vulnerable to an attack such as the one proposed in this paper. Specifically, with a small and simple adaptive front-end, one could possibly learn some real over-complete basis to be more robust against attacks like  PhaseFool. Real-basis have been successfully applied towards separation and enhancement problems but also show that can effectively represent speech with even a single layer [1, 2, 3, 4]. Could the authors provide ablation studies by replacing the fixed STFT layers with adaptive convolutions and still show that their findings still hold?

5. Based on the aforementioned point, the vulnerability of ASR models to phase perturbations or echoes is known for many years now [5] which partly explains the efficiency of PhaseFool that can easily drive the ASR model towards a mis-prediction while also not altering the structure of the sound significantly. Can PhaseFool be applied in reverberant scenarios where there are also other phase artifacts and still cannot be perceived by the human ear? Optionally, it would be wise to assume that there is at least some naive Wiener or minimum variance distortionless response (MVDR) filter and see how and if PhaseFool can trick those front-ends as well. There are other more sophisticated defences that the authors could also try, such as [6], to show that the proposed adversarial examples are not easily detectable by automated systems.

6. Table 1. should be WER 100% and the arrow next to WER should be facing up since  all of them are adversarial algorithms.

7. Could the authors specify what are the exact time-lengths of the input audio that the ~30seconds inference time corresponds to?

[1] Venkataramani S, Casebeer J, Smaragdis P. End-to-end source separation with adaptive front-ends. In2018 52nd Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers 2018 Oct 28 (pp. 684-688). IEEE.

[2] Luo, Y. and Mesgarani, N., 2019. Conv-tasnet: Surpassing ideal time–frequency magnitude masking for speech separation. IEEE/ACM transactions on audio, speech, and language processing, 27(8), pp.1256-1266.

[3] Tzinis, E., Venkataramani, S., Wang, Z., Subakan, C. and Smaragdis, P., 2020, May. Two-step sound source separation: Training on learned latent targets. In ICASSP 2020-2020 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP) (pp. 31-35). IEEE.

[4] Mimilakis, S.I., Drossos, K. and Schuller, G., 2021, January. Unsupervised interpretable representation learning for singing voice separation. In 2020 28th European Signal Processing Conference (EUSIPCO) (pp. 1412-1416). IEEE.

[5] Xiong, F., Meyer, B.T., Moritz, N., Rehr, R., Anemüller, J., Gerkmann, T., Doclo, S. and Goetze, S., 2015. Front-end technologies for robust ASR in reverberant environments—spectral enhancement-based dereverberation and auditory modulation filterbank features. EURASIP Journal on Advances in Signal Processing, 2015(1), pp.1-18.

[6] Jayashankar, T., Roux, J.L., Moulin, P. (2020) Detecting Audio Attacks on ASR Systems with Dropout Uncertainty. Proc. Interspeech 2020, 4671-4675, doi: 10.21437/Interspeech.2020-1846.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper introduces a new way of constructing adversarial examples for automatic speech recognition using the phase of the short-time Fourier transformation. The authors conduct experiments and subjective evaluations to show how their adversarial examples compare to previous state-of-the-art algorithms. The experimental results show that the proposed phase-oriented audio adversarial samples can be produced in reduced time as well as they are less perceivable by humans.

Main Review:
Overall, I believe that the paper has great potential since the idea of energy dissipation is quite interesting but there are several concerns that need to be addressed. If the following concerns are addressed, I will consider to increase my score. Please see below my concerns and questions to the authors in descending order of importance (0 is the most important):

0. The main problem of this paper stems from the limited experimental setup that the authors have chosen to show the significance of their contributions. Specifically, the authors have only tested their algorithm for generating sequences with 100% WER and with a white-box ASR which was trained by the authors. It would be interesting to see if those adversarial examples are valid for pre-trained black box models even with not 100% WER. Moreoever, the 100% WER might be misleading since it would make much more sense to have a small change in a few words that would change the meaning than completely altering the whole target sentence. Furthermore, it might be the case that the other algorithms could alter a couple of words fairly easily but not all of them.

Real-basis have been successfully applied towards separation and enhancement problems but also show that can effectively represent speech with even a single layer [1, 2, 3, 4]. Could the authors provide ablation studies by replacing the fixed STFT layers with adaptive convolutions and still show that their findings still hold?

5.
